fspl.name: Free Space Path Loss
fspl.purpose: Tells you how much signal, in dB, is lost as it travels through open space between a transmitter and a receiver, based on the frequency and the distance.
fspl.whyHere: It's the first step in any link budget. Use it to estimate how much signal a point-to-point or outdoor link loses before you add antennas and cables into the picture.
howToUse: Enter the frequency and pick its unit (GHz or MHz).
Enter the distance and pick its unit (km, mi, or m).
Read the path loss in dB. The result stays blank ("—") until both fields hold valid positive numbers.
fspl.algorithm: Path loss in dB is 20 × log10(frequency in GHz) + 20 × log10(distance in km) + 92.45. That 92.45 is the fixed constant for the kilometer-and-GHz version of the formula. Frequency in MHz is divided by 1000 to get GHz; miles are multiplied by 1.60934 and meters divided by 1000 to get kilometers. The answer is rounded to one decimal place, and if either value is zero or less the result blanks out.
fieldNotes: This is free space only. No obstructions, no ground reflection, no atmospheric effects. Real links always lose more, so treat this as a best-case floor, not a prediction.
The reference card lists anchor values (2.4 GHz at 1 km = 100.1 dB, 6 GHz at 1 km = 108.0 dB).
fspl.source: Calculated live from the frequency and distance you enter using the standard free space path loss formula.

wavelength.name: Wavelength
wavelength.purpose: Converts a frequency into its wavelength, shown in meters, centimeters, millimeters, feet, and inches all at once.
wavelength.whyHere: Sizing antenna elements, setting ground-plane and spacing rules, and the quarter-wave and half-wave rules of thumb all depend on wavelength. Handy quick reference when you're laying out antennas.
howToUse: Enter the frequency and pick its unit (MHz or GHz).
Read the wavelength in all five units at once.
wavelength.algorithm: Wavelength in meters is 300 divided by the frequency in MHz. The 300 comes from the speed of light (about 3 × 10⁸ meters per second) scaled for the MHz-and-meter form. From there: centimeters is meters × 100, millimeters is meters × 1000, feet is meters × 3.28084, and inches is feet × 12. Frequency in GHz is multiplied by 1000 to get MHz. Results show as meters to 4 decimals, centimeters to 2, millimeters to 1, feet to 4, and inches to 3.
fieldNotes: This uses the speed of light rounded to 3 × 10⁸ m/s (the 300 constant) rather than the exact 299792458. The tiny difference doesn't matter for antenna work.
This is free-space wavelength; it does not apply the velocity factor of cable or dielectric material.
wavelength.source: Calculated live from the frequency you enter using the speed of light.

antenna-length.name: Antenna Length
antenna-length.purpose: A frequency, wavelength, and antenna-element calculator: enter a frequency (or a wavelength) and get the full wavelength plus the half-wave dipole and quarter-wave vertical physical lengths (adjusted for velocity factor) in meters, feet, and inches, right alongside the classic 468/f and 234/f rules of thumb.
antenna-length.whyHere: Cutting a dipole or a ground-plane vertical means turning a frequency into a length. This shows both the physics length (wavelength × velocity factor) and the field rules of thumb side by side, so you can see how velocity factor and end-effect change the cut.
howToUse: Use the toggle to choose whether you're entering a Frequency (MHz/GHz) or a Wavelength (m).
Type the value, then set the velocity factor (default 0.95 for thin bare wire; lower for insulated wire or tubing).
Read the full wavelength, then the half-wave dipole and quarter-wave vertical lengths in m / ft / in, each beside its rule-of-thumb figure.
antenna-length.algorithm: Wavelength in meters is 299.792458 divided by the frequency in MHz (and the reverse gives frequency from wavelength). A half-wave dipole's physical length is half the wavelength times the velocity factor; a quarter-wave vertical is a quarter of the wavelength times the velocity factor. The field rules of thumb, in feet, are 468 divided by the frequency in MHz for a dipole and 234 divided by the frequency in MHz for a quarter-wave. Physical length is always the electrical length times the velocity factor. This uses the exact speed of light (299.792458), not the rounded 300.
antenna-length.example: 14.2 MHz gives a wavelength of 21.112 m; a half-wave dipole at velocity factor 0.95 is 10.03 m / 32.9 ft, and the 468 rule gives 32.96 ft. At 146 MHz a quarter-wave vertical (velocity factor 0.95) is about 19.2 in. At 2400 MHz a quarter-wave is about 2.97 cm.
fieldNotes: The 468 and 234 rules of thumb already build in a typical end-effect shortening, so they read a little shorter than the pure half- or quarter-wavelength.
Velocity factor is the ratio of physical to electrical length; 0.95 suits thin bare wire, while insulated wire and tubing run lower.
This is element-length math only; it does not model feedpoint impedance, height above ground, or matching.
antenna-length.source: Calculated live from the frequency or wavelength and velocity factor you enter, using the speed of light and the standard antenna-length formulas.

maidenhead-grid.name: Maidenhead Grid Square
maidenhead-grid.purpose: Turn a latitude/longitude into a Maidenhead (QTH) locator at 4, 6, or 8 characters, decode a locator back into its square center and corner bounds, and measure the great-circle distance and bearing between two grid squares.
maidenhead-grid.whyHere: Grid squares are the amateur-radio shorthand for position, and the distance and bearing between two squares is exactly the aiming math for a point-to-point Wi-Fi or microwave link. One tool covers both.
howToUse: To Grid: enter latitude and longitude in decimal degrees and pick a precision (4 / 6 / 8 characters). The result shows the locator plus the square center and corners.
To Lat/Lon: enter a 4/6/8-character locator (for example JO62qm) to get its center latitude/longitude and bounds.
Distance: enter two locators to get the great-circle distance (km/mi) and initial bearing between their centers.

inputs.name: Locator
inputs.range: field A-R, subsquare A-X, the rest 0-9

maidenhead-grid.algorithm: The locator system divides the globe into ever-smaller cells, longitude first, then latitude, one level at a time. The first pair (the Field, letters A-R) covers 20° of longitude and 10° of latitude; the second pair (the Square, digits 0-9) covers 2° and 1°; the third pair (the Subsquare, letters a-x) covers 5 and 2.5 arc-minutes; the fourth pair (Extended, digits 0-9) covers 0.5 and 0.25 arc-minutes. Decoding runs this in reverse to the southwest corner, then adds half a cell to land on the center. Distance and bearing use the standard spherical (haversine and forward-bearing) formulas with an Earth radius of 6371 km.
maidenhead-grid.example: 37.75, -122.45 gives CM87 (San Francisco Bay). 52.5 N, 13.4 E gives JO62 (Berlin). 48.14666, 11.60833 gives JN58td (Munich). CM87 to JO62 is about 9100 km.
fieldNotes: A locator names a rectangular square, not a single point; a longer locator means a smaller square. Decoding returns the square's center.
Exactly +180 longitude or +90 latitude is nudged one step inside so it stays in the last cell. A coordinate sitting on a square boundary (for example longitude -122.0) falls into the higher square.
Distance and bearing are measured from the square centers, so they are only as precise as the locators you give.
maidenhead-grid.source: Calculated on your device from the coordinates or locators you enter, using standard Maidenhead and great-circle math.

hear-frequency.name: Hear the Frequency
hear-frequency.purpose: Turn a frequency into a real-time tone so you can build RF intuition by ear: pitch is frequency, an octave is a doubling, twelve equal-tempered semitones span an octave, harmonics are whole-number multiples, and the same logarithmic instinct underlies both octaves and dB.
hear-frequency.whyHere: RF frequencies are millions of times too high to hear, but the rules that govern them (ratios, octaves, harmonics, logarithmic compression) are the same ones your ear already knows. Hearing them makes the RF math intuitive instead of abstract.
howToUse: Type a frequency in Hz (20 to 20,000) and press Play. Higher number, higher pitch.
Press x2 or div 2 to jump one octave up or down and hear the same note higher or lower; a sounding tone retunes smoothly without a click.

Tap the piano keys C4 to C5, or a preset, to hear named notes; switch between Sine / Square / Triangle to hear timbre. Use the interval explorer to compare any two frequencies (including Wi-Fi bands) as a ratio in octaves and semitones.

inputs.name: Frequency
inputs.range: playback limited to about 20 to 20,000 (the audible range)

inputs.name: Waveform
inputs.range: sine default; sine forced above 5 kHz to avoid aliasing

hear-frequency.algorithm: Note pitches come from 12-tone equal temperament: each note is 440 Hz times 2 raised to the power of the number of semitones away from A4 (the note 440 Hz, ISO 16), divided by 12. One octave is a doubling (12 semitones); one semitone is the 12th root of 2, about 1.05946. The nearest note and how many cents off come from comparing the frequency to 440 Hz on a base-2 scale. Any interval ratio maps to a number of octaves (base-2 log of the ratio) and semitones (12 times that). Harmonics are whole-number multiples: 2f, 3f, 4f, and so on. The tones are generated live as you play.
hear-frequency.example: 440 Hz is A4. Press x2 and you get 880 Hz, which is A5, the same note one octave up. Middle C is 261.63 Hz, and its octave C5 is 523.25 Hz. The step from 2.4 to 4.8 GHz is exactly 1 octave; 2.4 to 6 GHz is about 1.32 octaves (a major third into the next octave).
fieldNotes: An octave is NOT a dB. An octave is a base-2 frequency ratio; a dB is a base-10 power ratio (10 × log10 of a power ratio, or 20 × log10 of an amplitude ratio). Both are logarithmic, which is the real bridge, but they are not the same unit.

Audio harmonics are wanted; RF harmonics usually are not. The whole-number-multiple math (2f, 3f, and so on) is identical, but in music the overtones create timbre, while in a transmitter they are spurious emissions a filter suppresses.
Equal temperament is a compromise: the piano's evenly spaced ratios approximate the pure whole-number harmonic ratios, and the idea that C# equals Db holds true only in equal temperament.
Band labels like 2.4 / 5 / 6 GHz name spans, not single tones, so octave math on one number is illustrative, not a claim about exact channel edges.
No sound? Check that the device isn't muted and the volume is up (especially on iOS, where the hardware mute switch silences playback).
hear-frequency.source: Tones are generated live on your device from the frequency you enter, using standard equal-temperament music math.

ham-band-plan.name: US Amateur Band Plan
ham-band-plan.purpose: The corrected US amateur radio band plan from HF through SHF: each band's frequency range, the Technician / General / Amateur Extra privileges, the maximum power, and the key mode segments, grouped by HF / VHF / UHF / SHF and searchable.
ham-band-plan.whyHere: A Wi-Fi pro who also holds (or is studying for) an amateur license needs the privileges-by-class and power limits in one place. The 13 cm and 5 cm bands also overlap Wi-Fi, so the band edges matter for the Part 15 versus Part 97 picture.
howToUse: Scroll the bands grouped by region, or type a band name or frequency in the search box to filter (for example "20 m" or "14.2").
Each band card shows the frequency range, the Technician / General / Amateur Extra privileges (or a single line where all classes share access), the max power, and any mode notes.
The 60 m channel detail card lists the 4 channels (by center, with the USB dial annotation) plus the 9.15 W ERP segment.
Use Copy results to put the whole plan, the 60 m channels, and the power / data / 9 cm notes on the clipboard as a table.

inputs.name: Search
inputs.range: band name or frequency; empty shows all bands

ham-band-plan.example: Search "60 m" to see the corrected channel plan: 4 channels at 100 W ERP plus the 5351.5-5366.5 kHz segment at 9.15 W ERP. General class runs 1500 W PEP on 80/40/15/10 m; only 30 m (200 W PEP) and 60 m are lower.
fieldNotes: Power column rebuilt from 47 CFR 97.313: a 1500 W PEP general ceiling, with 30 m = 200 W PEP, 60 m = 100 W ERP (channels) / 9.15 W ERP (segment), 2200 m = 1 W EIRP, 630 m = 5 W EIRP, and a Technician on any HF band = 200 W PEP.
60 m is current as of 13 Feb 2026 (4 channels plus the 9.15 W ERP segment); older 5-channel charts are wrong.
There is no symbol-rate (baud) column: the HF baud limit was removed (FCC 23-93) and replaced by a 2.8 kHz bandwidth limit.
9 cm (3.3-3.5 GHz) is omitted: it is in sunset and does not overlap Wi-Fi. Novice and Advanced classes are closed to new applicants and are omitted.
US (FCC) Part 97. Verify current rules before operating; this band plan is a reference, not legal advice.
ham-band-plan.source: A built-in reference table compiled from FCC Part 97, current as of 13 Feb 2026.

ham-band-wavelengths.name: Band Names & Wavelengths
ham-band-wavelengths.purpose: The translation between the two ways of naming the same spectrum: amateur band names (wavelength in meters or centimeters, like 160 m, 2 m, 13 cm) and their actual frequency ranges, with the wavelength = 300/frequency explainer and a worked example each direction.
ham-band-wavelengths.whyHere: Hams name bands by wavelength; Wi-Fi pros name them by frequency. This is the lookup that lets you cross between the two vocabularies, including the Wi-Fi crossover (2.4 GHz sits in the 13 cm band, 5 GHz in the 5 cm band).
howToUse: Read the two-column table: each amateur band name maps to its frequency range and region tag.
The formula card gives the exact wavelength in meters = 299.792458 / frequency in MHz, plus the wavelength ≈ 300 / frequency working approximation.
The worked examples show name-to-frequency, frequency-to-name, and the Wi-Fi crossover.
ham-band-wavelengths.algorithm: Wavelength in meters is 299.792458 divided by the frequency in MHz (and the reverse gives frequency from wavelength). As a quick working approximation, wavelength is about 300 divided by the frequency, which is within roughly 0.07 percent.
ham-band-wavelengths.example: 146 MHz gives a wavelength of 299.792458 / 146 = 2.05 m, which is the "2 m" band. 2442 MHz (Wi-Fi channel 7) gives 12.28 cm, which is the "13 cm" amateur band.
fieldNotes: The wavelength is the nominal band name; the listed range is the actual amateur allocation, not a literal 300/frequency conversion of the edges.
9 cm (3300-3500 MHz) is shown for completeness but is in sunset; it does not overlap Wi-Fi 5 GHz.
ham-band-wavelengths.source: A built-in reference table with wavelengths derived from frequency using the speed of light.

band-designations.name: Spectrum Band Designations
band-designations.purpose: The ITU decade-band designations (HF / VHF / UHF / SHF) with the propagation behavior each implies, plus the non-amateur neighbors a Wi-Fi pro should recognize: the VHF aviation airband (108-137 MHz AM) and the military UHF airband (225-400 MHz AM).
band-designations.whyHere: Wi-Fi 2.4 GHz lives in UHF and 5/6 GHz in SHF. Knowing the band names and their propagation explains why a 2.4 GHz signal behaves nothing like an HF one, and who else shares the spectrum with you.
howToUse: Read each ITU band card: its frequency and wavelength span and what its propagation means in practice.
The neighbors card lists the aviation and military airbands and the ISM / U-NII bands that overlap amateur allocations.
Copy results puts both tables on the clipboard.
band-designations.example: HF (3-30 MHz) is sky-wave / ionospheric skip, the talk-around-the-world bands. SHF (3-30 GHz) is strict line-of-sight, where Wi-Fi 5/6 GHz lives and rain fade begins.
fieldNotes: Each ITU band is ten times the previous one. Below HF: MF (300 kHz-3 MHz) and LF (30-300 kHz). Above SHF: EHF (30-300 GHz).
121.5 MHz is the civil aviation emergency Guard frequency; 243.0 MHz is the military Guard.
Airband and military allocations are confirmed against the FCC Table of Frequency Allocations.
band-designations.source: A built-in reference table based on ITU band designations and the FCC Table of Frequency Allocations.

part15-part97.name: Part 15 vs Part 97
part15-part97.purpose: Where the amateur 13 cm and 5 cm bands overlap the 2.4 GHz and 5 GHz Wi-Fi bands, mapped to the Wi-Fi channel grid, and the rule-by-rule comparison between Part 15 (unlicensed Wi-Fi) and Part 97 (licensed amateur): license, power, station ID, encryption, and commercial use.
part15-part97.whyHere: The same 802.11 chipset can run under Part 15 or Part 97. Amateur mesh networks like AREDN run everyday Wi-Fi hardware on the overlapping channels under Part 97 for the higher power ceiling, giving up encryption and commercial use in exchange. This is the signature Wi-Fi-to-ham bridge.
howToUse: The overlap card maps each amateur band to the Wi-Fi band it covers and the Wi-Fi channels that fall inside it.
The comparison card lines up Part 15 and Part 97 rule by rule; each side is always spelled out in words.
The AREDN card is the real-world example.
part15-part97.example: Amateur 13 cm (2390-2450 MHz) covers Wi-Fi channels 1-6 fully. Under Part 15 you are capped at 4 W EIRP, with encryption allowed and no ID required; under Part 97 you may run far more power but must ID by callsign every 10 minutes and run no encryption.
fieldNotes: Part 15 limits are 47 CFR 15.247 / 15.407; Part 97 conduct rules are 47 CFR 97.113 / 97.119 / 97.313.
9 cm (3.3-3.5 GHz) is being sunset and does NOT overlap Wi-Fi 5 GHz; don't confuse it with the 5 cm band.
Part 97 prohibits encryption that hides the meaning of a message, so amateur mesh traffic must travel in the clear.
part15-part97.source: A built-in reference table drawn from FCC Part 15 and Part 97 rules.

ham-study-resources.name: Ham Radio Study Resources
ham-study-resources.purpose: A vetted, offline list of where to study for the US amateur-radio exams: hamstudy.org, the ARRL license manuals, FCC Part 97, and AREDN, each credited, with the stable exam structure and the two currency caveats that shape how to read any older chart.
ham-study-resources.whyHere: The exam-physics and band material is best served by pointing to the current, maintained study platforms rather than freezing a static notes sheet. This curates the strongest pointers and bakes in the two landmines: the new Technician question pool and the 60 m rule change.
howToUse: Read the two currency caveats at the top first; they govern everything below.
Each resource card shows what it's for, the classes it covers, how current it is, the credit line, and a vet note. Tap Open website to launch it in your browser.
The exam structure card lists the question count and pass threshold for each element.
ham-study-resources.example: Technician and General are each 35 questions, 26 to pass; Amateur Extra is 50 questions, 37 to pass. hamstudy.org tracks the live question pools, including the 1 Jul 2026 Technician pool.
fieldNotes: A new Technician question pool takes effect 1 Jul 2026. The exam structure is stable at 35 questions / 26 to pass; the pool's question count rotates every four years, so it is deliberately not stated here.
The 60 m rules changed 13 Feb 2026; any chart or notes predating it are wrong on 60 m.
No Morse code requirement since 23 Feb 2007; the FCC application fee is $35.
The ARRL band chart is copyrighted; this app builds its own band plan from FCC Part 97 data and credits ARRL rather than reproducing the chart graphic.
ham-study-resources.source: A curated list of maintained study resources, each credited to its source.

general-license-frequency-chart.name: General License Frequency Chart
general-license-frequency-chart.purpose: A ready-to-read reference card with Keith's corrected frequency chart for a US General-class amateur radio license: which HF, VHF, UHF, and SHF bands you can use, the power limits, and the current 60 m channel plan. It lives inside the app and works offline, and you can pinch to zoom.
general-license-frequency-chart.whyHere: If you hold (or are studying for) a General-class amateur license, this puts the band edges, segment limits, and power ceilings on the phone already in your hand, with no signal needed. The 13 cm and 5 cm amateur bands also overlap Wi-Fi, so those edges matter when you're thinking about where amateur rules end and Wi-Fi rules begin.
howToUse: Open the card; it fits the screen automatically. Pinch or double-tap to zoom in.
Read across each band for its frequency segments, the voice sideband, and the maximum power column. The 60 m detail table lists the four channels by center frequency plus the 9.15 W ERP segment.

This card is a flat printed page, so a screen reader can't read the content inside it. If you need spoken-out or searchable text, use the interactive US Amateur Band Plan reference, which covers the same information.
fieldNotes: Frequency segments follow the FCC General-class rules; the power figures follow the FCC power rules: a 1500 W PEP ceiling overall, with 30 m capped at 200 W PEP, 60 m channels at 100 W ERP (and the 9.15 W ERP segment), 2200 m at 1 W EIRP, and 630 m at 5 W EIRP. A Technician operating on HF is capped at 200 W PEP.

The 60 m information is current as of 13 Feb 2026 (4 channels plus the 9.15 W ERP segment). Older 5-channel charts are wrong. There is no HF symbol-rate (baud) limit anymore; data is governed instead by a 2.8 kHz bandwidth limit.
The 9 cm band (3.3 to 3.5 GHz) is left off because it is being phased out and has no Wi-Fi overlap. This card reflects US (FCC) rules and is a reference, not legal advice.
A screen reader can't read the content inside the card because it's a flat image of a printed page. The card title and the pinch-to-zoom gesture are announced.
Based on Keith Parsons' corrected General License Frequency Chart. The card is stored in the app and displayed offline.
general-license-frequency-chart.source: Keith Parsons' corrected General License Frequency Chart, shown as a bundled reference card that works offline.

ham-radio-general-exam-study-notes.name: Ham Radio General Exam Study Notes
ham-radio-general-exam-study-notes.purpose: A ready-to-read study sheet with Keith's condensed notes for the US amateur radio General-class exam (Element 3): the rules, operating practice, RF safety, antennas, and propagation you need to know, written for someone who already understands RF from Wi-Fi work. It lives inside the app, works offline, and you can pinch to zoom.
ham-radio-general-exam-study-notes.whyHere: If you're a Wi-Fi pro studying for the General upgrade, this gives you one offline notes sheet you can review anywhere, using the RF concepts you already know as the on-ramp to the amateur-specific rules and operating practice.
howToUse: Open the card; it fits the screen automatically. Pinch or double-tap to zoom in.
Use it as a revision sheet alongside a live question pool. Pair it with the Ham Radio Study Resources tile for hamstudy.org and the ARRL manuals.
This card is a flat printed page, so a screen reader can't read the content inside it.
fieldNotes: The General exam (Element 3) has 35 questions, and you need 26 correct to pass. The question pool rotates on a four-year cycle, so these notes focus on the stable concepts rather than memorized pool answers.
Check the current rules before you operate: 60 m changed on 13 Feb 2026 and the Technician question pool changes on 1 Jul 2026. This reflects US (FCC) rules and is a study aid, not legal advice.
A screen reader can't read the content inside the card because it's a flat image of a printed page. The card title and the pinch-to-zoom gesture are announced.
Based on Keith Parsons' Ham Radio General Exam Study Notes. The card is stored in the app and displayed offline.
ham-radio-general-exam-study-notes.source: Keith Parsons' Ham Radio General Exam Study Notes, shown as a bundled reference card that works offline.

eirp.name: EIRP Calculator
eirp.purpose: Works out your EIRP (Effective Isotropic Radiated Power), the real power leaving your antenna once you account for transmitter power, cable and connector loss, and antenna gain.
eirp.whyHere: EIRP is the number regulators cap. Check it against FCC or ETSI limits, and use it as the transmit-side starting point for a link budget.
howToUse: Enter your transmit power and pick its unit (dBm, W, or mW).
Enter cable loss in dB and antenna gain in dBi.
Read the EIRP in dBm, plus a second line in watts or milliwatts.

inputs.name: TX power
inputs.range: In watts or milliwatts it must be greater than 0; a zero or negative value can't be converted and leaves the result blank.

eirp.algorithm: EIRP in dBm is simply your transmit power plus antenna gain minus cable loss. If you enter power in watts or milliwatts, it's first converted to dBm. The result is also shown in watts (or milliwatts when under 1 W) so you can compare against regulatory limits stated either way.
eirp.example: Transmit power 20 dBm, cable loss 1.5 dB, antenna gain 14 dBi gives 20 minus 1.5 plus 14, which is 32.5 dBm, or about 1.78 W.
fieldNotes: Your cable loss figure should include every connector and jumper loss on the transmit chain.

The reference card lists regulatory EIRP ceilings (for example, 2.4 GHz FCC point-to-multipoint at +36 dBm, or 4 W). Treat those as planning anchors; they vary by sub-band, channel width, and power-control rules, so always check current local regulations.
eirp.source: Standard EIRP arithmetic done on the values you enter, right on your device.

fresnel.name: Fresnel Zone
fresnel.purpose: Works out the radius of the first Fresnel zone along a point-to-point path, plus the 60% clearance figure planners treat as the keep-it-clear threshold.
fresnel.whyHere: A clear line of sight isn't enough for a reliable link; the first Fresnel zone needs to be mostly clear too. This tells you how much clearance you need above terrain, rooftops, and trees.
howToUse: Enter the frequency in GHz.
Enter the total path distance in meters.
Optionally, enter a distance from the transmitter to get the radius at that exact point; leave it blank to see just the midpoint (the widest point).
Read the first-zone radius and the 60% clearance, shown in meters with feet alongside.

inputs.name: Point from TX
inputs.range: Must fall somewhere between 0 and the total distance to add an at-that-point result; otherwise it's ignored.

fresnel.algorithm: The zone is widest at the midpoint and pinches toward each end. At any point, the radius grows with the wavelength and with how far the point sits from each end of the path. Wavelength comes from the frequency (about 0.3 divided by the frequency in GHz, in meters). The tool always shows the midpoint radius (the worst case) and multiplies it by 0.6 for the 60% clearance figure. Feet come from multiplying meters by 3.28084.
fresnel.example: At 5.8 GHz over a 10,000 m path, the wavelength is about 0.0517 m, the midpoint radius works out to about 11.37 m, and 60% clearance is about 6.82 m.
fieldNotes: The midpoint radius is the worst case along the path, so clearance there is the constraint that binds.
The frequency unit here is fixed to GHz (unlike the FSPL tool, which also offers MHz).
The 60% clearance figure is the common rule of thumb; some designs need more in heavy foliage or reflective environments.
fresnel.source: Standard Fresnel-zone geometry computed from the frequency and distances you enter, right on your device.

cable-loss.name: Cable Loss
cable-loss.purpose: Estimates the total signal loss for a run of coax of a known type, length, and frequency, plus the loss per 100 feet at that frequency.
cable-loss.whyHere: Cable loss comes straight off both your transmit and receive signal. Pick a cable type and length, and the tool tells you how much signal the feedline will eat.
howToUse: Pick the cable type from the list (six LMR types).
Enter the frequency and pick its unit (GHz or MHz).
Enter the run length and pick its unit (feet or meters).
Read the total loss in dB plus the per-100-foot figure.

inputs.name: Cable type
inputs.unit: One of: LMR-100A, LMR-200, LMR-400 (default), LMR-600, LMR-900, LMR-1200

cable-loss.algorithm: Times Microwave publishes an attenuation equation on every LMR datasheet: loss per 100 feet equals k1 times the square root of the frequency in MHz, plus k2 times the frequency in MHz, where k1 and k2 are constants for that specific cable. The tool evaluates that equation at your exact frequency, then scales it by your run length (loss per 100 feet times length in feet, divided by 100). Nothing is interpolated between table rows, so there is no frequency at which the tool has to guess. For example, LMR-400 at 2400 MHz is 6.6 dB per 100 feet.
cable-loss.example: LMR-400 at 2.4 GHz over 50 feet: 6.6 dB per 100 feet times 50, divided by 100, is 3.31 dB total.
fieldNotes: These are manufacturer figures at the datasheet's stated conditions (VSWR 1.0, 25 C). Real loss rises with heat and age, and connectors add loss this tool doesn't count.
The equation is Times Microwave's own, so it reproduces their published tables wherever the two overlap, and it stays exact at frequencies they never tabulated (2400 MHz among them).

There is no RG-58, RG-8, RG-213 or RG-214 here, on purpose. "RG" is not one cable: Belden 8259 and Belden 9201 are both stamped RG-58 and differ by 54 percent at 900 MHz, and no manufacturer publishes RG attenuation at Wi-Fi frequencies at all. For an RG run, read dB/100 ft off your actual cable's datasheet by part number.

Five cables are offered (LMR-100A, LMR-200, LMR-400, LMR-600, LMR-900). Times Microwave tabulates all five to 5800 MHz, so the published attenuation equation is only ever used inside its validated range. LMR-1200 is deliberately omitted: Times stops tabulating it at 2500 MHz and the equation under-predicts its loss at Wi-Fi frequencies.
Above 5800 MHz the tool says plainly that it is extrapolating rather than quietly showing a confident number. Treat a 6 GHz result as a lower bound and confirm against your cable's datasheet.
cable-loss.source: Times Microwave LMR datasheet attenuation equations (k1, k2 per cable), evaluated on your device.

architectural-scale.name: Architectural Scale
architectural-scale.purpose: Converts a named drawing scale to its dimensionless ratio and back, and converts a distance measured on a drawing to its real-world length (or the reverse). Covers US architectural fractional-inch scales, engineer's decimal scales, and common metric scales.
architectural-scale.whyHere: WLAN pros work inside plan sets and scaled PDFs they were never taught to read. Every coverage prediction, wall distance, and mounting height is wrong if the scale is wrong. This is the same calibration step Ekahau, Hamina, and iBwave ask for on import.
howToUse: Pick the scale family (Architectural, Engineering, or Metric).
Pick the specific scale, for example 1/4" = 1'-0". Read its ratio (1:48).
In Measure, choose a direction: Drawn → Real, or Real → Drawn.
Set the drawing units (in / mm / cm) and real-world units (ft / m).
Enter your measurement and read the converted length, with a friendly feet-inches or fractional-inch form beneath it.

inputs.name: Scale
inputs.unit: A named scale within the family (default 1/4" = 1'-0")

inputs.name: Measurement
inputs.unit: Drawing units (in / mm / cm) or real-world units (ft / m), per direction

architectural-scale.algorithm: ratio = real ÷ drawn. Imperial: ratio = 12 ÷ inches-per-foot (1/8" = 1'-0" → 12 ÷ 0.125 = 96 → 1:96). Engineer's: feet-per-inch × 12 (1" = 20' → 240 → 1:240). Metric scales are already ratios. Drawn → real multiplies the measurement by the ratio; real → drawn divides. The ratio is dimensionless, so the conversion runs through a millimetre base and the two ends can carry different units.
architectural-scale.example: 3.5" measured on a 1/8" = 1'-0" (1:96) sheet is 3.5 × 96 = 336 inches = 28 ft. In reverse, a 45 ft hallway at 1/4" = 1'-0" (1:48) draws at 45 ÷ 48 = 0.9375 ft = 11-1/4 inches.
fieldNotes: The result is only as good as the scale. If a PDF has no embedded scale, confirm which scale it is by measuring a known dimension: a 3'-0" door or a 2×4 ft ceiling tile.
Printed and PDF plans are often not to true scale (fit-to-page printing, cropped sheets). Trust a dimensioned callout on the sheet over any measurement.
US-primary: architectural scales use fractional inches, engineer's scales use decimal feet per inch. Metric ratios (1:50, 1:100) are provided for ISO drawing sets.

enclosure-ratings.name: Enclosure Ratings
enclosure-ratings.purpose: A field reference for the two enclosure-rating systems a WLAN pro reads on outdoor and industrial spec sheets: the IP code (IEC 60529, international) and NEMA types (NEMA 250, US). It decodes each IP digit, lists the NEMA types that matter for Wi-Fi, and shows the one-way NEMA-to-IP translation.
enclosure-ratings.whyHere: US spec sheets quote NEMA and international sheets quote IP, so pros translate between them to compare gear on the same terms and spec an enclosure, AP, antenna, or PoE injector to the real hazard at the mount point.
howToUse: Read an IP code left to right: first digit is solids and dust (0 to 6), second digit is water (0 to 9K). Each digit maps to a defined lab test.
Use the common-ratings table to place a code (for example IP66 = dust-tight plus powerful jets, the mainstream outdoor AP rating).
For a US spec sheet, read the NEMA type, then use the NEMA-to-IP table as a minimum floor. The reverse (IP to NEMA) is not valid.
Use the placement table to pick a rating for where the gear mounts.
fieldNotes: X means not tested, not zero: IPX7 is water-rated 7 with the solids digit unrated. Read X as no data, never as fails.
NEMA to IP is valid only as a minimum. NEMA tests corrosion, icing, gasket aging, and oil, which IP never checks, so a NEMA 4X box exceeds IP66 but an IP66 box is not automatically NEMA 4X.
The water ladder is not a clean superset past 6: an IP67 (immersion) device is not guaranteed to pass IP66 (jets). Gear that must survive both is dual-marked, such as IP66/IP67.
IP tests use fresh water only. Salt, detergents, and solvents need separate chemical-resistance verification.
This is a field reference, not code or design guidance. Confirm requirements with the AHJ, the architect of record, and a licensed electrician.
enclosure-ratings.source: IP per IEC 60529; NEMA per NEMA 250.

hazardous-locations.name: Hazardous Locations
hazardous-locations.purpose: A recognize-and-defer field reference for classified (hazardous) areas: the NEC Class and Division system (US, Article 500) and the IEC Zone system (Article 505/506, ATEX, IECEx). It names what the hazard is (Class), how often it is present (Division / Zone), the recognized protection concepts, and the field read, then stops.
hazardous-locations.whyHere: A standard commercial AP is a genuine ignition source in a flammable atmosphere. Mounting one in a classified area is illegal, uninsurable, and dangerous. WLAN pros need to recognize a refinery, grain elevator, fuel depot, or spray booth before quoting, and route the install to rated gear and the AHJ.
howToUse: Read Class as what the hazard is made of: Class I (gases/vapors), Class II (dust), Class III (fibers/flyings).
Read Division as how often it is present: Div 1 during normal operation, Div 2 only under fault. Div 2 is the far larger wireless market.
Map to the IEC Zone system where a facility uses it (Div 1 is roughly Zone 0+1 for gas; Div 2 is roughly Zone 2).
Match a recognized protection concept (Ex d, Ex i, Ex p, Ex e/nR) to the Division or Zone, or keep the AP out and remote the antenna in.
fieldNotes: Never just mount a commercial AP in a classified area. The default move is to keep the AP in the adjacent general-purpose area and remote the antenna into the zone through a rated penetration.

"Class I Div 2 rated" buys permission to install that specific listed device, in that specific Division or Zone, wired per its control drawing. It is not a blanket safe-anywhere stamp; a Div 2 device is not approved for Div 1.
You cannot mix Division and Zone classification in the same installation. Which one is in force is set by the facility area-classification drawing and the AHJ, not the installer.
This is a field reference, not code or design guidance. Confirm requirements with the AHJ, the architect of record, and a licensed electrician.
hazardous-locations.source: Class/Division per NEC Article 500; Zone per NEC 505/506, ATEX, and IECEx.

nec-gotchas.name: NEC Gotchas
nec-gotchas.purpose: A recognize-and-defer field reference for the NEC articles that actually bite a WLAN installer: elevator hoistways (620), plenum air spaces (300.22), the communications-cable rating ladder (800), PoE bundle heat (725.144), antenna and mast grounding (810), firestopping fire-rated assemblies (300.21), and abandoned cable (800.25).
nec-gotchas.whyHere: These are the code articles most likely to surface on an everyday install. Recognizing each one on site, then handing it to the AHJ, a licensed electrician, or the equipment listing, keeps a WLAN pro out of trouble. This is recognize-and-defer, never how-to-comply.
howToUse: Use it to recognize a code issue on site: the hoistway you cannot put an AP in, the plenum that dictates your cable jacket, the PoE bundle that can overheat, the antenna that needs grounding, the fire wall you must not breach, and the dead cable you must pull.
Read the cable-rating ladder as a jacket fire-rating only (CMP plenum, CMR riser, CM/CMG general, CMX limited); substitution runs downhill only.
On the two STOP items (PoE ampacity, firestop assembly), do not eyeball a number or pick an assembly yourself; hand it to the licensed electrician or the listed system.
Then defer to the right authority for the actual requirement.
fieldNotes: STOP on PoE bundle heat (725.144): the exact ampacity and bundle-count numbers come from the code table and the specific install conditions. Do not eyeball them. Size the bundle with a licensed electrician or the cabling designer against the adopted NEC.
STOP on firestopping (300.21): the approved firestop is a specific listed assembly matched to the wall type, penetrant, and opening. Never improvise it and never pick the assembly yourself.
Grounding (810) caveat: nothing survives a direct strike. Bonding and surge protection mitigate nearby strikes and static, not a direct hit. Conductor sizing is an electrician's call.
This is a field reference, not code or design guidance. Confirm requirements with the AHJ, the architect of record, and a licensed electrician.
nec-gotchas.source: Article numbers per the NEC (NFPA 70); confirm against the locally adopted edition.

safety-basics.name: Safety Basics
safety-basics.purpose: A field-awareness reference for the personal protective equipment a general contractor expects before badging you onto an active site, a short note on ESD (protecting the electronics you install, not the person), and the recognize-and-STOP hazards a WLAN pro must hand off rather than work.
safety-basics.whyHere: Half of a WLAN pro's installs happen on a ladder or lift, on an active construction site, around other trades. Knowing the PPE ratings a GC will ask for keeps you from being turned away at the gate, cheap ESD gear prevents latent failures in the gear you install, and the STOP flags keep a routine cable pull from becoming a health or legal incident. This is awareness and lookup, not compliance instruction.
howToUse: Use the PPE ladder to recognize the four common baseline items and the standard each is rated to (hard hat Z89.1, safety-toe F2413, hi-vis ANSI 107, eye protection Z87.1).
Read the ESD note as gear protection: a grounded wrist strap, mat, and static bags under ANSI/ESD S20.20 guard against latent damage to APs, optics, and boards.
On the four recognize-and-STOP hazards (asbestos/lead, arc flash and energized work, confined spaces, seismic bracing), stop and hand it off. Never run any of them as a procedure yourself.
Confirm the site's specific PPE policy with the employer and GC, who own the assessment.
fieldNotes: The four PPE items are the common 'let me on the site' baseline. On a finished office install street clothes are usually fine; on active construction expect the GC to require all four to badge on.
ESD risk is highest in data centers and any time you handle bare optics or boards. For a sealed AP coming out of a static bag, mounted and cabled, the risk is lower but not zero.
The recognize-and-STOP items are named-and-stopped on purpose: the Toolbox names the hazard and tells you to hand it off, and deliberately gives no procedure for working any of them.
This is a field reference, not code or design guidance. Confirm requirements with the AHJ, the architect of record, and a licensed electrician.
safety-basics.source: Standards designators (ANSI/ISEA Z89.1, ASTM F2413, ANSI/ISEA 107, ANSI Z87.1, ANSI/ESD S20.20, NFPA 70E); confirm the site's PPE policy with the employer and GC.

plan-set-literacy.name: Plan-Set Literacy
plan-set-literacy.purpose: A field reference for reading an architectural drawing set: how a sheet number is built (discipline letter, sheet-type digit, sequence), what each discipline designator and sheet-type digit means, why the Reflected Ceiling Plan (RCP) is the sheet for AP placement, the rest of a plan set worth knowing, and US drawing scales.
plan-set-literacy.whyHere: A WLAN designer who can pull the right sheet, read the RCP, and speak in sheet numbers looks like a peer to the other trades instead of a junior. The RCP is where a coverage design meets the physical ceiling, and catching an AP-versus-diffuser conflict on paper is far cheaper than catching it at rough-in.
howToUse: Read a sheet number left to right: discipline letter, sheet-type digit, sequence. A-201 is Architectural, an elevation, sheet 01.
Use the discipline table to find the sheet owner (A architectural, E electrical, T telecommunications; the WLAN and structured cabling usually live on the T sheets).
Use the sheet-type digit to find the drawing kind (1 plans, 2 elevations, 6 schedules), so A-1xx is an architectural plan and E-6xx an electrical schedule.
For AP placement, open the RCP: overlay your AP locations and resolve conflicts with diffusers, troffers, sprinklers, and soffits before rough-in. Never place APs on the floor plan alone.
fieldNotes: The RCP is drawn as if a mirror on the floor reflects the ceiling upward, so it reads in the same left-right orientation as the floor plan; it carries the ceiling grid, mounting heights, and every ceiling-mounted element.
Always build to the latest revision: the revision cloud marks what changed and the delta triangle carries the revision number.
US architectural sheets use fractional-inch scales (1/8" = 1'-0" is 1:96); to get the ratio, invert the fraction and multiply by 12.
Reference only. Confirm drawing conventions, revisions, and responsibility with the architect of record and your contract for the specific project.
plan-set-literacy.source: Conventions per the US National CAD Standard; sheet-numbering practice varies on smaller jobs.

site-access.name: Site Access
site-access.purpose: A 'Know Before You Go' pre-mobilization checklist: eight site types (aerial and man-lifts, rail, hospitals, maritime, warehouse and distribution, schools, data centers, correctional) and the credential, screening, orientation, or escort that can gate you from reaching the work before it even starts.
site-access.whyHere: On many sites you cannot reach the work area without a specific credential, background check, orientation, or escort. That is a quoting and scheduling factor, not just a safety one. Rail screening, a hospital ICRA permit, or lift-operator proof can each add days or weeks between winning a job and touching a cable.
howToUse: Before you quote, scan the checklist for the site type you are bidding and read what may gate you.
Use the 'ask about' column as the questions to put to the GC, site owner, or authority: screening programs, orientations, escorts, flagman, PFD and TWIC, ICRA and ILSM, tool control.
Budget the lead time (rail screening and hospital ICRA in particular run long) into the schedule you promise.
Confirm each requirement with the site, general contractor, and authority before you mobilize; the Toolbox certifies nothing and clears no one.
fieldNotes: The pattern across every item: the requirement is set by someone other than you, must be satisfied before work starts, and carries real lead time and cost.
Maritime and over-water work adds PFD and drowning-hazard rules plus heavy salt corrosion, so spec NEMA 4X or high-IP with explicit corrosion resistance.
There is no OSHA-issued 'lift license'; the employer trains and certifies lift operators, and GCs demand documented proof.
This is a field reference, not code or design guidance. The site, general contractor, and authority set every requirement; confirm each one with them before you mobilize.
site-access.source: Access requirements are set by the site, GC, and authority having jurisdiction; confirm each before mobilizing.

cad-bim-formats.name: CAD & BIM Formats
cad-bim-formats.purpose: A field reference for what the building files an architect hands you actually are: the format decode table (DWG, DXF, DGN, IFC, RVT, NWD/NWC, COBie), the Level of Development (LOD 100-500) ladder for how much of a model to trust, and how a building file flows into a Wi-Fi design tool (Ekahau, Hamina, iBwave).
cad-bim-formats.whyHere: You receive these files on every design job. The difference between a clean import and a wasted afternoon is knowing what the format is, what LOD you were handed, which CAD layers to ask for, and that scale calibration is the single step that makes or breaks the whole design. The Toolbox explains these formats; it does not open, render, or convert them.
howToUse: Use the decode table to recognize a format on sight: DWG and DXF are Autodesk CAD, IFC is the open BIM standard, RVT is a Revit model, NWD/NWC are Navisworks coordination files, COBie is asset data.
Read the LOD you were handed before trusting geometry: LOD 100-200 is a massing study (do not derive wall attenuation from it), LOD 300 and up is dimensionally trustworthy.
Follow the import flow: import the CAD or PDF, calibrate the scale on a known distance (get this wrong and every downstream distance is wrong), then assign wall materials and run the prediction.
Ask the architect for a clean layer set (walls, doors, structure; strip furniture, dimensions, title blocks) instead of an unusable 80-layer dump.
fieldNotes: Scale calibration is the step that matters most: draw a line over a known length and enter the real measurement. An error here corrupts every distance, coverage prediction, and attenuation value downstream.
LOI (Level of Information) is the data-completeness sibling of LOD: LOD is geometry, LOI is data.
The boundary: the Toolbox explains DWG, IFC, RVT, and the rest and how they reach the design tools. It is not a CAD or BIM viewer, converter, or editor.
Reference only. Confirm file handling, model reliability, and responsibility with the architect of record and your design-tool documentation.
cad-bim-formats.source: Format and LOD conventions per Autodesk, buildingSMART, and the AIA/AGC LOD framework.

structured-cabling.name: Structured Cabling
structured-cabling.purpose: A field reference for the TIA and BICSI structured-cabling standards that decide where an AP can go and what feeds it: the TIA-568 family, the 90 m permanent link plus 10 m of cords for a 100 m channel, the cable-category ladder (Cat 5e through Cat 8), and the MDF-IDF topology that ties AP locations to telecom rooms.
structured-cabling.whyHere: The 90 m rule and the Cat 6A bar shape real WLAN decisions: they set the outer edge of where you can place an AP and what uplink it can carry, which quietly drives IDF placement, switch selection, and the whole coverage plan. The physical grounding-as-safety side lives in the NEC codes reference; this is the TIA and BICSI infrastructure side.
howToUse: Use the TIA family to know who owns what: 568 is cabling, 569 pathways and spaces, 606 labeling and administration, 607 telecom bonding and grounding.
Apply the 90+10 m channel rule as an AP-cable-run reality check: an AP more than about 90 m of cable-path from the IDF needs an intermediate closet, a different topology, or fiber.
Read the cable-category table for the multi-gig bar: Cat 6A carries 10 Gbps to 100 m and is the practical minimum for Wi-Fi 6, 6E, and 7 APs with multi-gig uplinks.
Coordinate with the RCDD (BICSI's cabling-design credential) whose 90 m constraint ties AP locations to IDF locations.
fieldNotes: The 90 m permanent link is solid horizontal cable from the telecom room to the outlet; add up to 10 m of stranded patch and equipment cords for a 100 m channel maximum. Exceed it and you are outside TIA.
T568A and T568B are the two pin-out standards; a jack is wired to one, so be consistent end to end.
AP count and placement drive IDF count and PoE-switch port budgeting, so the WLAN design and the cabling design have to talk to each other.
Reference only. Confirm cabling design and standards currency with the RCDD, the architect of record, and your contract for the specific project.
structured-cabling.source: Standards per ANSI/TIA-568/569/606/607 and BICSI; confirm the current edition.

aec-process-glossary.name: AEC Process & Glossary
aec-process-glossary.purpose: A field reference for the architecture-engineering-construction workflow you work inside and the shorthand the other trades use: the AIA design phases (Programming, SD, DD, CD, Bidding, CA) and when Wi-Fi should engage, plus the AEC glossary (RFI, submittal, ASI, AHJ, GC, MEP, OAC, AOR/EOR, and more) that trips WLAN pros up. US convention (AIA).
aec-process-glossary.whyHere: Speak the process fluently and you look like a peer in the room instead of the network person who wandered in. The RFI, the submittal, the punch list, and the OAC are the levers that get your coverage design built the way you drew it. Engaging at Schematic Design is the difference between designing Wi-Fi in and retrofitting it later.
howToUse: Use the phase table to engage at the right moment: establish RF requirements and reserve IDF and ceiling access at SD, coordinate AP locations against the RCP at DD, finalize plans and telecom sheets at CD.
Raise an RFI the moment the Reflected Ceiling Plan and your AP plan disagree, so the conflict is resolved on paper before rough-in.
Get your APs, mounts, and cable approved as a submittal (the contractor's proof that what is installed matches the spec).
Treat anything about contractual responsibility as 'confirm with the architect of record and your contract,' never as a ruling.
fieldNotes: The AHJ (Authority Having Jurisdiction) is the building official, fire marshal, or inspector who interprets and enforces code locally; the AHJ's word governs.
An ASI is a minor clarification with no cost or time impact; a Change Order is a formal, signed change to scope, cost, or schedule (what an ASI is not).
The OAC is the recurring Owner-Architect-Contractor coordination meeting; if Wi-Fi matters, someone has to carry it into the OAC.
Reference only. Confirm contractual responsibility, code compliance, and phase deliverables with the architect of record, the AHJ, and your contract for the specific project.
aec-process-glossary.source: Phase and contract vocabulary per US AIA convention.

link-budget.name: Link Budget
link-budget.purpose: A full point-to-point link budget: it adds up transmit power, antenna gains, cable losses, path loss, and any other losses into a received signal level, then compares that against your receiver's sensitivity to give a fade margin.
link-budget.whyHere: This is the tool for deciding whether a link will close, and by how much. Add up all the gains and losses to see the margin before you deploy.
howToUse: Enter your transmit power and pick its unit (dBm, W, or mW).
Enter transmit antenna gain, transmit cable loss, path loss, receive cable loss, receive antenna gain, and receiver sensitivity.
Optionally enter any other or miscellaneous losses (treated as 0 if left blank).
Read the received signal in dBm and the link margin in dB, color-coded by health.

inputs.name: TX power
inputs.range: In watts or milliwatts it must be greater than 0

link-budget.algorithm: The received signal is your transmit power, plus both antenna gains, minus every loss along the way (transmit cable, path, receive cable, and any miscellaneous). The link margin is that received signal minus the receiver's sensitivity. Margins are graded: 10 dB or more is healthy (green), 0 to 10 dB is marginal (amber), and below 0 is negative (red). Power entered in watts or milliwatts is converted to dBm first.
link-budget.example: Transmit 30 dBm, transmit gain 20, transmit loss 2, path loss 120, receive loss 2, receive gain 20, no misc loss, receiver sensitivity minus 85: received signal is minus 54 dBm, and the margin is 31 dB, which is healthy.
fieldNotes: You supply the path loss yourself; work it out with the FSPL tool, then add real-world margins.
This budget doesn't include rain fade; use the PtP Link Check for that.
A healthy margin of 10 dB or more is the common design target, giving you room to ride out fading and multipath.
link-budget.source: Standard link-budget arithmetic done on the values you enter, right on your device.

noise-floor.name: Noise Floor
noise-floor.purpose: Works out the thermal noise floor for a channel, the receiver's noise floor (thermal noise plus its noise figure), and the quick minus 174 dBm per Hz rule-of-thumb value.
noise-floor.whyHere: Signal-to-noise ratio is your signal minus the noise floor. Knowing the noise floor for a given channel width sets the minimum usable signal and frames any link-margin discussion.
howToUse: Pick the channel bandwidth.
Enter the receiver's noise figure in dB.
Optionally adjust the temperature (defaults to 20 degrees C if left blank).
Read the thermal noise floor, the receiver noise floor, and the rule-of-thumb value.

inputs.name: Noise figure
inputs.range: Must be 0 or more (defaults to 7; a negative or invalid value leaves the outputs blank)

noise-floor.algorithm: Thermal noise rises with both temperature and channel width; wider channels let in more noise. The tool computes the thermal noise floor from those two, then adds your receiver's noise figure to get the receiver noise floor. It also shows a quick rule-of-thumb value: minus 174 dBm per Hz (thermal noise at about 0 degrees C) plus 10 times the log of the bandwidth in Hz.
noise-floor.example: At 20 MHz, noise figure 7 dB, 20 degrees C: the thermal floor is about minus 100.9 dBm, the receiver floor about minus 93.9 dBm, and the rule-of-thumb value about minus 101.0 dBm.
fieldNotes: This is the theoretical noise floor. Real receivers sit higher because of co-channel interference, adjacent-channel leakage, and ambient RF.
The minus 174 rule is a 0 degrees C approximation, so it differs slightly from the temperature-aware thermal value.
noise-floor.source: Standard thermal-noise physics computed from the bandwidth, noise figure, and temperature you enter, on your device.

rain-fade.name: ITU Rain Fade
rain-fade.purpose: Estimates how much rain will weaken a microwave link, using the ITU-R P.838-3 model for specific attenuation and a simplified ITU-R P.530 for effective path length.
rain-fade.whyHere: Above roughly 10 GHz, rain is the biggest cause of fading on outdoor links. This sizes the fade margin a backhaul link needs to keep working through heavy rain.
howToUse: Enter the frequency in GHz.
Enter the rain rate in mm/hr.
Enter the path length and pick its unit (km or mi).
Pick the polarization (Horizontal or Vertical).
Read the total rain attenuation, the specific attenuation, and the effective path length.
rain-fade.algorithm: The tool uses the ITU-R P.838-3 rain model, which sets how strongly rain attenuates the signal based on frequency and polarization. It carries all 116 published rows of the recommendation's Table 5, covering 1 GHz to 1000 GHz, and fills in between them where needed. From those it gets the specific attenuation in dB per km at your rain rate. Heavy rain rarely falls evenly along a long path, so the model also shrinks the path to an effective length before multiplying the two together for the total attenuation.
rain-fade.example: At 23 GHz, 42 mm/hr, 5 km, horizontal polarization: specific attenuation works out to about 5.85 dB per km, the effective path length shrinks to about 3.94 km, and the total rain attenuation is about 23.07 dB.
fieldNotes: The model assumes the rain rate is uniform along the whole path; real storm cells are smaller, which the effective-length reduction partly accounts for.
Choose a rain rate that matches the availability you're targeting (for example, the rate exceeded only 0.01% of the time in your region). The tool doesn't supply regional rain statistics.
Below roughly 10 GHz, rain fade is usually negligible.
rain-fade.source: ITU-R P.838-3 Table 5 (all 116 rows) plus the ITU-R P.530 path reduction, computed from the values you enter, on your device.

ptp-link.name: PtP Link Check
ptp-link.purpose: A complete point-to-point backhaul link budget from end to end: transmit power through antenna gains, cable losses, free-space path loss, and rain fade to the received signal, then compared against sensitivity for a fade margin and a PASS, MARGINAL, or FAIL verdict.
ptp-link.whyHere: The all-in-one decision tool for a microwave or Wi-Fi backhaul link. It combines free-space path loss and rain fade with the link budget, so you get a single go or no-go answer.
howToUse: Enter frequency (GHz), distance (km or mi), transmit power (dBm), transmit gain (dBi), receive gain (dBi), and receiver sensitivity (dBm).
Optionally enter transmit loss, receive loss, rain rate, required margin, and pick the polarization.
Read the EIRP, free-space loss, rain fade, received signal, link margin, and the verdict.

inputs.name: RX sensitivity
inputs.range: Positive or negative; sensitivity is usually negative

ptp-link.algorithm: First it works out your EIRP (transmit power plus transmit gain minus transmit loss). Then free-space path loss, which grows with both distance and frequency. If you enter a rain rate, it adds rain fade using the same ITU-R rain model as the Rain Fade tool; with no rain rate, rain fade is zero. The received signal is EIRP minus free-space loss minus rain fade, plus receive gain, minus receive loss. The margin is that received signal minus the receiver's sensitivity. The verdict: margin at or above your required margin is PASS, between 0 and required is MARGINAL, and below 0 is FAIL.
ptp-link.example: At 5.8 GHz over 10 km, transmit 20 dBm, transmit and receive gain 23, sensitivity minus 78, no cable losses, no rain, required margin 10: EIRP is 43.0 dBm, free-space loss about 127.7 dB, received signal about minus 61.7 dBm, and the margin about 16.3 dB, so PASS. (Add 1 dB of transmit loss and 1 dB of receive loss and the margin drops to about 14.3 dB, still PASS.)
fieldNotes: This is the most complete link tool in the suite. Prefer it over the plain Link Budget when the link is outdoors and above roughly 10 GHz.
Free-space loss on its own underestimates real-world loss, and the rain-fade leg only covers rain, not fog, foliage, or multipath.
The MARGINAL band (the link closes but sits below your required margin) is the tool's own warning state; the pass or fail underneath it is purely whether the margin meets what you required.
ptp-link.source: Standard link-budget, free-space-loss, and ITU-R rain-fade math, computed from your inputs on your device.

throughput-calc.name: Throughput Calculator
throughput-calc.purpose: Works out the PHY rate and an estimated real-world throughput for a Wi-Fi connection from the standard, channel width, MCS index, number of spatial streams, and guard interval.
throughput-calc.whyHere: Turns a client's negotiated connection settings into expected data-rate numbers, and sets realistic expectations for what speed you should actually see.
howToUse: Pick the Wi-Fi standard (802.11n, ac, ax, or be).
Pick the channel width, MCS index, number of spatial streams, and guard interval (the choices narrow to what's valid for the standard you picked).
Read the modulation, PHY rate, and estimated real throughput in Mbps.

inputs.name: MCS index
inputs.range: Maximum per standard: 802.11n up to 7, 802.11ac up to 9, 802.11ax up to 11, 802.11be up to 13

inputs.name: Spatial streams
inputs.range: Up to 4 for 802.11n; up to 8 for 802.11ac, ax, and be

throughput-calc.algorithm: The PHY rate goes up with three things: how many data subcarriers fit in the channel width, how many bits each subcarrier carries (set by the MCS index), and how many spatial streams you're running. Wider guard intervals stretch each symbol out slightly, which lowers the rate a little. To estimate real throughput, the tool trims the PHY rate by a flat efficiency factor for overhead: about 70% for 802.11n, 72% for 802.11ac, 76% for 802.11ax, and 80% for 802.11be.
throughput-calc.example: 802.11ax, 80 MHz, MCS 11, 2 spatial streams, 0.8 microsecond guard interval: the PHY rate is about 1201.0 Mbps, and estimated real throughput is about 912.7 Mbps (1201.0 times 0.76).
fieldNotes: The efficiency factors (70% to 80%) are flat per-standard estimates of overhead, not measured for your environment. Real throughput depends on contention, retries, frame aggregation, and airtime sharing.
The PHY rate is the theoretical peak for a single, clean link. Treat the real-throughput number as an optimistic ceiling.
throughput-calc.source: Standard Wi-Fi PHY-rate math with per-standard efficiency estimates, computed from your selections on your device.

downtilt.name: Antenna Downtilt
downtilt.purpose: Tells you how far to tilt an antenna downward so the center of its beam lands on the ground where your users are.
downtilt.whyHere: When you mount a sector or directional antenna up high, you need a tilt angle that aims the main beam at the people below instead of over their heads.
howToUse: Enter the antenna's height above the ground and pick its unit (ft or m).
Enter how far out you want to cover and pick its unit (km, ft, or m).
Read the downtilt angle in degrees.
downtilt.algorithm: It is straight geometry. Picture a right triangle: the antenna height is the vertical side and the coverage distance is the horizontal side. The downtilt angle is the angle whose tangent equals height divided by distance. Everything is converted to meters first (feet times 0.3048, kilometers times 1000).
downtilt.example: An antenna 30 m up aimed at coverage 200 m away needs about 8.53 degrees of downtilt.
fieldNotes: This aims the center of the beam at your target distance. It does not account for how wide the beam is up and down, so use Downtilt Coverage to see the near and far edges.
It is pure geometry. It does not model the antenna's beam shape or any slope in the ground.
downtilt.source: Works out the tilt angle from simple right-triangle geometry using the height and distance you enter.

downtilt-coverage.name: Downtilt Coverage
downtilt-coverage.purpose: Shows where a tilted antenna's beam actually lands on the ground: the near edge, the far edge, and how deep the covered strip is, based on height, tilt, and how tall the beam is.
downtilt-coverage.whyHere: This is the companion to the Downtilt tool. It shows the footprint the beam really covers, so you can spot coverage gaps or signal that overshoots your area.
howToUse: Enter the antenna height above the ground and pick its unit (ft or m).
Enter the downtilt angle in degrees.
Enter the antenna's vertical beamwidth in degrees (how tall the beam is).
Read the near edge, far edge, and coverage depth.
downtilt-coverage.algorithm: The beam has a top edge and a bottom edge, set by the tilt angle plus or minus half the beamwidth. Each edge, together with the antenna height, forms a right triangle, so the distance to that edge is the height divided by the tangent of the edge's angle below horizontal. The near edge comes from the steeper (lower) beam edge and the far edge from the shallower (upper) one; the depth is the far edge minus the near edge. If the upper beam edge reaches or rises above the horizon, the far edge stretches out with no defined limit, so the tool reports that the beam is above the horizon instead of a distance. It leaves the results blank if the height is not positive or the beamwidth is outside 0 to 180 degrees.
downtilt-coverage.example: At 30 m height, 10 degrees of tilt, and an 8-degree beamwidth: the near edge falls about 120.3 m out, the far edge about 285.4 m out, for a covered strip about 165.1 m deep.
fieldNotes: When the tilt is shallow compared to the beamwidth (say 6 degrees of tilt with a 15-degree beam), the top of the beam clears the horizon and the far edge has no limit. The tool flags this rather than showing a nonsense negative distance.
The edges are the half-power (or stated) beamwidth points. Real coverage fades gradually beyond them rather than stopping cleanly.
downtilt-coverage.source: Uses right-triangle geometry on the top and bottom edges of the beam, worked out from the height, tilt, and beamwidth you enter.

earth-curvature.name: Earth Curvature
earth-curvature.purpose: Tells you the earth bulge, the height of the earth's curvature at the middle of a path, for a given path length and atmospheric K-factor.
earth-curvature.whyHere: On long microwave links the earth's curve, bent a little by the atmosphere, eats into your Fresnel clearance. This shows how much extra antenna height the curvature costs you.
howToUse: Enter the path length and pick its unit (km or mi).
Pick the K-factor.
Read the bulge in both meters and feet.

inputs.name: K-factor
inputs.range: 4/3 standard atmosphere (1.333, default), 1.0 geometric (no bending), 2/3 worst case (0.667), or 2.0 superrefraction

earth-curvature.algorithm: The bulge at the midpoint grows with the square of the path length and shrinks with a larger effective earth radius. In words: take the path length in kilometers, square it, multiply by 1000, then divide by 8 times the earth's mean radius (6371 km) times the K-factor. That gives the bulge in meters; multiply by 3.28084 to get feet. A smaller K-factor means a tighter effective curve and a bigger bulge.
earth-curvature.example: A 20 km path with K = 1.333 gives a bulge of about 5.89 m at the midpoint.
fieldNotes: K = 4/3 assumes a standard atmosphere. K = 2/3 is the conservative worst case for links that have to stay up, and it produces more bulge.
The bulge is the maximum, at the midpoint. Add it to the Fresnel clearance you need when sizing tower heights.
The K-factor shifts with weather and geography, so treat this as a planning number, not a guarantee.
earth-curvature.source: Calculates the midpoint bulge from the path length and K-factor using the standard earth-curvature relationship.

rf-attenuation.name: RF Attenuation
rf-attenuation.purpose: Estimates the total signal loss through building materials by adding up the loss from each layer for the Wi-Fi band you choose.
rf-attenuation.whyHere: For indoor coverage planning. Count the walls, floors, and obstructions between the access point and the client and get a quick total loss in dB to add to your link budget.
howToUse: Pick the band (2.4, 5, or 6 GHz).
Enter how many of each material the signal passes through.
Read the total attenuation in dB.

inputs.name: Per-material quantities
inputs.range: only counts greater than 0 add to the total

rf-attenuation.algorithm: Each material has a typical loss per layer that varies by band. Loss climbs with frequency, so a layer costs more at 6 GHz than at 2.4 GHz. For example, drywall runs about 3, 4, and 5 dB at 2.4, 5, and 6 GHz; poured concrete about 13, 16, and 19 dB; a metal door about 20, 26, and 30 dB; foil insulation about 25, 30, and 35 dB. The tool multiplies each material's per-layer loss for your chosen band by how many you entered, then adds them all up.
rf-attenuation.example: At 5 GHz, two sheets of drywall (4 dB each) plus one concrete block (13 dB) come to 21 dB total.
fieldNotes: These are typical figures, not measurements of your building. Real walls vary a lot with construction, moisture, and rebar.
Metal, foil and vapor barriers, and low-E glass are near-total blockers and will dominate the total.
Use this for a first-pass estimate, then confirm with a survey.
rf-attenuation.source: Adds up published typical per-material loss figures for the band you pick and the quantities you enter.

poe-budget.name: PoE Budget
poe-budget.purpose: Checks a PoE switch's total power budget against the power draw of everything plugged into it, and gives you an over, caution, or OK verdict.
poe-budget.whyHere: Before you hang a batch of access points (plus cameras and phones) off a switch, make sure the switch can power them all without blowing past its PoE budget.
howToUse: Enter the switch's PoE budget in watts.
For up to six device rows, enter the watts per device and how many (quantity defaults to 1).
Read the total draw, remaining budget, percent used, and the verdict.

inputs.name: Switch PoE budget
inputs.range: must be greater than 0, or you get no result

inputs.name: Device rows (up to 6)
inputs.unit: watts per device and a quantity
inputs.range: leave a field blank and it counts as 0; quantity defaults to 1

poe-budget.algorithm: For each row it multiplies the watts per device by the quantity, then adds the rows together for the total draw. The remaining budget is the switch budget minus that total, and the percent used is the total over the budget (shown capped at 100 percent). The verdict follows a simple order: if you are over budget it says OVER; otherwise if you are past 80 percent it says CAUTION; otherwise OK.
poe-budget.example: A 370 W budget with six access points at 25.5 W each comes to 153 W drawn, 217 W remaining, 41 percent used, so OK.
fieldNotes: Use each device's worst-case (maximum) draw, not idle, and mind the PoE class.

Budget in PSE watts, not PD watts. PSE is what the switch port must supply; PD is what survives the cable and reaches the device. Twelve Class-4 APs are 306 W of PD but 360 W of PSE: against a 370 W switch that is the difference between comfortable and nearly exhausted. The standards card on this screen shows both columns.
802.3bt Type 4 supplies 90 W (IEEE 802.3-2022, Table 145-11, Class 8 P_Class). The 99.9 W figure sometimes quoted is the PSE power rating band (P_Type, Table 145-16), which is a different quantity.
The percent shown stops at 100 for display, but the OVER verdict triggers on the real negative remaining, so an over-budget case is never hidden.
This checks the switch's total budget only. It does not model per-port limits or power lost over long cable runs.
poe-budget.source: Adds up the draw of every device row and compares it against the switch budget you enter.

metric-conversion.name: Metric Conversion
metric-conversion.purpose: Converts a length between meters, kilometers, miles, feet, centimeters, inches, and nautical miles.
metric-conversion.whyHere: Field work mixes metric and imperial constantly: datasheets in meters, tape measures in feet, link distances in miles. A quick converter saves you from arithmetic slips.
howToUse: Enter a value and pick the unit it is in.
Read it converted into every other unit at once.

inputs.name: Value and source unit
inputs.unit: one of m, km, mi, ft, cm, in, nmi

metric-conversion.algorithm: It converts by way of meters. Each unit has an exact meters-per-unit value: 1 meter, 1000 per kilometer, 1609.344 per mile, 0.3048 per foot, 0.01 per centimeter, 0.0254 per inch, and 1852 per nautical mile. To convert, it turns your value into meters, then divides into the unit you want. Each unit is shown to a sensible number of decimals for its scale.
metric-conversion.example: 1 mile is 1609.344 m, which is 1.609344 km, 5280 ft, and 0.868976 nautical miles.
fieldNotes: It uses the international mile (1609.344 m) and international foot (0.3048 m), not the US survey foot.
The math is exact; the rounding you see is for display only.
metric-conversion.source: Converts every length through meters using exact per-unit conversion factors.

dist-bearing.name: Distance and Bearing
dist-bearing.purpose: Gives you the straight-line-over-the-globe distance between two points, plus the compass bearing to aim from each end to the other.
dist-bearing.whyHere: For aiming point-to-point antennas. You need the distance (for free-space path loss) and the compass bearing (to point the dish) between two sites.
howToUse: Enter the latitude and longitude for point 1 and point 2 in decimal degrees.
Read the distance in km, the forward bearing, and the reverse bearing.
dist-bearing.algorithm: It treats the earth as a sphere with a mean radius of 6371 km and finds the great-circle distance, the shortest path across the curved surface, using the standard haversine method. The forward bearing is the compass heading you would start out on to travel that path from point 1 to point 2; the reverse bearing is simply that heading plus 180 degrees, wrapped to a 0 to 360 range.
dist-bearing.example: San Francisco to Los Angeles works out to about 559.12 km, a forward bearing of 136.5 degrees and a reverse bearing of 316.5 degrees.
fieldNotes: It uses a spherical earth (6371 km mean radius), which is accurate to a fraction of a percent for terrestrial links but not for survey-grade geodesy.
The forward bearing is the starting heading of the great-circle path, and it shifts along the route on long paths.
The reverse bearing is just the forward plus 180 degrees, which is only exact on a sphere.
dist-bearing.source: Uses the haversine great-circle method on a spherical earth to find distance and bearings from the two coordinates.

midpoint.name: Midpoint
midpoint.purpose: Finds the true halfway point along the shortest path over the globe between two coordinates.
midpoint.whyHere: For siting a relay or repeater partway along a long link, or finding the center point of a coverage area between two known locations.
howToUse: Enter the latitude and longitude for point 1 and point 2.
Read the midpoint latitude and longitude.
midpoint.algorithm: It finds the point halfway along the great circle, the shortest curved path across the globe, between your two coordinates, then reports its latitude and longitude wrapped to a normal range. This is a proper spherical calculation, not just averaging the two sets of numbers.
midpoint.example: Halfway between (40, 0) and (40, 90) the point sits well north of the two latitudes, at about (49.88, 45.00), because the shortest path bows toward the pole.
fieldNotes: This is the true great-circle midpoint, not the average of the coordinates. On east-west paths it lands noticeably closer to the pole than a simple average would suggest.
It uses a spherical earth, with the same accuracy caveats as Distance and Bearing.
midpoint.source: Finds the halfway point along the great-circle path between the two coordinates on a spherical earth.

final-point.name: Final Point
final-point.purpose: Given a starting point, a starting bearing, and a distance, it works out the coordinates where you end up.
final-point.whyHere: For projecting where a link points. Given a site, a heading, and a distance, find the coordinates of the far end, for example to check a candidate tower location.
howToUse: Enter the starting latitude and longitude.
Enter the starting bearing in degrees.
Enter the distance and pick its unit (km, mi, or m).
Read the destination latitude and longitude.

inputs.name: Distance
inputs.range: miles and meters are converted to kilometers

final-point.algorithm: Starting from your point, it travels the distance you gave along the great circle in the direction of your starting bearing, treating the earth as a sphere with a 6371 km radius, and reports where that path ends. The longitude is wrapped to a normal range.
final-point.example: From (40, -105) heading due east (90 degrees) for 100 km, you land at about (39.99, -103.83). The latitude dips a hair because a constant starting bearing follows a great circle, not a line of latitude.
fieldNotes: This is a great-circle path on a sphere. The bearing is the starting heading, and the path curves over long distances.
It carries the same spherical-earth accuracy caveats as the other geographic tools.
final-point.source: Projects the endpoint along a great-circle path on a spherical earth from your start point, bearing, and distance.

lat-long.name: Lat / Long Conversion
lat-long.purpose: Converts a coordinate between the three common notations: decimal degrees (DD), degrees and decimal minutes (DDM), and degrees, minutes, and seconds (DMS).
lat-long.whyHere: Different maps and tools write coordinates in different notations. This normalizes between them when you enter a site location.
howToUse: Enter a latitude value (and/or longitude) in decimal degrees.
Read it in DD, DDM, and DMS forms with the correct hemisphere letter.

inputs.name: Latitude
inputs.range: must be within plus or minus 90

inputs.name: Longitude
inputs.range: must be within plus or minus 180

lat-long.algorithm: The sign of your number sets the hemisphere: a positive latitude is North and negative is South; a positive longitude is East and negative is West. To build the other notations, the whole degrees are the part before the decimal, the minutes come from the leftover fraction times 60, and the seconds come from what is left times 60 again. Degrees and decimal minutes just keeps degrees and expresses the rest as minutes with a decimal. Anything out of range or not a real number leaves the output blank.
lat-long.example: 40.7128 degrees latitude shows as DD 40.712800; DMS 40 degrees 42 minutes 46.08 seconds N; and DDM 40 degrees 42.7680 minutes N.
fieldNotes: It checks the ranges (plus or minus 90 for latitude, plus or minus 180 for longitude) and blanks the output if you go outside them.
The sign drives the hemisphere letter, so enter west longitudes and south latitudes as negative numbers.
There is no datum or projection handling. These are plain WGS84-style decimal degrees in, formatted out.
lat-long.source: Splits your decimal-degree coordinate into degrees, minutes, and seconds and formats each notation with the right hemisphere letter.

hex-ascii.name: Hex / ASCII
hex-ascii.purpose: Convert a whole number between decimal, hexadecimal, and binary in real time, and look up any printable ASCII character (codes 32 to 126) in one place.
hex-ascii.whyHere: Handy when you're reading packet captures, MAC and OUI bytes, or hex values in a config and want to flip a number between bases or check an ASCII character without leaving the app.
howToUse: Type a value into any of the three fields (decimal, hexadecimal, or binary) and the other two update instantly.
Scroll the ASCII table to find a printable character and its decimal, hex, and binary codes.

inputs.name: Hexadecimal
inputs.unit: 0-9, a-f, A-F (an optional 0x prefix is ignored)

inputs.name: Binary
inputs.range: Whole numbers of any size, so even very long strings never overflow; leaving a field blank or entering something invalid clears the other two.
