hex-ascii.algorithm: It converts a single whole number from whatever base you typed into the other two bases. In the reference table, hex is shown as two uppercase digits and binary as eight bits with leading zeros, and every character is generated from its code so nothing is mistyped.
hex-ascii.example: Enter decimal 65 and you get hex 41 and binary 1000001. In the table, decimal 65 is the letter A, hex 41, binary 01000001.
fieldNotes: Whole numbers only: no negatives, no fractions.
The table covers printable ASCII (codes 32 to 126) only; control characters and extended or Unicode characters are not included.
This converts one number between bases; it does not turn a line of text into its byte sequence.
hex-ascii.source: The result is calculated on your device using standard base conversion.

ipv6-subnet.name: IP Subnetting (IPv6)
ipv6-subnet.purpose: Enter an IPv6 address and prefix length and get back the expanded and compressed forms, the network address, the first and last address in the prefix, the number of hosts, and the address type.
ipv6-subnet.whyHere: IPv6 subnetting and address-type lookups during design and troubleshooting, where doing 128-bit math by hand invites mistakes.
howToUse: Enter an IPv6 address (default 2001:db8::1).
Enter the prefix length (default 32).
Read the expanded form, compressed form, network and prefix, first and last address, host count, and address type.

inputs.name: IPv6 address
inputs.range: only one :: shortcut allowed; an invalid format shows an inline error

inputs.name: Prefix
inputs.range: 0 to 128 (anything outside that shows an error)

ipv6-subnet.algorithm: It expands the address to its full form, works out which bits the prefix fixes, and from that gives you the network address plus the first and last address in the block. It also shows the shortest valid compressed form by collapsing the longest run of zero groups. Host count is 2 raised to the number of host bits (128 minus the prefix); anything above 2 to the 63rd is reported as "More than 2^63" rather than a full number. The address type is identified by matching the address against the well-known IPv6 ranges (unspecified, loopback, link-local, unique-local, multicast, 6to4, IPv4-mapped, documentation, or global unicast).
ipv6-subnet.example: 2001:db8::1 with a /64 gives expanded form 2001:0db8:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0001, compressed form 2001:db8::1, network 2001:db8::/64, 64 host bits (so the host count shows "More than 2^63"), and type Documentation (2001:db8::/32).
fieldNotes: Host counts above 2 to the 63rd are shown as "More than 2^63" rather than a full number.
The "first address" is the network address itself. IPv6 has no broadcast address and does not reserve the all-zeros host the way IPv4 does.
Address-type detection covers the common well-known ranges, not every reserved block.
This tool lives under Networking Tools in the current menu.
ipv6-subnet.source: The result is calculated on your device.

non-wifi-channels.name: Non-Wi-Fi Wireless Channels
non-wifi-channels.purpose: Look up the channel and frequency plans of the common non-Wi-Fi radios that share or sit next to the bands a Wi-Fi pro works in: LoRaWAN, IEEE 802.15.4, Bluetooth Classic, Bluetooth LE, and Zigbee.
non-wifi-channels.whyHere: For when you're chasing interference or co-existence in the 2.4 GHz band, or sizing a sub-GHz IoT deployment, and need to know where these radios actually sit.
howToUse: Each technology has its own section.
LoRaWAN plans marked with a "verify" chip are version-dependent or thinly sourced, so confirm them before you rely on them.

Bluetooth LE rows are listed by actual frequency (not by index) so you can see how the 3 advertising channels are spaced out; an "Advertising" chip marks channels 37, 38, and 39 (2402, 2426, and 2480 MHz).
Zigbee's "common picks" (11, 15, 20, 25, 26) are a convention, not a requirement.
fieldNotes: What it shows: one card per technology. LoRaWAN: regional plan (EU868, US915, AU915, AS923, IN865, KR920, CN470, CN779, RU864), frequency range in MHz, and a channel-plan description. IEEE 802.15.4: band (868 MHz / 915 MHz / 2.4 GHz), channel-number range, spacing, center summary, and region. Bluetooth Classic (BR/EDR): 79 channels, 1 MHz spacing, 2402 to 2480 MHz, about 1600 hops per second, global. Bluetooth LE: all 40 channels in frequency order with index, frequency in MHz, and kind (Advertising or Data). Zigbee: 2.4 GHz channels 11 to 26, sub-GHz bands, and common 2.4 GHz channel picks.
Bluetooth, Bluetooth LE, and 802.15.4 use globally fixed channel grids. LoRaWAN frequency plans are set entirely by region; there is no single global LoRaWAN channel map.

Plans marked "verify" (CN470 is version-dependent, CN779 is deprecated or limited, RU864 is thinly sourced) should be confirmed against the LoRa Alliance RP002 regional parameters and your local regulator.
The Bluetooth LE table lists each channel's true frequency directly rather than using the simple "2402 + 2 x index" formula, which is a common shortcut that produces the wrong frequencies.
The 868 and 915 MHz bands of 802.15.4 are region-restricted; channels 11 to 26 in 2.4 GHz are global.

Data source: cross-checked against the LoRa Alliance RP002, IEEE 802.15.4, the Bluetooth SIG Core Spec, and the CSA/Zigbee spec. The 802.15.4 center frequencies were verified against the standard's formula (channel 0 = 868.3 MHz; channels 1 to 10 = 906 + 2 x (k-1); channels 11 to 26 = 2405 + 5 x (k-11)).
non-wifi-channels.source: Values are cross-checked against the published standards.

channel-map.name: Channel Map
channel-map.purpose: A visual channel-bonding map showing, per band, how 20/40/80/160/320 MHz channels bond together, which primary (center) channel labels each bonded block, and which blocks require DFS.
channel-map.whyHere: For planning channel widths and bonding, so you can see at a glance which 20 MHz primaries fold into a given 80 or 160 MHz channel, and which bonds pull in a DFS sub-channel.
howToUse: Scroll the 5 and 6 GHz maps sideways. Each block's number is its primary (center) channel.

Color and chip legend: No DFS (neutral, just a fact not a warning), DFS (amber, radar detection required), Mixed / DFS (red, a 160 MHz bond that spans both DFS and non-DFS sub-bands, where any DFS sub-channel subjects the whole bond to DFS), and PSC (lime, the 6 GHz preferred scanning channels).
The 6 GHz 320 MHz row shows channel 31 as the primary block and channel 63 as a dashed alternative; they overlap, so only one is used at a time.
fieldNotes: What it shows: a three-way band toggle. 2.4 GHz: the 11 US channels as 20 MHz blocks, with 1, 6, and 11 emphasized (non-overlapping) and the rest faint. 5 GHz: rows for 20/40/80/160 MHz bonded widths, each block labeled with its primary/center channel and tinted by DFS class. 6 GHz: rows for 20/40/80/160/320 MHz across the full US band (channels 1 to 233, UNII-5 through UNII-8), with PSC channels marked.
US by default. The map covers the full US 6 GHz band, channels 1 to 233 (UNII-5 through UNII-8). 6 GHz needs no DFS, and no AFC for low-power indoor (LPI) use.

Data source: US (FCC). 5 GHz DFS: No DFS = UNII-1 (36 to 48), UNII-3 (149 to 165), and UNII-4 (169 to 177); DFS = UNII-2A and 2C. UNII-4 (169/173/177) is included per Keith's RF Channel Allocations chart.
channel-map.source: Values come from the US (FCC) channel plan.

spectrum.name: Spectrum Reference
spectrum.purpose: A per-band fact sheet for the three Wi-Fi bands: total usable spectrum, supported standards, channel counts, non-overlapping counts, channel widths, DFS/radar requirements, common co-existing interferers, and key deployment notes.
spectrum.whyHere: The one-screen "tell me everything about this band" reference: what lives in it, what interferes with it, and what the power and DFS rules are.
howToUse: Pick a band (2.4, 5, or 6 GHz) and read the eight facts top to bottom.
The colored range badge is just a visual pairing with the band label (2.4 GHz amber for congested, 5 GHz blue, 6 GHz green).
The "Co-existence" row names the band's common interferers or managed incumbents.
fieldNotes: What it shows: each band gets a range badge plus eight facts: Total spectrum, Standards, Channels (US), Non-overlapping, Channel widths, DFS / Radar, Co-existence, and Key notes.
US by default; every band carries the footnote "US (FCC) regulatory domain. Verify local rules before deployment."

Includes useful specifics: UNII-1 is indoor-only in some regions; UNII-2A/2C DFS means a 60-second channel-availability delay after radar is detected; 6 GHz has three US power modes, namely Standard Power (up to 36 dBm EIRP, AFC outdoors), LPI (up to 30 dBm, no AFC), and VLP (up to 14 dBm EIRP, no AFC, mobile); and WPA3 is mandatory on 6 GHz.
Data source: US (FCC) regulatory domain. Values: 2.4 GHz = 83.5 MHz (US); 5 GHz = about 580 MHz usable (UNII-1/2A/2C/3); 6 GHz = 1200 MHz (5925 to 7125 MHz).
spectrum.source: Values come from the US (FCC) regulatory domain.

capacity-planner.name: Capacity Planner
capacity-planner.purpose: An informational statement, not a calculator: it explains why Wi-Fi capacity planning cannot honestly be reduced to a few input boxes and a confident AP count.
capacity-planner.whyHere: The tool that used to live here returned an AP-count from a single formula. That math was removed because one formula cannot honor the variables that actually drive capacity. This screen replaces the false precision with an honest explanation and points you to a real plan.
howToUse: There is nothing to enter. Read the statement: capacity planning is a design problem, not a calculation.
For a plan you can trust, bring in a Wi-Fi professional experienced in your kind of environment; a real plan comes from measuring and modeling your specific space and usage.
fieldNotes: This is a read-only disclaimer screen: no inputs, no math, no copyable result. The tile, the /tools/capacity-planner route, and the "Capacity Planner" title are kept so the tile still resolves where users expect.

Real Wi-Fi capacity depends on the applications in use and their airtime cost, client device capabilities and roaming, AP and antenna selection, channel reuse and co-channel interference, band steering, airtime fairness, QoS, and how people move through the space. Change any one and the answer changes.
A confidently wrong number is worse than no number, which is why the calculator was removed rather than kept with a caveat.
capacity-planner.source: Verbatim from Keith's approved disclaimer copy; no external data source (the screen performs no calculation).

mcs-index.name: MCS Index
mcs-index.purpose: Look up the modulation, coding rate, and PHY data rate for any 802.11 MCS index across channel widths, for 802.11n (HT), 802.11ac (VHT), 802.11ax (HE), and 802.11be (EHT / Wi-Fi 7), scaled by the number of spatial streams.
mcs-index.whyHere: For when you see an MCS index in a capture or client stats and want to know the modulation and coding behind it and the rate it should deliver at a given width and stream count.
howToUse: Choose the standard (n, ac, ax, or be) and the stream count; the rates update. 802.11n offers 1 to 4 streams (MCS 0-31 is eight indices across four streams - the standard defines no more); ac, ax, and be offer 1 to 8.
MCS 0 (BPSK 1/2) is the most robust and slowest; higher MCS indices use denser modulation (up to 1024-QAM for ax and 4096-QAM for be) for higher rates but need a better signal.
Cells shown as "N/A" are genuinely invalid combinations, not zero and not made up. Some are invalid only at certain stream counts.
Above 4 spatial streams no rate is shown at all. That is not an "N/A" - it is a gap in the published source. The screen says so explicitly rather than computing a number it cannot source.
fieldNotes: What it shows: two selectors, the 802.11 standard (n, ac, ax, or be) and the number of spatial streams (1 to 8). Columns per standard: 802.11n = 20 LGI, 20 SGI, 40 LGI, 40 SGI; 802.11ac = 20/40/80/160 SGI; 802.11ax = 20/40/80/160 MHz; 802.11be = 20/40/80/160/320 MHz (both at an 800 ns guard interval).

802.11ac MCS 9 at 20 MHz is valid ONLY at 3 spatial streams - it is N/A at 1, 2 and 4. MCS 9 at 40 MHz is valid at every stream count. Two further stream-dependent exclusions: MCS 6 at 80 MHz and MCS 9 at 160 MHz are both invalid at 3 streams.

The source tables publish 1 to 4 spatial streams. Above 4 the app shows no rate, because the per-stream exclusions up there are unpublished and are not derivable from the ones below - a rule that reproduces MCS 9 at 20 MHz exactly still gets MCS 6 at 80 MHz and MCS 9 at 160 MHz wrong. A known gap beats an invented number.

Guard-interval definitions: 802.11n LGI = 800 ns and SGI = 400 ns; 802.11ac uses SGI. 802.11ax and 802.11be define 0.8, 1.6, and 3.2 microsecond guard intervals only - there is no 400 ns short GI above 802.11ac.
The notes card points out that real throughput is typically 50 to 65% of the PHY rate. These rates are PHY-layer maximums, not delivered throughput.

Rates are rounded from the exact PHY value at the chosen stream count, not by multiplying a rounded single-stream figure - so 802.11ac MCS 9 at 160 MHz with 2 streams reads 1733.3, matching the source, not 1733.4.
Data source: Keith Parsons' MCS Index Chart and mcsindex.net (by @VergesFrancois, SemFio Networks), cross-checked against the IEEE 802.11n/ac/ax/be PHY rate tables.
mcs-index.source: Values come from Keith Parsons' MCS Index Chart and mcsindex.net, cross-checked against the IEEE 802.11n/ac/ax/be PHY rate tables.

modulation.name: Modulation
modulation.purpose: Explains what a modulation constellation is on the I/Q plane and why each step up in order (BPSK to QPSK to 16/64/256/1024-QAM) carries more bits per symbol but needs a cleaner link (lower EVM, higher SNR).
modulation.whyHere: The visual companion to the MCS Index table. Reach for it when you want to understand why a higher MCS needs a better signal, or to explain constellations, bits per symbol, and EVM to someone, rather than to look up a spec rate.
howToUse: Read the six constellation diagrams in order; each step doubles the points and adds one bit per symbol while the points pack closer together.
Read the Error Vector Magnitude (EVM) explainer to see what the receiver has to overcome to keep each symbol on the correct side of its decision boundary.
Read the summary card for the order, bits per symbol, and SNR/EVM relationship at a glance.
Tap any diagram to open a full-screen pinch-to-zoom view for the detail-dense planes.
fieldNotes: The SNR and EVM figures on the cards are representative, order-of-magnitude demands, not exact 802.11 MCS thresholds. The relationship, not the precise number, is the point. Use the MCS Index tool for the spec rate and modulation table.

EVM (Error Vector Magnitude) is how far the received symbol lands from the ideal point, expressed as a percentage (or in dB) of the reference amplitude. As the order rises, the decision boundaries shrink, so higher-order QAM needs a lower EVM and a higher SNR.

The diagrams are shown on an always-dark card in both light and dark app themes so they never look inverted. They are marked decorative for screen readers, and the summary card below them carries the same order, bits, SNR and EVM figures as real on-screen text - so nothing in the diagrams is available only to sighted users.
The Copy action exports the same summary table as the screen shows, plus the note that the numbers are representative.

signal-thresholds.name: Signal Thresholds
signal-thresholds.purpose: RSSI and SNR design targets: a recommended quality scale for RSSI, minimum RSSI and SNR by application, and the SNR needed to reach each typical MCS index.
signal-thresholds.whyHere: For validating a survey or troubleshooting a complaint: "is -68 dBm good enough for VoIP?" or "what SNR do I need to hold MCS 7?"
howToUse: RSSI bands carry a colored verdict dot plus the quality word (green = good, amber = marginal/Fair, red = bad/Poor); the word always carries the meaning, never the color alone.
In the application table, read across to the minimum RSSI and minimum SNR your use case needs.
The SNR-to-MCS table tells you the SNR floor to sustain a given rate.
fieldNotes: What it shows: three blocks. RSSI quality scale: Excellent (> -60 dBm) / Good (-60 to -67) / Fair (-67 to -72) / Poor (-73 or weaker). Minimum signal by application: VoIP/real-time, HD video, general browsing, email/basic data, IoT/low-rate, and location/RTLS, each with a minimum RSSI, minimum SNR, and a note. SNR to MCS (80 MHz, 1 spatial stream): the SNR floor for each MCS index (MCS 0 at 5 dB up to MCS 11 at 35 dB) with an indicative rate.
These are recommended design targets, not an industry standard. IEEE 802.11 does not define "good" or "poor" signal levels, and appropriate thresholds vary by site and by customer requirement.
Values vary by client hardware, environment, and AP vendor; treat them as field-planning guidelines, not guarantees.
The SNR-to-MCS rates are indicative (80 MHz, single stream); the MCS 10 and 11 rates are noted as 802.11ax.
signal-thresholds.source: Recommended design targets. Not an industry standard: IEEE 802.11 does not define signal-quality levels.

80211-standards.name: 802.11 Standards
80211-standards.purpose: A PHY-layer comparison of every major 802.11 amendment, from the original 802.11 (1997) through Wi-Fi 7, with year, bands, maximum PHY rate, MIMO, channel widths, and modulation.
80211-standards.whyHere: For settling "which generation does what": the bands it reaches, its maximum rate, its MIMO ceiling, and its modulation.
howToUse: Scan by generation badge (Wi-Fi 4 through Wi-Fi 7).
The band filter answers "which generations reach 6 GHz" (Wi-Fi 6E and Wi-Fi 7).
Amendments before Wi-Fi 4 (802.11, 802.11b, 802.11a, 802.11g) show a dash for generation, since official Wi-Fi Alliance naming begins at Wi-Fi 4.
fieldNotes: What it shows: one card per amendment with the IEEE designation, a Wi-Fi generation badge, year, and rows for Bands (GHz), Max PHY rate, MIMO, Channel width (MHz), and Modulation. An optional band filter (All / 2.4 / 5 / 6 GHz) narrows the list.

Two footnotes: (1) "Official Wi-Fi Alliance generation naming begins at Wi-Fi 4 (802.11n); earlier amendments are shown by their 802.11 names only"; (2) "Wi-Fi 7 certification began 2024; IEEE 802.11be was published 2025."
The max PHY rate is the theoretical aggregate ceiling; real-world throughput is typically 50 to 60% of it.

Data source: the IEEE 802.11 amendments. Key rows: 802.11ac = Wi-Fi 5 (2014, 6.9 Gbps); 802.11ax = Wi-Fi 6 (2019) and Wi-Fi 6E (2020, adds 6 GHz); 802.11be = Wi-Fi 7 (2024, 23.1 Gbps with MLO, 4K-QAM, up to 320 MHz).
80211-standards.source: Values come from the IEEE 802.11 amendments.

roaming.name: Roaming Parameters
roaming.purpose: The 802.11k/r/v fast-roaming protocols (what each does and what it needs) plus RSSI, SNR, and latency design thresholds for enterprise roaming.
roaming.whyHere: For designing or troubleshooting roaming for VoIP and unified comms, confirming the role of each protocol and the signal-overlap targets that make handoffs work.
howToUse: Each protocol heading shows the designation (lime) and the full name.

In the thresholds table, the scenario word is color-tinted with a dot: green = good (the two design targets), amber = marginal (the overlap zone), red = bad (the sticky-client trigger and the unusable level).
Design rules carry the actionable target (for example, "at least 2 APs at -67 dBm everywhere" or "a second AP at -72 dBm or better across the overlap zone").
fieldNotes: What it shows: a Protocols block covering 802.11r (Fast BSS Transition), 802.11k (Neighbor Report), and 802.11v (BSS Transition Management), each with what it does, its deployment requirements, and a field note. A Thresholds block covers five scenarios (VoIP/UC design target, standard data design target, roaming overlap zone, sticky-client trigger, and unusable), each with a minimum RSSI, minimum SNR, roam latency, design rule, and a status verdict.
The intro notes that targets vary by client hardware and AP vendor: these are design guidelines, not guarantees.

Field notes flag real client behavior: some older clients have 802.11r compatibility issues; Android and iOS generally honor 802.11v, but some Windows drivers ignore BSS Transition Management entirely.
Data source: IEEE 802.11k/r/v. This reference covers exactly these three protocols; OKC is not included.
roaming.source: Values come from the IEEE 802.11k/r/v standards.

frame-exchange.name: Association Sequence
frame-exchange.purpose: Step-by-step maps of how a device joins Wi-Fi and roams between access points, showing the order and direction of every message that passes between the device, the access point, the RADIUS server, and the DHCP server.
frame-exchange.whyHere: When you are reading a capture or explaining how a join happens, this confirms which message should come next and who sends it to whom.
howToUse: Pick a scenario (Open / WPA2-PSK, WPA3-SAE, OWE / Enhanced Open, Passpoint / Hotspot 2.0, WPA2-Enterprise (802.1X/EAP), 802.11r Roam) and read the phases from top to bottom.
Each step is tagged with a plain type: MGMT (management), EAP (EAP / EAPOL key), WIRED (RADIUS/DHCP traveling over the wired network), or DHCP.
The legend at the bottom spells out each tag. The tag is shown as text, so you never have to rely on color to read it.
fieldNotes: What it shows: a picker with six scenarios. Each one is split into named phases (for example Probe & Auth, Association, 4-Way Handshake, EAP Authentication, DHCP), and each step lists a step number, a direction, the message name, a type tag, and a short note.

OWE / Enhanced Open: Open System authentication happens first, then the association exchange carries each side's public key inside a Diffie-Hellman element (the request carries the device's key, the response carries the access point's key). A shared secret is derived from those keys, and a normal 4-Way Handshake follows. No passphrase is used.

Passpoint / Hotspot 2.0: the distinctive part is a query the device makes before it joins, asking which networks an access point can authenticate for. Once that is answered, an ordinary 802.1X/EAP exchange plus 4-Way Handshake runs.

eduroam and OpenRoaming are not separate diagrams. They are behind-the-scenes agreements between networks that run over an exchange already shown here. eduroam is 802.1X/EAP with your home network authenticating you by realm (noted on the WPA2-Enterprise scenario); OpenRoaming is a Passpoint plus EAP arrangement triggered by a matching network identifier (noted on the Passpoint scenario). The 802.11r scenario also notes the roam variant that hands off through the wired network alongside the over-the-air version.

These are representative sequences, not an exhaustive list. Optional and passive-scan paths, and the back-and-forth inside EAP methods, are summarized (for example the WPA3 DHCP phase is shown as one combined line, identical to the WPA2 flow).

Built from IEEE 802.11 join and handshake behavior, cross-checked against published research (RFC 8110 for OWE, the 802.11u query mechanism for Passpoint). It reflects the real exchanges: the WPA3 commit and confirm steps, the way OWE rides its keys inside association, the pre-join query for Passpoint, 802.1X/EAP carried over RADIUS, and both the over-the-air and through-the-wired-network forms of an 802.11r fast roam. General reference, not region-specific.
frame-exchange.source: The sequences reproduce the standard 802.11 join and handshake behavior; nothing is invented.

packet-decode.name: Packet Decode
packet-decode.purpose: Byte-level anatomy of the Layer 3 and 4 headers a capture shows beneath 802.11: the field, its bit offset and width, and what it means, for IPv4, IPv6, TCP, UDP, and ICMP. Plus the TCP control bits, the TCP connection states with the three-way handshake and teardown, a common IP-protocol-numbers table, and the ICMP and ICMPv6 type and code tables.
packet-decode.whyHere: When you are reading a packet in Wireshark and need to confirm which field sits at which offset, what a TCP flag combination means, or what an ICMP type and code is telling you, without leaving the app or going online.
howToUse: Scroll to the header you are decoding. Each header table lists the field, its bit offset (counted from bit 0), its bit length, and a plain-language meaning.
The IPv4 Type-of-Service octet is shown the modern way: DSCP (6 bits) plus ECN (2 bits). The legacy precedence reading is noted beneath.
For TCP, the flags table lists the eight control bits (CWR, ECE, URG, ACK, PSH, RST, SYN, FIN); the reserved bit that some tools still label NS is shown separately with its history.
The IPv4 Protocol and IPv6 Next Header fields point at the IP-protocol-numbers table. For TCP and UDP port numbers, use the Well-Known Ports reference instead.
Use the copy button in the toolbar to copy every table as tab-separated text, one section per header.
fieldNotes: Bit offsets count from bit 0 of each header, where bit 0 is the first (most-significant) bit. Bit lengths are exact, never approximate. Variable-length fields (IPv4/TCP Options, padding, payload) are labeled variable.

TCP control bits use RFC 9293's canonical layout: a 4-bit reserved field plus 8 control bits. The bit at position 103 that Wireshark historically labels NS is Reserved in RFC 9293. It was NS (Nonce Sum, RFC 3540), which RFC 8311 made Historic, and RFC 9768 reassigns it as AE (Accurate ECN). It is shown for decoder completeness, flagged historic, not as a current flag.
ICMP type 4 (Source Quench) is kept in the table but marked deprecated per RFC 6633.

Every field offset, width, flag, state, type, and code is transcribed from the RFCs and the IANA registries and cited per table: IPv4 (RFC 791, with DSCP/ECN per RFC 2474 and RFC 3168), IPv6 (RFC 8200), TCP (RFC 9293), UDP (RFC 768), ICMP (RFC 792 plus the IANA ICMP Parameters registry), and ICMPv6 (RFC 4443, with NDP per RFC 4861).
packet-decode.source: Every value is transcribed from the primary RFC or IANA registry, cited per table; nothing is invented or approximated.

reason-codes.name: 802.11 Reason Codes
reason-codes.purpose: The 802.11 reason codes (why a device was kicked off) and status codes (why a join succeeded or failed) that show up in captures, with a search box to find one fast.
reason-codes.whyHere: When a capture shows a deauth with reason code 15, or a join response with status 17, and you need the plain-language meaning right away.
howToUse: Type a number or a keyword (for example "15" or "handshake") to filter. A "no match" card appears if nothing fits.
Reason codes show up in Deauthentication and Disassociation frames; status codes show up in Authentication, Association, and Reassociation Response frames.
In the status group, code 0 means success and is shown in green.
fieldNotes: What it shows: reason codes grouped by theme: Common (1 to 9), Capability/Channel mismatch (10 to 11), Security frame/element errors (13 to 14, 17 to 22, 24), Security handshake failures (15, 16, 23), QoS/load management (34 to 39), and Fast Roaming/802.11r (45 to 48). Plus a separate Association Status Codes group (the most common subset, 0 to 104), where code 0 ("Successful") is highlighted in green.
The status-code list is the most common subset, not the full table.
The codes and their meanings come straight from the standard; nothing is invented.
Source and standard: IEEE 802.11-2020, section 9.4.1.7 (reason codes) and section 9.4.1.9 (status codes), cited in the footnote.
reason-codes.source: The codes and meanings are reproduced directly from the IEEE 802.11 standard.

http-status-codes.name: HTTP Status Codes
http-status-codes.purpose: The full set of HTTP response codes, grouped by class, each with a plain-English meaning. A fast offline lookup when a captive portal, web service, proxy, or API returns a code and you need to know what it means.
http-status-codes.whyHere: When a check comes back 403 or 503, or a captive-portal probe returns 511, and you want the meaning without leaving the app or going online.
howToUse: Type a number or keyword (for example "404" or "redirect") to filter. A "no match" card appears if nothing fits.
The filter looks at the code number, the short reason phrase, and the plain-English meaning, so typing "timeout" finds 408 and 504.
Use the copy button in the toolbar to copy the whole reference as tab-separated text, one section per class.
fieldNotes: What it shows: codes grouped by class: 1xx Informational, 2xx Success, 3xx Redirection, 4xx Client Error, 5xx Server Error. Each row is the code number, its reason phrase, and a short meaning.
511 (Network Authentication Required) is the tell-tale sign of a captive portal: the network blocks access until the device signs in.
418 is listed in the official registry as "(Unused)". It is widely known as the "I am a teapot" joke code from RFC 2324; the tool labels it honestly and notes the backstory.

Source: the official IANA HTTP Status Code Registry, fetched 2026-06-04. Most codes are defined by RFC 9110 (HTTP Semantics). Code numbers and reason phrases match the registry exactly; the plain-English meanings were written for this tool. Unassigned and retired codes are left out; nothing is invented.
http-status-codes.source: Code numbers and reason phrases come from the official IANA HTTP status registry; the plain-English meanings were written for this tool.

wpa-security.name: WPA Security
wpa-security.purpose: A side-by-side table of Wi-Fi security modes (from WEP through WPA3-Enterprise) showing encryption, key method, protected-management-frame support, and a deploy-or-avoid verdict, plus a rundown of the advanced features that set them apart.
wpa-security.whyHere: When choosing or auditing the security on a network, this confirms a mode's cipher, key method, whether management-frame protection is required, and whether it is recommended, acceptable, or best avoided.
howToUse: Each mode has a colored verdict chip, and the word is always spelled out: WEP and WPA1 read "Do not deploy" / "Deprecated" (red); WPA2-Personal reads "Acceptable" (amber); WPA3-Personal and WPA3-Enterprise read "Recommended" (green); Enhanced Open reads "Open networks" and WPA2-Enterprise reads "Enterprise std" (blue/info).

Below the table, the feature rows explain the mechanisms (for example SAE replaces the older shared-key handshake and adds forward secrecy; OWE encrypts open networks without a password; 6 GHz requires WPA3 or OWE).
fieldNotes: What it shows: security modes: WEP, WPA (WPA1), WPA2-Personal, WPA3-Personal, Enhanced Open, WPA2-Enterprise, WPA3-Enterprise, each with its category, encryption suite, key method, protected-management-frame support (Not supported / Optional / Required), and a verdict chip. Advanced features: SAE, protected management frames (802.11w), OWE, forward secrecy, 192-bit security mode, WPA3-required-on-6-GHz, and the 802.1X/RADIUS roles.
WPA3-Enterprise's 192-bit mode (GCMP-256 with HMAC-SHA-384 and ECDH/ECDSA-384) is noted as required for government and classified deployments. 6 GHz does not allow WPA2 or anything older.
The verdicts are reference guidance reflecting current best practice.
Source and standard: IEEE 802.11 and Wi-Fi Alliance security standards. The verdict colors were adjusted from their original shades to meet accessibility contrast requirements.
wpa-security.source: The modes and features come from IEEE 802.11 and Wi-Fi Alliance security standards.

db-reference.name: dB Reference
db-reference.purpose: A decibel cheat sheet: how a dB change maps to a power or voltage ratio with rules of thumb, plus common dBm values with their actual power and a real-world example.
db-reference.whyHere: Quick mental math in the field: "+3 dB is double the power," "what power is +30 dBm," "what is the FCC 2.4 GHz limit in dBm."
howToUse: In the ratio table, gains show in lime and losses in red.
Handy anchors: +3 dB = 2x power; +10 dB = 10x; +30 dBm = 1 W; 0 dBm = 1 mW; -67 dBm = enterprise voice minimum; -70 dBm = enterprise data minimum; -80 dBm = typical Wi-Fi receiver sensitivity.
fieldNotes: What it shows: dB power ratios: a dB change (+3 to +30, -3 to -20) mapped to a power ratio, a voltage ratio, and a rule-of-thumb note. Common dBm reference points: dBm anchors (+36 down to -100 dBm) mapped to power (watts / mW / nW / pW) and context (regulatory limits, typical transmit powers, sensitivity floors).
Footnote: "0 dBd is about 2.15 dBi (dipole reference). dBW = dBm - 30. Regulatory limits shown are US FCC; verify before compliance decisions."
Mostly US (FCC) limits with one ETSI anchor; read the context cell to see which jurisdiction each limit belongs to.

Source: the dBm context column cites specific regulatory limits: FCC 6 GHz standard-power EIRP (+36 dBm, AFC required), FCC 2.4 GHz maximum conducted (Part 15.247, +30 dBm), the FCC UNII-2A/2C and UNII-1 conducted maximums, and the ETSI 5 GHz EIRP limit (EN 301 893, +23 dBm).
db-reference.source: The ratios are standard decibel math and the dBm anchors cite published FCC and ETSI limits.

poe-reference.name: PoE Reference
poe-reference.purpose: A Power-over-Ethernet reference: the 802.3 PoE standards (power supplied, power delivered, which pairs carry it, class range) and the powered-device power classes (0 to 8) with the maximum power that reaches the device.
poe-reference.whyHere: When sizing PoE, this confirms what a switch port sends versus what actually reaches the device, and which 802.3 standard or class a given access point needs.
howToUse: Supplied power is what leaves the switch; delivered power is what is left at the device after cable loss (for example 802.3at supplies 30 W and delivers 25.5 W).
The class table maps a device's negotiated class to its maximum draw (for example Class 4 = 25.5 W = the PoE+ maximum; Class 8 = 71.3 W = the Type 4 maximum).
fieldNotes: What it shows: PoE standards: 802.3af (PoE), 802.3at (PoE+), 802.3bt Type 3 (PoE++/4PPoE), 802.3bt Type 4 (PoE++ Hi), each with watts supplied, watts delivered, powered pairs (2 of 4 or 4 of 4), and supported class range. Powered-device power classes: class 0 to 8, each with the maximum power at the device, the 802.3 standard that defines it, and a note.
A footnote points to the PoE Budget tool for sizing a switch against the devices connected to it.
Written "802.3" (not "802.3x"). General reference, not region-specific.
Source: the IEEE 802.3af/at/bt standards.
poe-reference.source: The values come from the IEEE 802.3af/at/bt standards.

coax-cable.name: Coax Cable
coax-cable.purpose: A coaxial cable reference: impedance, velocity factor, outer diameter, highest usable frequency, and typical use for the common RG- and LMR-series cables.
coax-cable.whyHere: When planning an antenna run, this helps you pick the right LMR size for the length and frequency, and steer clear of a 75-ohm mismatch.
howToUse: Each cable is a block: the name, then a spec line (impedance / velocity factor % / diameter / max GHz), then a use note.
The 75-ohm entry (RG-6) is shown dimmed: it does not match the 50-ohm world of Wi-Fi and is listed for reference only ("CATV/satellite, NOT for Wi-Fi").
A higher velocity factor means slightly less propagation delay and loss.
fieldNotes: What it shows: for each cable (RG-58, RG-8/U, RG-213, RG-214, LMR-100A through LMR-1200, RG-6): impedance, velocity factor (%), diameter (mm), maximum frequency (GHz), and a typical-use note.

A footnote points to the Cable Loss tool for exact attenuation. Wi-Fi is a 50-ohm system, so the dimmed 75-ohm RG-6 is a mismatch. The maximum frequencies are typical ceilings. General reference, not region-specific.
Source: published manufacturer and industry spec values for the named cable series.
coax-cable.source: Published manufacturer and industry spec values for the named cable series.

ethernet-cable.name: Ethernet Cable & Connector
ethernet-cable.purpose: The twisted-pair side of structured cabling in one place: the Cat5e-through-Cat8 capability chart (bandwidth, top speed, distance at 1G and 10G, PoE, shielding, typical use) plus the T568A / T568B RJ-45 pinout (pin, wire color, twisted pair, and 100/1000 Base-T function).
ethernet-cable.whyHere: For picking a cable category for a run (its bandwidth, 10G reach, and PoE++ suitability), or terminating an RJ-45 jack when you need the pin colors for the standard you chose.
howToUse: Cable categories section: scroll the table sideways. "N/A" in a distance column means that speed is not supported (for example Cat5e has no 10G distance). Cat6 reaches 10G only out to 55 m; Cat6A reaches 10G across the full 100 m and supports all of 802.3bt; Cat8 carries 1G/10G to 100 m, but its 25/40G design speed is limited to about 30 m.

RJ-45 pinout section: switch between T568B and T568A to see each pin's wire color, twisted pair, and Base-T function. Every color is named as well as shown, so you never rely on color alone. The view is "plug face, clip down, pin 1 on the left."
Use the copy button to grab the categories, speed grades, shielding codes, notes, and the selected pinout as text.
fieldNotes: Consolidated 2026-06-12 from the former Ethernet Cable, Ethernet Pinout, and Cable & Connector tiles into this single tool.

Cable chart: for each peer category (Cat5e, Cat6, Cat6A, Cat8): maximum bandwidth (MHz), top speed, distance at 1 Gbps and 10 Gbps, PoE support, shielding, and a typical-use note. Plus a BASE-T speed-grade table (1G/2.5G/5G/10G) and an ISO/IEC 11801 shielding-code key.

Cat 7 is not a TIA standard. TIA never ratified Category 7; it exists only as ISO/IEC Class F (Cat 7A as Class FA) and uses GG45 / TERA connectors rather than the usual RJ-45. For 10G structured cabling, Cat 6A is the TIA-recognized choice. A warning callout flags this.
PoE++ tip: bundled Cat6 running PoE++ builds up significant heat; Cat6A sheds it better, and TIA-568 recommends Cat6A for PoE++ in bundles.

Pinout: each standard's eight pin rows show the pin number, a wire-color swatch and its name (for example "Orange / White"), the twisted-pair number (1 to 4), and the 100/1000 Base-T function. Only pairs 2 and 3 (orange and green) differ between T568A and T568B; a crossover cable uses A on one end and B on the other, rarely needed today since most switches and network cards auto-adjust.
Source: the cable categories reflect the TIA-568 and ISO 11801 standards, with the Cat 7 ISO/IEC Class F caveat called out.
ethernet-cable.source: The categories and pinout reflect the TIA-568 and ISO 11801 standards.

fiber-optic.name: Fiber Optic
fiber-optic.purpose: Fiber types (OM1 to OM5, OS1/OS2) with core and cladding sizes, jacket color, and reach, plus connector types (LC/SC/ST/FC/MPO) and polish types (PC/UPC/APC).
fiber-optic.whyHere: When specifying fiber, this confirms a fiber type's reach at a given speed, its jacket color, and whether it is current or legacy.
howToUse: Scroll the distance table sideways. A blank speed column means that speed is not supported (OM1 and OM2 do not do 40G or 100G).
OM1 and OM2 are shown dimmed (legacy). Jacket colors: OM1/OM2 = Orange, OM3 = Aqua, OM4 = Aqua (violet is a manufacturer convention, not the standard), OM5 = Lime Green, OS1/OS2 = Yellow.
Connectors: LC and SC are the most common; LC's smaller 1.25 mm ferrule packs in tighter than the 2.5 mm SC/ST/FC. MPO/MTP is the multi-fiber ribbon connector.
Polish: APC is the green, 8-degree angled endface with the lowest back-reflection. APC and UPC never mate. Connector body color and cable jacket color are two separate systems.
fieldNotes: What it shows: two sub-tables. Distance by data rate, per fiber type: core/cladding, modal bandwidth (MHz-km), and supported distance at 1G/10G/40G/100G. Jacket color code and notes, per fiber type: a jacket color swatch and name, and a deployment note.

Footnote: distances follow TIA-568/ISO 11801; the real limit depends on the transceiver, the number of splices, and connector loss. OM3 and OM4 are the current deployment standards; OM1 and OM2 are legacy (dimmed). OM5's wideband window note (about 1,850 to 2,470 MHz-km near 953 nm) is kept as written. General reference, not region-specific.
Source and standard: TIA-568 / ISO 11801, cited in the footnote.
fiber-optic.source: The values follow the TIA-568 and ISO 11801 standards.

antenna-connectors.name: Antenna Connectors
antenna-connectors.purpose: A practical guide to 18 Wi-Fi antenna connectors. For each one you get the full name, its reverse-polarity variant, where it typically shows up in Wi-Fi, whether it suits indoor or outdoor use, how it attaches, its body size, how the signal travels through it, its impedance, its frequency rating, what it mates with, and a few real-world notes. It also includes a diagram explaining polarity, an at-a-glance comparison table, a summary of what enterprise vendors tend to ship, a to-scale size comparison, and the six connectors a Wi-Fi engineer meets most often.
antenna-connectors.whyHere: For when you are trying to identify or connect an antenna lead in the field. It answers the questions you actually ask on the job: is this RP-SMA or SMA, will these two connect, does it handle 6 GHz, and which connector does this vendor use.
howToUse: Browse the connectors grouped by where they live (enterprise panel or external, outdoor and point-to-point, board-level or internal, and test or cellular). Each card shows the connector name, its full name, an RP marker when it is a reverse-polarity variant, and a real photo where one is freely available (some connectors show a line diagram instead).
Each card lists typical use, indoor or outdoor fit, how it attaches, size, signal path, impedance, frequency, and what it mates with, followed by a field-notes line.
Type in the search box to filter instantly across every field: name, vendor, attachment style, size, signal path, frequency, or any word in a note.

Below the table you'll find a polarity diagram, a compare-at-a-glance table (connector, size, signal path, typical use) that scrolls sideways on a phone, a summary of enterprise vendor trends, the connectors listed largest to smallest with a to-scale diagram, and the six connectors most engineers meet.
fieldNotes: The key field gotcha: every connector here is 50 ohm, and a reverse-polarity connector will thread onto its standard counterpart, but the center contacts will not actually connect.
U.FL and I-PEX MHF I share the same footprint and connect to each other; the smaller MHF 4 does NOT connect to either.
DART is Cisco's Smart Antenna Connector, a proprietary multi-port interface that breaks out to RP-TNC, N-type, or RP-N using Cisco adapter cables.
Size is the connector body width (across the flats for threaded parts, outer diameter for board-level parts). Treat it as a rough field-recognition aid, not a precise mechanical spec.
Connector photos appear only where a freely-licensed one exists. N-Type, TNC, and RP-TNC currently have no such photo, so they show a line diagram rather than a stand-in.

plmn-id-reference.name: PLMN ID Reference
plmn-id-reference.purpose: An offline, searchable, grouped lookup of US Public Land Mobile Network identifiers. Each row pairs a carrier or operator with its MCC, MNC, full PLMN ID, and operational status, covering MCCs 310 through 316 (the US mainland plus Puerto Rico, Guam, the US Virgin Islands, and American Samoa).
plmn-id-reference.whyHere: For when you have a PLMN ID, MCC, or MNC off a cellular scan, a SIM, or a private-LTE / CBRS deployment and need to know which carrier it belongs to, or you need a carrier's code to set up or verify a private cellular network. It works fully offline, so it stays reliable in the field where there is no data connection.
howToUse: Browse the entries grouped by MCC (310 through 316), each group sorted from lowest PLMN ID to highest.
Type in the search box to filter instantly by code (MCC, MNC, or full PLMN ID) or by carrier or operator name.
Each row shows the PLMN ID, the MCC/MNC pair, the carrier (and its parent operator when different), and the operational status.
Use the copy action to grab the current view as plain text (the filtered results when you're searching, otherwise the full table).
fieldNotes: MNC and PLMN ID are text with meaningful leading zeros (for example, MNC 004, PLMN ID 310004). A two-digit and a three-digit MNC are different codes, so never read them as plain numbers.
Status values include operational, not operational, reserved, and unknown. A reserved or not-operational allocation may still show up on the air or in old records, so the status column matters.
US-only by design. The data covers MCCs 310 through 316; it does not include non-US carriers.
The data comes from US MCC/MNC (PLMN ID) allocations, checked against the published 'Mobile country code' tables (2026-06-05).
plmn-id-reference.source: The tool reads from a bundled list of US carrier codes, kept up to date and available offline.

wifi-glossary.name: Wi-Fi Glossary
wifi-glossary.purpose: Plain-language definitions of 92 Wi-Fi terms across 8 categories: bands and spectrum, standards and Wi-Fi generations, signal and RF basics, speed and modulation, how devices share the air, access points and roaming, security, and performance and troubleshooting. Each entry pairs the full term with its companion identifier, where it has one, and a definition written for working engineers. Many terms carry both their IEEE name and their Wi-Fi Alliance name.
wifi-glossary.whyHere: For when a term in a spec sheet, a log, or a standards document is the one thing standing between you and understanding what's going on. It tells you what CCI, DFS, FSPL, or OFDMA actually mean in Wi-Fi terms, and it pairs the IEEE generation number with the Wi-Fi Alliance name so 802.11ax and Wi-Fi 6 read as one thing.
howToUse: Browse the terms grouped by category, arranged so related ideas sit together rather than alphabetically.
Type in the search box to filter instantly across the term name, abbreviation, and definition.
Each entry shows the full term, its abbreviation or IEEE / Wi-Fi Alliance identifier when it has one, and a definition in Keith's voice.
Use the copy action to grab the current view as plain text (the filtered results when you're searching, otherwise the full list).
fieldNotes: Vendor-neutral by design. Definitions describe the standards-based behavior, not one vendor's implementation.
Naming is dual where the standard and the marketing brand differ: both the IEEE designation and the Wi-Fi Alliance generation name appear, so you can find the same term either way.
This is the general-vocabulary companion to the Wi-Fi Authentication Glossary; both use the same searchable, grouped layout with a different set of terms.
The content is a curated edition of 92 terms.
wifi-glossary.source: The tool reads from a curated, bundled list of 92 Wi-Fi terms, available offline.

wifi-auth-glossary.name: Wi-Fi Authentication Glossary
wifi-auth-glossary.purpose: Plain-language definitions of the 58 Wi-Fi authentication terms a network or security pro actually meets: the 802.1X / EAP framework, RADIUS and AAA, WPA2 / WPA3 and SAE, PSK and Enterprise modes, certificates, and the supporting acronyms. Each entry pairs the full term and its abbreviation with a definition written for working engineers, grouped by topic.
wifi-auth-glossary.whyHere: For when a term in a config screen, a log, or a standards document is the one thing standing between you and understanding the authentication flow. It tells you what AAA, SAE, PMF, or EAP-TLS actually mean in Wi-Fi terms, without a vendor's slant.
howToUse: Browse the terms grouped by category (Core Authentication, EAP methods, key management, and the rest).
Type in the search box to filter instantly across the term name, abbreviation, and definition.
Each entry shows the full term, its abbreviation when it has one, and a definition in Keith's voice.
Use the copy action to grab the current view as plain text (the filtered results when you're searching, otherwise the full list).
fieldNotes: Vendor-neutral by design. Definitions describe the standards-based behavior, not one vendor's implementation.
This is the authentication-focused companion to the general Wi-Fi Glossary; both use the same searchable, grouped layout with a different set of terms.
The content is a curated edition of 58 authentication terms.
wifi-auth-glossary.source: The tool reads from a curated, bundled list of 58 Wi-Fi authentication terms, available offline.

osi-model.name: OSI Model
osi-model.purpose: The 7-layer OSI reference model: layer number, name, one-word function, protocol data unit (PDU), example modern protocols, and typical hardware.
osi-model.whyHere: For pinning down where a fault lives: knowing which layer is failing tells you which tool to reach for.
howToUse: Scroll the table sideways and read it by layer number.

Example mappings: Layer 3 Network = Routing, PDU is a Packet, protocols like IPv4/IPv6/ICMP/IPsec, hardware like a router or L3 switch; Layer 2 Data Link = Framing, PDU is a Frame, protocols like Ethernet (802.3) / Wi-Fi (802.11) / 802.1Q / ARP, hardware like a switch, access point, bridge, or NIC; Layer 1 Physical = Bits, PDU is a Bit, media like RF, fiber, or copper, hardware like a cable, radio, or hub.
fieldNotes: What it shows: the 7 layers, top (7 Application) to bottom (1 Physical), each with its layer number (in lime), name, a one-word function keyword, PDU (Data / Segment / Packet / Frame / Bit), example protocols, and typical hardware.

Footnote: PDU means protocol data unit; layers 5 through 7 are commonly grouped together as "data" in TCP/IP practice; ARP is widely placed at Layer 2 (some texts call it L2/L3), and it resolves Layer 3 addresses to Layer 2 addresses.
The function column is a neutral keyword. Standard reference, not region-specific.
Based on the ISO/IEC 7498-1:1994 standard plus the usual IETF/IEEE mappings of protocols to layers.
osi-model.source: The tool draws on the published OSI standard and the standard protocol-to-layer mappings.

top-level-domains.name: Top-Level Domains
top-level-domains.purpose: A curated reference to the DNS top-level domains a network or IT pro actually meets, grouped by registry type: generic (gTLD), country-code (ccTLD), sponsored or restricted, infrastructure, and notable newer gTLDs. Each entry shows the TLD, its type, and a short note on who manages it and how it's typically used.
top-level-domains.whyHere: Knowing a TLD's type tells you who runs it and what to expect: a sponsored domain like .gov or .edu is eligibility-verified, .arpa is reverse-DNS infrastructure you never register, and .io or .ai are country-code domains used generically rather than true generic TLDs.
howToUse: Scroll the grouped cards, or use the Type filter to narrow to one registry class.
Read the TLD (in lime, on the left) then its note. The copy action exports the full curated set as a table, no matter which filter is active.
fieldNotes: Curated, not exhaustive: the live root zone has roughly 1,500 generic TLDs and about 250 country-code TLDs. This lists the meaningful, field-relevant set.

Accuracy: .io (British Indian Ocean Territory), .ai (Anguilla), .co (Colombia), .tv, and .me are technically country-code TLDs commonly used generically. They are NOT true generic TLDs, and the notes say so.
Registry classification and sponsoring organizations come from the IANA Root Zone Database; new-gTLD program facts come from ICANN.
top-level-domains.source: The tool draws on the official IANA and ICANN registry records for TLD classification.

rj-connectors.name: RJ Connectors
rj-connectors.purpose: A reference to the registered-jack connector shapes (RJ11, RJ14, RJ25, RJ45/8P8C, RJ48, RJ48C, RJ48X): positions, conductors, the modular body each uses, and its typical use. This is about the connector body, not the wiring.
rj-connectors.whyHere: In the field you meet the same modular bodies doing very different jobs: RJ11 carries one phone line, RJ45 (8P8C) carries Ethernet, and RJ48 reuses the 8P8C body for T1/E1 with a different pin assignment. Telling them apart keeps you from cabling the wrong jack.
howToUse: Read each connector card: the name, its modular notation (for example, 8P8C), positions, conductors, and typical use.
For T568A / T568B pin-to-pair-color wiring on the RJ45 (8P8C) connector, tap the cross-link card to open the Ethernet Pinout tool.
fieldNotes: Accuracy: "RJ45" is the everyday name for the 8-position, 8-conductor (8P8C) modular connector used for Ethernet. Strictly speaking, RJ45 was a telephone wiring standard; the data connector is properly the 8P8C modular jack.
The PnCm notation means an n-position body with m conductors populated. For example, 6P2C is a 6-position body with 2 conductors (RJ11), and 8P8C is fully populated (RJ45/RJ48).
This table does NOT repeat the T568A/T568B pin colors: that wiring lives in the Ethernet Pinout tool, which this screen links to.
Based on the registered-jack (USOC) interface standards and the common modular-connector shape conventions.
rj-connectors.source: The tool draws on the registered-jack (USOC) and modular-connector standards.

ascii-reference.name: ASCII / Hex / Binary
ascii-reference.purpose: The full 128-character US-ASCII table with decimal, hex, octal, and binary for each code, plus extra quick-reference tables for reading hex dumps and protocol fields.
ascii-reference.whyHere: For when you're decoding a hex dump or a protocol field and need to look up a byte's character, its four numeric forms, or the meaning of a control code.
howToUse: The numeric columns (dec / hex / oct / bin) and the character itself use a monospaced font so octets and look-alike characters (l / I / O / 0) read unambiguously.
Control rows show a short mnemonic (NUL, LF, CR, ESC, and so on); printable rows show the character (a space is shown as "SP").
Filter by typing a decimal, hex (with or without 0x), octal, binary value, a character or mnemonic, or a keyword.
fieldNotes: What it shows: a "how to read this" card, then Control codes (0 to 31, plus 127) and Printable characters (32 to 126) as tables with Dec / Hex / Oct / Bin / Char / Description. Plus extra cards: range boundaries worth memorizing, newlines on the wire, the case bit (0x20), a nibble-to-hex map, powers of two, hex place values, and the high range (128 to 255): there's no single "extended ASCII".

The high-range card is deliberately honest: ASCII stops at 127; bytes 128 to 255 mean different things depending on the encoding, so there is no single "extended ASCII." It documents UTF-8 (bytes 0 to 127 are identical to ASCII; 128 to 255 are part of multi-byte sequences), ISO-8859-1 / Latin-1, and Windows-1252 (a common source of garbled text), with the rule: bytes 0 to 127 are portable; 128 to 255 are not, so know the encoding before you decode.
Based on the US-ASCII standard for the 128 values, with the high-range guidance following the ISO-8859-1, Windows-1252, and Unicode / UTF-8 specifications. Standard reference, not region-specific.
ascii-reference.source: The tool draws on the US-ASCII standard and the common character-encoding specifications.

emoji-reference.name: Top 30 Emoji
emoji-reference.purpose: The 30 most-used emoji, ranked 1 to 30, each with its official Unicode name and a plain "common use" note.
emoji-reference.whyHere: A lightweight guide to what people actually mean by an emoji, handy when meanings drift (for example, the skull = "that's hilarious," not death).
howToUse: Ranked most-used first (face with tears of joy at number 1).
The official Unicode name is the searchable key for each row.
The "common use" note describes how people typically read it, with generational or fandom context where it matters.
fieldNotes: What it shows, per emoji: rank (1 to 30), the emoji itself, its official Unicode name, and a "common use" note on how people usually read it today.
The intro is explicit: "common use" is how people usually read each emoji today, not an official Unicode definition; meanings drift by audience, region, and generation.
The emoji is drawn using your device's built-in color-emoji font, so it looks native on each platform.
Names come from Unicode; the ranking is weighted toward private-messaging use, not social-media chatter.
emoji-reference.source: The tool draws on the official Unicode names, with a messaging-weighted popularity ranking.

test-my-connection.name: Test My Connection
test-my-connection.purpose: Answers the everyday question "is the slowdown my Wi-Fi or my internet?" in plain English, and tells you what to say to support.
test-my-connection.whyHere: The consumer-friendly front door. It uses the same measurements and the same verdict logic as the pro Wi-Fi vs Internet tool, dressed up for a non-technical user. Reach for it when you want a one-tap answer plus vetted self-help steps, not engineer-grade numbers.
howToUse: Open the tool and tap Run.

On iOS, the Wi-Fi signal details (RSSI, channel, rate, PHY) come from the companion Shortcut, and running the test fires it AUTOMATICALLY. You don't have to tap anything to capture the Wi-Fi RF. The details then appear both on screen (the live "Wi-Fi signal" graphs) and in the copied report. If the automatic capture can't reach the Shortcut (not installed, or the launch was cancelled), the "Your Wi-Fi link" card offers a manual "Capture Wi-Fi details" button instead of a grid of "Unavailable," so you know it's a capture step, not a broken tool. The security type and BSSID still fill in on their own without the Shortcut.

On macOS, granting Location only affects whether the network name and BSSID show (macOS 14 and later hide those two fields until you allow Location); the verdict itself is based on link rate and never needs it. To save you a trip into System Settings, running the test brings up the native "Allow Location" prompt the FIRST time the permission hasn't been set (at most once per screen open, never on every run). If Location isn't granted, the "Your Wi-Fi link" card shows a short inline hint where the network name would sit, "Wi-Fi network name hidden. Location access needed," with a one-tap action: "Allow Location" while the prompt can still appear, or "Open settings" (a direct link to the macOS Location Services pane) once it's been denied. After you grant it, the network name and BSSID fill in; until then the copied report labels them "Unavailable (grant Location access to show the network name)" rather than a bare "Unavailable."
Read the two status chips (Wi-Fi / Internet) and the headline.

The whole test takes up to about a minute (three back-to-back measurement windows of up to about 15 seconds each: download, upload, then a loaded-responsiveness check). A short "This usually takes about half a minute" note shows under the loader.
Use the "Run again" action at the top right to re-run the whole Wi-Fi and internet test.

Tap "Copy these details" for a thorough, help-desk-ready report. It opens with a "WLAN Pros Toolbox: Connection Report" header plus the test date and time, then clearly labelled sections: Wi-Fi (SSID, BSSID, RSSI, noise, SNR, Tx/Rx rate, channel, width, band, PHY, security), Internet (download, upload, latency, jitter, loss, responsiveness), DNS (a real resolution-time measurement), Network (local IP, subnet mask, default gateway, plus DHCP server / DNS server(s) / VLAN marked honestly unavailable), ISP (public IP, ISP/org, ASN, when reachable), cloud apps reachability, and the verdict. Unavailable fields say so; the ISP and cloud-apps sections are left out cleanly when you're offline.

Scroll to the bottom for the "Cloud apps reachable?" panel. It checks whether your device can reach recognizable services (Facebook, Instagram, TikTok, YouTube, Netflix, Google, iCloud, Microsoft 365, Cloudflare, AWS, Zoom, Slack, GitHub, and a Cloudflare DNS anchor) and times each connection. Its "Re-check cloud apps" button re-runs ONLY this panel, not the whole test.
test-my-connection.algorithm: It runs two things in one pass and feeds both into the shared verdict logic: a read of your current Wi-Fi link (from macOS directly, or from the companion Shortcut on iOS), and a full network-quality run against a diverse set of neutral public servers. It turns the network-quality grades into a simple internet-health call: internet is rated GOOD only when download, upload, latency, and loss all grade good or excellent; otherwise it's marginal. The five engineer-grade verdicts are collapsed into four everyday outcomes, plus a two-part chip status where Wi-Fi and internet each read "Fine," "Slow," or "Couldn't check." The outcomes are: "Looks like your Wi-Fi"; "Mostly your Wi-Fi"; "Looks like your Internet"; "Both look fine"; "Couldn't check everything" (internet measured, Wi-Fi not); and "Couldn't complete the check" (neither measured).
topNotes: The internet number is a real-world download measurement. The app pulls from several independent public servers at once, drops any that come back throttled or far below the pack, and adds up the rest. That makes it an honest figure for your everyday path to the internet, and it can read lower than a one-number tool that hits only the single nearest cache. Upload is measured and shown on its own. (reference: How the Toolbox Measures Throughput)

Why your speed test may read differently: no single number is "the" speed of a connection. When the Toolbox shows you an internet download figure, it is real, sustained throughput to a diverse set of neutral public servers, measured over about a 15-second window with the slow-start ramp discarded. If another test on the same connection reads higher, that's the method talking: a single nearby edge cache, or a run that counts its ramp-up. If the Toolbox can't get a clean read, it tells you so rather than inventing a number. (reference: How the Toolbox Measures Throughput)

What the Wi-Fi number is, and what it is not: it's your usable Wi-Fi capacity, which the app estimates at about 55% of the negotiated Wi-Fi link rate. Real-world throughput over Wi-Fi runs roughly 50 to 60% of that negotiated rate once you account for protocol overhead, acknowledgments, contention, and retries. This number describes the Wi-Fi hop only. It is the road between your device and the access point, and nothing past it. It says nothing about your internet speed. Don't compare the Wi-Fi capacity number to an internet speed test. (reference: How the Toolbox Measures Throughput)
fieldNotes: What's on screen is what's copied: the live "Wi-Fi signal" graphs, the technical Wi-Fi sub-card, and the "Copy these details" report all read one unified Wi-Fi source, the single link read taken when the test finishes, combined with the live sampler's latest reading (the same stream the graphs draw from). On iOS the one-shot read can arrive before the companion Shortcut's stream delivers, so without this the copy could list RSSI, channel, or rate as "Unavailable" while the graphs showed live signal. The merge only adds: the security and BSSID info is never lost, and the live stream only fills gaps the one-shot read lacked, so the copy can only ever gain a real reported field, never a made-up one.

Platform differences: macOS reads the link natively (Tx rate, RSSI, SNR, but no Rx; the Rx row reads "Unavailable (not exposed on macOS)," a known platform limit, not a glitch). iOS reads the signal details via the Shortcut (Tx and Rx, fired automatically by a run) and the security and BSSID natively. If the link can't be read (wired, or iOS without a Shortcut capture), it falls back to the honest "couldn't check" path; it does not guess a side.

Honest unknown values: a channel of 0 is the "no/unknown channel" placeholder some systems return; it's never a real Wi-Fi channel, so it shows as "Unavailable" rather than "0." Any unknown, zero, or empty Wi-Fi field shows an honest "Unavailable," never a misleading 0 or blank.

The headline is a hedge by design ("Looks like...," not "your Wi-Fi is broken"). The two chips teach the idea that Wi-Fi and internet are two separate things. "Both look fine" is the most useful real-world answer; it points you at the specific app or site instead of the connection.
The verdict wording is deliberately plain and non-diagnostic for a layperson. The underlying numbers and bands are the same as the Wi-Fi vs Internet tool; if you want the numbers, use that tool.

How it picks a side: it compares how much internet speed you're actually getting against how much your Wi-Fi link could realistically carry. When the internet measures fine on its own, it says both look fine; otherwise it leans toward Wi-Fi or internet depending on which one has the headroom to spare.
"Make sure you're on Wi-Fi and try again" is the honest answer when nothing measured, never a fake zero.

The cloud apps panel times a connection to each service on port 443 (sandboxed iOS and macOS apps can't send raw pings). A green check means the service answered and shows how long that connection took; it is NOT a measure of in-app call or stream quality. An unreachable service shows the word "unreachable" with its own distinct icon, never color alone. The list is a mix both the public and a WLAN pro recognize: social and consumer services (Facebook, Instagram, TikTok, YouTube, Netflix) plus pro and infrastructure services (Google, iCloud, Microsoft 365, Cloudflare, AWS, Zoom, Slack, GitHub) and a Cloudflare DNS liveness anchor.

The copied report and ISP info: "Copy these details" produces a sectioned report (header and date/time, then Wi-Fi / Internet / DNS / Network / ISP / cloud apps / verdict). The ISP section comes from a keyless lookup of your device's public internet address, giving the public IP, ISP or org name, and ASN. It runs alongside the measurement, never holds up the verdict, and fails gracefully: offline or a failed lookup simply leaves the ISP section out rather than inventing an address.

The test isn't a fixed length. It runs a quick under-a-second latency burst, then a roughly 15-second download window, an up-to-15-second upload window, and a roughly 15-second loaded-responsiveness check, so roughly 30 to 45 seconds end to end, near the low end on a fast link and the high end on a slow or stalled endpoint.

DNS resolution time: the report and the on-screen Network card carry a REAL DNS resolution-time measurement, timing a lookup of a stable host (cloudflare.com, then google.com, then apple.com) through your device's own resolver. It's a native in-app measurement, so it's safe inside the sandboxed macOS App Store build and works on iOS. It's labelled "DNS resolution time" so it's never confused with anything the network-quality measurement produces (that measurement does not test DNS). A failed lookup marks DNS "Not available," never a made-up time.

Network detail fields, what is and isn't obtainable: local IP, subnet mask, and default gateway are read natively and work on both platforms. DHCP server and DNS server(s) are NOT available to a sandboxed app (reading them needs system-level access the macOS sandbox blocks and iOS doesn't provide), so both show "Not available on this device." A VLAN (802.1Q) tag is stripped by the switch or access point before the frame reaches the device's OS, so no app on the device can see it; it shows "Not visible to endpoint devices." None of these are ever guessed.
test-my-connection.source: The tool reads your current Wi-Fi link and runs a live network-quality test, then applies the same verdict logic as the pro Wi-Fi vs Internet tool.

analyze-results.name: Analyze Results
analyze-results.purpose: Turns a finished Test My Connection result into a plain-language, conclusion-first report: what your connection is telling you and what to do about it, most important thing first.
analyze-results.whyHere: Test My Connection gives you the headline verdict and the raw numbers. Analyze Results explains them. Reach for it when you want the why behind the verdict and the recommended next steps, all worked out right on your device. It sits next to Copy on the result screen: Copy saves the raw report to send to support, Analyze explains it.
howToUse: Run Test My Connection first so there is a measured result to explain.
Tap "Analyze my results" beneath the verdict.

Read the findings top to bottom. The most important ones lead (the verdict, an open network, packet loss, a dead path), then signal, band, and internet-quality context follow. Each finding carries a plain severity word (ACTION / WORTH A LOOK / CONTEXT) plus a color, never color on its own.
Tap Copy (top right) to save the whole analysis as plain text.
analyze-results.algorithm: The tool runs a set of about 33 plain-English rules across nine areas: the overall verdict, signal, noise and signal-to-noise, band and channel width, internet quality, DNS, security, whether popular cloud apps are reachable, and honesty checks for anything the phone didn't measure. It checks those rules against the same result already on your screen, using the exact same thresholds the rest of the app uses, so nothing contradicts. Findings are ordered by how serious they are (critical, then important, then context), which is why the verdict always comes first. Reassuring "no problem here" notes are hidden unless a real issue also turned up, so the report never wastes your time narrating a non-issue. Everything happens on your device: no network call, nothing stored, nothing leaves the phone.
fieldNotes: Some recommendation wording is still being finalized. Any finding whose wording isn't final yet carries an honest "draft guidance" note, and the report shows a draft banner, so advice never reads as final before it is.

Two rules deliberately give you the honest trade-off instead of a blanket "switch": the "you're on 2.4 GHz" note and the "narrow channel width" note never tell you to just change bands or force the widest channel. Wider is not automatically better, and 2.4 GHz is the right choice for range and IoT.

If the platform didn't measure a field, no rule fires on it. Where something genuinely couldn't be read (for example channel width on an iPhone, or Wi-Fi signal details), the report explains the gap rather than guessing.
analyze-results.source: Reads the result already on your screen and runs a fixed set of plain-language rules against it, entirely on your device.

net-quality.name: Network Quality
net-quality.purpose: A one-shot measurement of how well your connection carries traffic: latency, jitter, loss, download, upload, and responsiveness, plus a reachability check against a set of popular cloud apps. Each measure is graded on its own; there is deliberately no single combined "score".
net-quality.whyHere: When you want to characterize how a connection behaves, not just "how many Mbps". Reach for it to see whether the internet path is healthy across several measures at once, the way a full network-quality check would.
howToUse: Open the tool. A live latency trend starts sampling right away (every 30 seconds) while the screen is open.
Tap "Run test" for the full one-shot measurement. Download, upload, and responsiveness only run on a full test.
Read the six graded rows and the popular-sites reachability table.
net-quality.algorithm: The app measures everything itself. Latency, jitter, and loss come from timing 10 back-to-back connection attempts to a well-known public host on port 443 (it times connections rather than classic ping, because the desktop sandbox blocks that). Jitter is the average variation between one sample and the next; loss is the share of attempts that failed. If every attempt fails, latency and jitter honestly read "Unavailable" while loss is a real 100%. Download runs several streams at once (five by default), each against a different independent public server or CDN from a diverse pool, over a shared window of about 15 seconds. The first few seconds of TCP slow-start ramp are discarded, so the figure is sustained steady-state throughput, not the ramp. Each server's own rate is measured, any server that comes back below roughly half the median is dropped as a throttled outlier, and the survivors' rates are summed into the aggregate download, so one slow server cannot drag the number down; if every stream fails it says so rather than showing a fake 0. Upload is an honest single stream (only one public destination is a verified large-upload sink), and an empty or failed transfer reads as a failure, not 0. Responsiveness is a simplified estimate of how the connection holds up while busy: it measures round-trip time during a download and converts it to a responsiveness-per-minute figure (this is a lightweight take inspired by the industry method, not the full standard). Reachability is a quick connection test to 14 well-known cloud hosts a mix of everyday and professional users recognize (Facebook, Instagram, TikTok, YouTube, Netflix, Google, iCloud, Microsoft 365, Cloudflare, AWS, Zoom, Slack, GitHub, and a DNS anchor). Grade bands: Latency ms Excellent under 20, Good under 50, Fair under 100, Poor 100 or more (grounded in the ITU-T G.114 guidance). Jitter ms under 5 / under 15 / under 30. Loss % 0 / under 1 / under 2.5. Responsiveness 1000+ / 500+ / 100+. Download Mbps 100+ / 25+ / 5+ (a rule of thumb). Upload Mbps 20+ / 5+ / 1+ (a rule of thumb).
topNotes: Why your speed test may read differently: no single number is "the" speed of a connection. When the Toolbox shows an internet throughput figure, it is real throughput to neutral public destinations, measured over a fixed window and reported honestly. If another test on the same connection reads higher, that is the method talking: a different server, more streams, a longer ramp-up. If the Toolbox can't get a clean read, it tells you it couldn't rather than inventing a number. (see: How the Toolbox Measures Throughput)
fieldNotes: Platform differences: runs on macOS, Windows, Android, and iOS, and is coming to the WLAN Pi. On the web version it points you to download the app instead.

Read each grade on its own: a connection can be Excellent on latency and Poor on upload at the same time, which is exactly the point. The download and upload Mbps grades are "good enough for a household" rules of thumb, not standards. Responsiveness is directional only.
Latency times a full connection handshake to a real host, so it's a faithful stand-in for ping and reads slightly higher.

The on-screen footnote says it plainly: these are this app's own measurements, not any other service's score. Download adds two streams together, so it reflects your link's total capacity rather than a single flow. A failed measurement reads "Unavailable" with a note, never 0.
net-quality.source: The app measures everything itself against neutral public servers; nothing is outsourced to another service.

wifi-info.name: Wi-Fi Information
wifi-info.purpose: Shows the live details of your connection to the access point: SSID, BSSID, RSSI, noise, SNR, Tx/Rx rate, channel, width, band, and 802.11 standard.
wifi-info.whyHere: The "what is my radio actually doing right now" read. Reach for it to confirm band, channel, and width, check signal and SNR against your design targets, or sanity-check the rate the client negotiated.
howToUse: macOS: open the tool and it takes a snapshot. Tap Refresh to re-read. SSID and BSSID are hidden behind macOS Location Services; tap Grant (or open Location settings) to reveal them. The radio metrics don't need Location.

iOS: this is live streaming only. Install the combined "WLAN Pros Live" companion Shortcut once (via the iCloud link), then tap Start. The Shortcut loops, reading the connected access point each cycle and handing the details back to the app; Stop freezes the last values. There is no one-tap snapshot on iOS.
wifi-info.algorithm: All the platforms feed into one common picture of the connected access point. On macOS the details come straight from the system Wi-Fi framework; on iOS they come from the companion Shortcut, read tolerantly so small formatting differences don't break anything. In iOS Live mode, RSSI and SNR are graded against the bands below, while Tx/Rx rates get a trend (Rising / Falling / Steady) rather than a hard grade, because a "good" data rate depends entirely on band, width, and modulation. RSSI bands (dBm): Excellent -59 or stronger, Good -67 or stronger, Fair -72 or stronger, Poor below that. SNR bands (dB): Excellent 36 or higher, Good 25 or higher, Fair 15 or higher, Poor below that.
fieldNotes: macOS shows SSID/BSSID (Location-gated), RSSI, noise, SNR (reported directly), Tx rate, the 802.11 standard, channel, channel width, band, country code, interface name, and hardware MAC. It does not expose the Rx rate or Tx power (a limitation of the macOS Wi-Fi framework); those read "Not exposed by macOS CoreWLAN", never estimated.

iOS (via the Shortcut) shows SSID, BSSID, channel, RSSI, noise, the standard, and both Rx and Tx rate. SNR is worked out on-device as signal minus noise, and band is worked out from the channel number (both labeled "derived"). The Shortcut doesn't return channel width, so width reads "Not reported by iOS", never made up. Channel-to-band: 1 to 14 is 2.4 GHz, 36 to 177 is 5 GHz, 181 to 233 is 6 GHz; the ambiguous low 6 GHz range is read as 2.4/5 GHz and never silently claimed as 6 GHz.
Android and Windows show an honest "coming in a later update" state. The web version points you to download the app.

RSSI and SNR carry hard grades; read the rate as a trend, not a pass/fail. The standard label combines the 802.11 designation and the Wi-Fi generation (for example "802.11be (Wi-Fi 7)"; 802.11ax on 6 GHz shows as Wi-Fi 6E). Anything marked "derived" was calculated, not read straight from the radio.

macOS having no Rx rate and iOS having no width are real platform ceilings, not bugs. On macOS, a blank SSID is almost always a missing Location grant, not a hidden network. The macOS read has a 5-second safety timeout: a stalled read shows an honest "No Wi-Fi reading" rather than freezing.
wifi-info.source: Reads the live connection from the system Wi-Fi on macOS, and from the companion Shortcut on iOS.

cellular-info.name: Cellular Information
cellular-info.purpose: Shows your iPhone's mobile-network details: carrier, radio technology, signal bars, country code, and roaming status.
cellular-info.whyHere: A quick read of what the cellular radio is doing, handy when you're comparing how Wi-Fi hands off to cellular or confirming a device fell back to LTE or 5G.
howToUse: iOS only. Install the same combined "WLAN Pros Live" companion Shortcut, then tap Start; the Shortcut reads the cellular details and hands them to the app.
cellular-info.algorithm: Reads the details the Shortcut provides, tolerantly enough to handle small formatting quirks. The fields are: carrier name, radio technology (raw system codes are mapped to friendly labels like "5G (NSA)" or "LTE"; anything unrecognized passes through rather than being blanked), signal bars (the coarse 0 to 4 status-bar indicator), country code, and whether you're roaming.
fieldNotes: Platform differences: iOS is the only source. There's deliberately no built-in cellular path on Apple's own APIs, because the carrier data is deprecated and returns placeholder junk, and true cellular signal strength (in dBm) is off-limits to apps and would get the app rejected. So the data comes only via the Shortcut. macOS (no radio), Android, and Windows show an honest "not available on this platform"; the web version points you to download the app.

Signal bars are bars (0 to 4), never dressed up as a dBm figure; the app doesn't have a raw signal value and won't pretend it does. Bars are held to the 0 to 4 range, and anything out of range is ignored.
