This is the one tool where iOS actually exposes more than a normal app could, because the Shortcut runs inside Apple's own privacy context. A missing field reads "Unavailable", never a made-up value.
cellular-info.source: Reads cellular details supplied by the companion Shortcut on iOS.

roaming-log.name: Roaming Log
roaming-log.purpose: Records each time your device roams from one access point to another on the same network during a monitoring session, capturing the time, the from-and-to access points, and the signal at the moment of the roam.
roaming-log.whyHere: A roam is when your device switches access points while staying on the same network. Watching the live Wi-Fi signal lets you see a roam happen, but nothing records it. Reach for this when you want a timestamped list of roams from a walk-around, for example to see where a device clings to a far access point or hops too often.
howToUse: A "Current connection" card sits at the top of the screen, so you see your live link (network, access point, band and standard, channel and width, signal, SNR, Tx rate) the instant you open the tool, before any roam happens. It reads the same live link the roam watch does and refreshes with it; a field the platform doesn't report is left out rather than guessed.
macOS: recording starts automatically and keeps reading the Wi-Fi link while the screen is open. Walk your space; each roam is logged as it happens.

iOS: install the "WLAN Pros Live" companion Shortcut (the same one Wi-Fi Information uses), tap Start, then walk with the screen open. Because firing the Shortcut switches to the Shortcuts app, it waits for your deliberate Start tap rather than starting on its own.
Read the list newest-first: time, network, the from-and-to access points, and the signal (RSSI/SNR) read at the roam.

Tap Copy results in the top bar to put the whole session on the clipboard as plain text (a header plus one line per roam), so you can paste it into Notes or a report instead of screenshotting. The button stays disabled until at least one roam is recorded.
roaming-log.algorithm: The tool watches the connected access point and records a roam only when the access point's radio address changes while the network name stays the same. A missing signal simply pauses the chain without inventing a roam; the first access point it sees is just the starting point (no roam); a repeat of the same access point is ignored; and moving to a different network is a network switch, not a roam, so it's excluded. On macOS it checks on every read, so a roam registers even if the signal is identical before and after. On iOS it checks each new reading from the Shortcut and clears when you Stop and Start again.
fieldNotes: iOS records only while the app is open and in front. There's no way for any iOS app to log roams with the app closed or the phone in a pocket, the same ceiling Wi-Fi Check runs into. macOS records continuously while the screen is open.
A roam between two radios inside the SAME physical access point (for example 2.4 GHz to 5 GHz) is still counted and logged. That's correct: it's a real roam.

The signal shown is the reading at the moment the new access point first appeared, not an average. If the platform didn't report the signal for that sample, it honestly prints "Signal unavailable" rather than a fake value.
On iOS, stopping and starting clears the session log so a new walk doesn't inherit the last walk's roams.
roaming-log.source: Watches the connected access point and logs a roam whenever it changes while the network name stays the same.

interface-info.name: Interface Information
interface-info.purpose: Shows your device's own network state: each interface's IPv4/IPv6 addresses, the interface name and its likely type, plus gateway, DNS, Wi-Fi SSID/BSSID, and your device's primary IP wherever the platform exposes them.
interface-info.whyHere: The "what's my IP, gateway, and link" foundation. Reach for it first when you need your device's own address (say, before a ping or a sweep) or to confirm which interface is active.
howToUse: Open the tool; it takes a snapshot. Any field a platform can't provide reads "Not available on this platform".
interface-info.algorithm: The address and interface list comes from the operating system, and Wi-Fi link details the OS list doesn't include (SSID, BSSID, gateway, subnet mask, and the Wi-Fi IP addresses) are read separately. Each of those reads is guarded on its own, so one blocked read (for example SSID) never blanks the whole screen. The interface type (Wi-Fi, Ethernet, Cellular, Loopback, VPN, or Other) is a careful guess based on the interface name; on a Mac, for instance, the first port is guessed as Wi-Fi and later ones as Ethernet. The primary IPv4 prefers the Wi-Fi link IP, otherwise the first real (non-loopback) IPv4.
fieldNotes: Platform differences: SSID, BSSID, and gateway depend on the platform and its permission state. iOS needs the Wi-Fi entitlement plus Location for SSID/BSSID; macOS and Android vary. The web version is turned off. SSID is cleaned of wrapping quotes and the "<unknown ssid>" placeholder.
The interface type is a name-based guess; on a Mac the port split is heuristic, so on unusual hardware trust the addresses over the label.
Empty fields read as an honest "not available", never 0 or blank. Whether gateway and DNS show up depends on the platform and its permissions.
interface-info.source: Reads the device's own interface and address list from the operating system, plus Wi-Fi link details where available.

device-info.name: Device Info
device-info.purpose: Shows your device's own system facts: model (both the marketing name and the raw identifier), total physical memory (RAM), how long since the last boot, and the cellular IP address where the device has a cellular connection.
device-info.whyHere: The companion to Interface Information: that tool answers "what's my IP, gateway, and Wi-Fi link", this one answers "what device is this, how much RAM, how long since boot, and what's my cellular IP". Reach for it to confirm the hardware model or to read the cellular-side address the Wi-Fi-focused view doesn't show.
howToUse: Open the tool; it takes a snapshot. Any field a platform can't provide shows an honest unavailable state rather than a made-up value.
Tap Refresh in the top bar to re-read (uptime advances; the rest stays put).
Tap Copy to put the whole snapshot on the clipboard as labeled text.
device-info.algorithm: The model and total memory come from the device itself: on iOS the raw model identifier (for example iPhone16,2) is mapped to its marketing name, alongside the RAM size; macOS reports its own model and memory the same way. Uptime is read directly from the system as seconds since the last boot. The cellular IP is found by scanning the device's interfaces for the one iOS uses for cellular.
fieldNotes: The cellular IP relies on matching the usual iOS cellular interface name, since Apple doesn't guarantee those names are stable. "No cellular interface" is the normal, honest result on a Wi-Fi-only iPhone, in airplane mode, or on a Mac (which has no cellular).
Total memory is shown in binary units (an 8 GiB device reads "8 GB") to match how RAM is actually sized, while keeping the familiar GB/MB label.
Uptime is seconds since boot, shown as something like "3d 4h 12m", and always shows at least minutes (a just-booted device reads "0m").
Empty fields read as an honest "not available", never 0 or blank. On Android, the model is shown but total RAM isn't available, so it reads unavailable.
device-info.source: Reads the device's model, memory, uptime, and cellular address straight from the system.

dns-lookup.name: Lookup (DNS)
dns-lookup.purpose: A portable DNS lookup for the field. Resolve a name as an all-records view (SOA, NS, A, AAAA, MX, TXT, SRV, and CAA at once), as a single record type, or run a one-tap reverse lookup on an IP.
dns-lookup.whyHere: Checks records authoritatively rather than from your local cache, and works through captive portals and corporate firewalls. The all-records view is the fast "show me everything" pass; single-type is the focused look; reverse lookup turns an IP back into a hostname without flipping any selector.
howToUse: Enter a hostname (or an IP). Leave the mode on All records for the full sweep, then look up.
For one record type, switch to Single type and pick A, AAAA, MX, TXT, NS, SOA, PTR, SRV, CAA, or SPF.
When the input is an IP, a Reverse lookup (PTR) button appears for a one-tap IP-to-hostname query.

Pick the resolver (Cloudflare default, Google, or Quad9) if one provider is blocked. Quad9 (9.9.9.9) filters known-malicious domains, so a name that resolves on Cloudflare or Google but comes back empty on Quad9 is flagged as a threat.
dns-lookup.algorithm: Lookups ride secure DNS over HTTPS rather than the old plain DNS port. That's the deliberate choice: plain DNS is often blocked (and gated on iOS), while DNS over HTTPS travels on the same secure web port that works almost everywhere. In All records mode it asks for each record type at once and groups the answers in a readable order; if one type fails, that becomes a small note for that section while everything else still shows. A reverse lookup rewrites the IP into its reverse-lookup name before querying. SPF isn't a separate query: it reads the TXT records and picks out the SPF policy line (per RFC 7208). Three resolvers are selectable: Cloudflare, Google, and Quad9 (9.9.9.9), all over HTTPS with no key needed, and all returning the same record shape. Quad9 is a security resolver that refuses known-malicious domains, so a blocked name comes back as an honest empty result on Quad9, not an error. Every query lands in one of three clearly separated states: success, empty (resolved fine, but no records of that type), or failure.
fieldNotes: Records resolve against a public resolver (Cloudflare, Google, or Quad9), not the resolver your device is configured to use, which is usually what a pro wants: authoritative and uncached. Your device's configured DNS servers are shown by Interface Information instead.

Quad9 (9.9.9.9) blocks domains on its threat-intelligence lists, so an empty result on Quad9 for a name that resolves on Cloudflare or Google means the domain is flagged as malicious, a fast malware check rather than a lookup failure. Use Cloudflare or Google when you want an unfiltered answer.
SRV and CAA records are parsed into readable fields; anything it can't parse is shown raw. Empty isn't an error: a name with no MX record simply returns the empty state.
Platform differences: identical on iOS, Android, macOS, and Windows. Turned off on the web version, in keeping with the native-only product decision.

A full delegation trace (walking from the root down to the authoritative servers) isn't built. DNS over HTTPS only reaches resolvers that hand back the final answer, so a true iterative trace would need lower-level plumbing that's heavier and less reliable in the field than this tool is meant to be.
dns-lookup.source: Resolves names over secure DNS over HTTPS against your choice of Cloudflare, Google, or Quad9.

port-scan.name: Port Scan
port-scan.purpose: A TCP connect scan of a host, either a common-ports preset or a custom range, reporting each port as open, closed, or filtered.
port-scan.whyHere: A privilege-free port scan for the field. Reach for it to see what services a host is exposing.
howToUse: Enter a host, pick the common-ports preset or type a custom spec (for example 22, 80, 443, 8000-8100), and run. Results stream in as ports settle.
port-scan.algorithm: For each port, the tool tries to open a connection with an 800ms default timeout. Open means the connection completed. Closed means the host actively refused it, which tells you the host is reachable and simply has nothing listening on that port. Filtered means nothing came back at all before the timeout: the request was silently dropped, or the host isn't there. That's the same open/closed/filtered result a full connect scan gives. Connections run in a capped pool (up to 64 at a time) and stream in as they finish. The common-ports preset is 44 ports a network pro actually checks (from 20 up to 27017, each labeled with its service, for example 443 as HTTPS and 3389 as RDP).
fieldNotes: Platform differences: works the same on every native platform (no special permission beyond ordinary network access). Turned off on the web version.
"Filtered" means the request went unanswered (likely a firewall); it does not mean the port is closed. A custom range is de-duplicated and kept within the valid 1 to 65535 range.

"Closed" is a positive signal: it means the host ANSWERED and refused. A host that isn't there reads as all-filtered, never all-closed. If every port comes back filtered, suspect the host is down or firewalled wholesale rather than idle. The copied report spells this legend out.
This is a TCP connect scan, not a stealth scan; it completes each connection and then tears it down.
port-scan.source: Tries to open a TCP connection to each port and reports whether it opened, was actively refused, or went unanswered.

ping.name: Ping (TCP)
ping.purpose: A reachability and round-trip-time probe that works on every platform, including the sandboxed desktop, by timing a TCP connection rather than a classic ICMP echo.
ping.whyHere: The portable ping. Classic ICMP ping is often blocked while a TCP port (443) still answers; this is the same approach pros already use with tools like tcping. It's also the way to ping on the desktop, where real ICMP can't run.
howToUse: Enter a host, optionally pick a probe port (443 default; presets 443, 80, 53, 22, 7) and a count, and run. Live min/avg/max/loss and a sparkline build as replies land.
ping.algorithm: Each "ping" times how long it takes to open a connection to the host on the chosen port (443 by default). Either a completed connection OR an active refusal counts as a successful round trip for timing (exactly how tcping treats it, because the host answered either way). A timeout, an unreachable host, or a failed name lookup counts as loss, and no round-trip time is recorded for it. Probes are spaced by your chosen interval minus the time the last probe took, so the cadence stays steady even under high latency.
fieldNotes: Platform differences: identical on every native platform. Turned off on the web version. The screen labels the metric "TCP RTT" and shows the target port, so it's never mistaken for classic ICMP ping.

The round-trip time is the connection time to a port, slightly higher than classic ping, and it depends on that port answering. Even a "refused" target gives you a valid timing number, because the host answered.
A host that never answers is reported as loss with no round-trip time. The tool never invents an RTT for a packet it did not get back.
This is not classic ICMP echo (see Ping (ICMP) for the real-ICMP path on mobile). If a host blocks the probe port, it reads as loss even if the host is up on another port; try a different probe port.
The copied report carries a Method line defining what counts as a reply (a completed handshake or an active refusal), so a pasted result can't be misread by someone who never saw the screen.
ping.source: Times how long it takes to open a TCP connection to the host, and reports that as the round-trip time.

ping-plotter.name: Ping Plotter
ping-plotter.purpose: Pings a target over and over and charts the round-trip time as it happens, instead of the single answer the Ping and ICMP Ping tools give. The live line, the jitter reading, and any dropped probes show you how steady a connection really is, not just whether it answered once.
ping-plotter.whyHere: This is the live performance graph. A single ping tells you a host is reachable right now; a trend tells you whether the path is steady, spiky, or losing packets. Reach for this to watch a flaky link over seconds or minutes, something a single ping can't show you.
howToUse: Enter a host, and if you like, pick a probe port (443 by default; you can also choose 443, 80, 53, 22, or 7) and how often to sample (every 0.5, 1, 2, or 5 seconds). Then tap Start plot.

The chart fills in from left to right as replies come back: a lime line for round-trip time and a red dot on the axis for any lost probe. The readout above shows the current, minimum, average, maximum, and jitter figures, plus the percentage of probes lost.
It keeps running until you tap Stop. The chart holds the most recent samples so a long run stays a fixed size. Copy exports the summary plus a sample-by-sample table.
ping-plotter.algorithm: It sends one probe every interval you choose and keeps going until you stop it. Each reply is added to a rolling window of the most recent samples (60 by default). The minimum, average, and maximum come from the round-trip times that came back within that window. Jitter is the average size of the change from one reply to the next, so a lost probe breaks the chain and jitter never measures across a gap. Loss percentage is simply lost probes divided by probes sent. A probe that times out or can't be reached is recorded as an honest gap with no time and drawn as a red dot on the axis, never as a made-up 0 ms.
fieldNotes: This is a TCP round-trip probe, not an ICMP echo. The reading is labeled "TCP RTT" and the probe port is shown, so you can't mistake it for ICMP. For true ICMP, see Ping (ICMP) on mobile.
It runs on every native platform, including the Mac desktop where true ICMP can't run. It's turned off on the web version, which shows a prompt to download the app.
Dropped probes are always shown, never hidden. A lost sample is a red dot on the axis and counts toward the loss percentage, so a flaky path reads honestly instead of as a smooth line.
The chart keeps a fixed number of recent samples so a long run stays light on memory, and the copy export notes how many of the total samples are shown.
The copied report carries a Method line defining what counts as a reply (a completed handshake or an active refusal), so a pasted result can't be misread by someone who never saw the screen.
ping-plotter.source: It repeatedly sends TCP round-trip probes to the host and charts each reply as it arrives.

icmp-ping.name: Ping (ICMP)
icmp-ping.purpose: True ICMP echo round-trip on mobile: live round-trip time, minimum/average/maximum, and packet loss.
icmp-ping.whyHere: For when you specifically want a genuine ICMP echo, the classic ping, on platforms that really support it.
howToUse: Enter a host and run it (mobile only). It streams replies with running stats, in the same layout as the TCP Ping tool.
icmp-ping.algorithm: It sends a real ICMP echo request and waits for the echo reply, using the phone's own ping capability. The result is genuine ICMP timing, always labeled "ICMP echo" and never a TCP probe dressed up as one.
fieldNotes: Where it works, honestly: iOS and Android both do real ICMP echo. On macOS and Windows the only way to run ICMP is the system ping program, which the Mac App Sandbox blocks, so it gives an honest "not available in the sandboxed desktop build" and points you at TCP Ping instead. Coming soon to the WLAN Pi platform. The web version has no way to do this and offers the download instead.
Where it's available, this is genuine ICMP timing and loss, the real thing, not a TCP stand-in.

Device-pending note: the iOS ICMP path is verified with a stand-in during testing but can't be fully confirmed without a real device, so the live round-trip is the piece still pending device verification. On desktop, use TCP Ping.
icmp-ping.source: It sends genuine ICMP echo requests using the phone's built-in ping capability.

ping-sweep.name: Ping Sweep
ping-sweep.purpose: Find which hosts on a subnet respond, using a TCP probe (no ICMP).
ping-sweep.whyHere: A quick "who's on this segment" without needing special access. Reach for it to list the live hosts on a /24.
howToUse: Enter a range: a CIDR block (192.168.1.0/24), a range (192.168.1.10-40, or with a full end address), or a single IP. Then run it. A live progress bar and a running count of responsive hosts build as it goes.
ping-sweep.algorithm: For each address it tries to open a TCP connection to a common port (443 by default; it can also try 80, 22, and 53, and a host counts as responsive the instant any of them answers). A host is listed when it ANSWERS: either the connection completes, or the host actively refuses it (a refusal is a reply, and proves something is there). Silence is not an answer, so a host that times out, is unreachable, or is simply not there is NOT listed and is NOT counted as live. It works through a batch of hosts at a time (32 in flight by default) to stay fast. It's capped at 254 hosts (a /24); anything larger is rejected with a clear "that's N hosts, the cap is M" message rather than being silently cut short.
fieldNotes: It works the same on every native platform and is turned off on the web version.
A host is reported as "responded," not "up." A host that stays silent on the probed ports may still be alive but only answering ICMP, or behind a firewall. The tool never claims ICMP-style liveness.

A refused connection still counts as responded, and that is deliberate: the host answered, it just isn't listening on that port. That is how tcping behaves too. The list and the copied report say which it was ("handshake" or "refused"), so a middlebox refusing on behalf of a whole range can't be mistaken for a range full of live servers.

This finds hosts that answer TCP on the probed ports. For richer detail (name, services, device type, vendor), use Network Discovery. A /31 or /32 includes every address; larger blocks leave out the network and broadcast addresses.
ping-sweep.source: It tries a quick TCP connection to a common port on each address and lists only the ones that answer, whether they accept the connection or refuse it.

network-discovery.name: Network Discovery
network-discovery.purpose: Find the live hosts on your local network and add detail to each: name, services, a best guess at device type, and, where it's exposed, the MAC address and vendor.
network-discovery.whyHere: A Fing-style LAN scan. Reach for it when you want a real inventory rather than a bare ping sweep: what each host is, not just that it answered.
howToUse: Open it and run. It works out your local network range, scans it for live hosts, looks up their names, browses for advertised services, and (on macOS) reads the address table for MAC and vendor.
network-discovery.algorithm: It works in four passes. First it figures out your local /24 range. Second it scans that range across a curated set of common ports, a batch at a time, streaming progress as it goes. Third it does a reverse name lookup on each host it finds (blank when the host has no name registered). Fourth it browses for advertised network services (the same technology behind AirPlay and printer discovery) across a curated service list. On macOS it then reads the system address table to attach a MAC address and vendor. Finally it makes a best guess at each device's type from its open ports and advertised services. The type rules go most-specific first: printing ports or a print service means Printer; a streaming-video service means Camera or NVR; a certain Apple service on port 62078 means an iOS device; file sharing on 445 means a Windows or SMB machine; music-speaker services mean Speaker; a casting service means a media streamer; AirPlay-family services mean an Apple device; then weaker hints, web ports mean a web server and port 22 means an SSH host; any advertised service otherwise means an mDNS device; and everything else is Unknown. The vendor comes from matching the MAC address prefix against the full built-in IEEE vendor registry, which stays honest: blank for randomized or local addresses, the raw prefix when the vendor isn't listed, and the named vendor otherwise.
fieldNotes: The platform differences are the heart of it. MAC address and vendor are macOS only, because reading the address table isn't allowed on iOS or Android, so those stay blank there. Service browsing works on iOS and macOS through Apple's built-in discovery; it deliberately avoids methods that iOS quietly drops or that carry a license incompatible with the app, and on Android and other platforms the service pass simply comes back empty. The core host scan is cross-platform; only the service and MAC enrichment are platform-specific.

Device type is a best guess from ports and advertised services, not a lookup in a hardware database, so Unknown is a normal, honest outcome. There's deliberately no "access point" guess, because the only reliable way to spot an access point is the vendor from its MAC address, which mobile can't read.

Any single pass can fail without stopping the run; a failed service browse just means no service detail, and nothing is invented to fill the gap. On mobile, expect no MAC or vendor, and access points to show up as SSH, Web, or Unknown, a known limit of the platform.
network-discovery.source: It scans the local network for live hosts, then looks up names, advertised services, device type, and (on macOS) MAC and vendor.

traceroute.name: Traceroute (System)
traceroute.purpose: Maps the path to a host hop by hop, using the computer's built-in traceroute (desktop).
traceroute.whyHere: The genuine traceroute, where it can actually run. Reach for it on a Mac or PC to see the routed path and where latency or loss creeps in.
howToUse: Desktop only. Enter a host and run it; hops fill in live as they come back. You can cancel partway through.
traceroute.algorithm: It runs the computer's own traceroute program and reads each hop as it appears: the hop number, the responding router's name and IP, the round-trip times for each probe, and any timeouts shown as three asterisks. A real traceroute has to read a specific kind of reply from each router along the way, which needs privileged access the built-in program has, so this tool refuses to fake hops from ordinary TCP timing.
fieldNotes: Where it works, honestly: on macOS and Windows it runs the built-in traceroute. Under the Mac App Sandbox (the App Store build) launching that program is blocked, so the tool quietly tests whether it can run and adapts: a Developer-ID Mac build and Windows run it fine, while the sandboxed build shows a clear "traceroute unavailable" message rather than hanging or pretending. On iOS and Android, running an external program isn't allowed, so it says "Traceroute runs on desktop, use Ping here." Coming soon to the WLAN Pi platform. The web version never reaches this tool.

Each hop shows its number, the responding router (name and IP), and up to three round-trip times. Three asterisks mean a hop that didn't answer, which is common and not necessarily a problem. Reaching the target is reported as "complete."
On a sandboxed Mac App Store build this tool honestly reports itself unavailable. For a path check on mobile, see Traceroute (Mobile), and note its iOS limitation.
traceroute.source: It runs the computer's built-in traceroute and reads each hop as it comes back.

mobile-traceroute.name: Traceroute (Mobile)
mobile-traceroute.purpose: Maps the path to a host hop by hop using an ICMP TTL-walk, Android only (not supported on iOS).
mobile-traceroute.whyHere: Brings traceroute to mobile where the platform genuinely supports it, built on the same ICMP foundation as Ping (ICMP).
howToUse: Android: enter a host and run it. Hops fill in one at a time as it steadily increases how far each probe travels, revealing the router that answers at each step.
mobile-traceroute.algorithm: It sends ICMP echoes that are allowed to travel one hop, then two, then three, and so on. Each router that stops a probe short reports back and names that hop, and when the target itself replies, the walk ends.
fieldNotes: The platform picture, honestly: on Android it works, because the system ping lets each probe travel a set distance and prints the router that answers each step. On iOS it isn't feasible and is honestly marked unavailable. iOS can send echoes, but its receive path only accepts the plain echo reply and never reads the "hop exceeded" message a traceroute needs to name each router, so a short-travel probe just times out with no hop. Rather than fake hops, iOS shows an honest "not on this device." On desktop, the system traceroute is the path to use; this mobile version reports itself as desktop-sandboxed there.
Where it works (Android), hops show the same hop-number, IP, round-trip-time, and asterisk-timeout shape as the desktop traceroute.
The iOS limit is a real platform ceiling and is the reason this tool is Android-only. Device-pending note: the Android path is still pending verification on a real device.
mobile-traceroute.source: On Android it sends ICMP echoes that travel one hop farther each time, naming the router that answers at each step.

ssl-inspect.name: Inspector (SSL/TLS)
ssl-inspect.purpose: Connects to a host and port over TLS and shows you the server's certificate in full, including expired, self-signed, and name-mismatch certificates.
ssl-inspect.whyHere: A field certificate inspector. Reach for it to read validity, the covered names, fingerprints, key size, and the issuer on any TLS service, especially broken ones.
howToUse: Enter a host (URLs are accepted and trimmed to just the host), an optional port (443 by default), and inspect.
ssl-inspect.algorithm: It deliberately accepts any certificate the server presents, so an expired, self-signed, or wrong-name certificate is still captured and shown to you rather than rejected. It then reads the certificate dates and tells you whether it's currently valid, expired, or not yet valid, along with the days left until it expires. Between two ways of reading the certificate it recovers the full picture: the PEM and DER forms, the subject and issuer, the valid-from and valid-to dates, the covered names, the serial number, the signature and public-key algorithms, the key size in bits, and both the SHA-1 and SHA-256 fingerprints. A bad certificate counts as a successful inspection, not a failure.
fieldNotes: It works the same on every native platform and is turned off on the web version.

Validity is shown with an icon and text, not color alone. Two honest limits are stated on screen rather than papered over. First, the ALPN result is not the TLS version or cipher, and because the platform only exposes ALPN, the negotiated version and cipher are reported as "not exposed by the platform" instead of being invented. Second, this shows the leaf certificate only, not the intermediate and root certificates it was checked against.
Connection problems (name lookup, refused, timeout) are failures; an invalid certificate is not. Don't read the ALPN line as the TLS version; the tool keeps them separate on purpose.
ssl-inspect.source: It opens a TLS connection to the host and port, accepts whatever certificate is offered, and reads it back for you.

http-headers.name: Inspector (HTTP Header)
http-headers.purpose: Sends a HEAD or GET request, follows the redirect chain and records each step, and returns the final status along with all the response headers.
http-headers.whyHere: Shows the full 301 to 302 to 200 story, not just the destination. Reach for it to debug redirects, security headers, caching, or CDN behavior.
howToUse: Enter a URL (a bare host is assumed to be https://), pick HEAD (the default) or GET, and inspect. Each step is shown with its status, its Location header, and its headers.
http-headers.algorithm: It follows the redirect chain itself, recording one step for every response until it hits a non-redirect status or the 10-redirect limit. If a HEAD request comes back as 405 (method not allowed) and you didn't specifically ask for GET, it quietly retries that step with GET and notes the switch. Relative Location values are resolved to full URLs per the HTTP standard.
fieldNotes: Platform note: on iOS, cleartext http:// is blocked by App Transport Security, and the app deliberately doesn't add a blanket exception, since that would weaken every request. So an http:// target fails on iOS and the tool shows a specific message ("On iOS, cleartext HTTP is blocked by ATS, try the https:// URL") rather than a generic failure. Otherwise it's the same on native and turned off on the web version.
The step chain reads top (the first request) to bottom (the final response). It flags when HEAD fell back to GET and when the redirect limit was hit. Header names are shown in title case and sorted.
Response bodies are read and thrown away; this inspects headers, it doesn't fetch pages. On iOS, use the https:// URL.
http-headers.source: It sends a HEAD or GET request, follows each redirect itself, and reports every step's status and headers.

whois.name: WHOIS
whois.purpose: Looks up the registration details for a domain or IP over WHOIS (TCP port 43), with the key facts pulled out plus the full raw record.
whois.whyHere: Registrar, dates, status, and name servers for a domain or IP, right from the field. Reach for it on ownership or expiry questions.
howToUse: Enter a domain or IP and look it up.
whois.algorithm: It queries WHOIS directly over the network on port 43. It follows the usual two-step chain: it asks the master directory who's in charge of the target, reads the referral to the authoritative registry, queries that registry, and follows one further step to the registrar's own WHOIS server when that returns a fuller record. The highlights (registrar, created, updated, and expiry dates, status, and name servers) are pulled from the free-form record wherever they're reliably present; anything missing is left out rather than guessed.
fieldNotes: It works the same on every native platform and is turned off on the web version.

There are three outcomes: success (the raw record plus highlights), empty (the server answered but the domain is unregistered or returned a "No match" notice), and failure (a connection problem, timeout, or bad input). The servers it consulted are listed so the path is clear.
WHOIS output varies by registry and is free-form, so the highlights are a best effort. The raw record is the source of truth.
whois.source: It queries WHOIS servers directly over the network, following the referral chain to the authoritative registry.

wake-on-lan.name: Wake-on-LAN
wake-on-lan.purpose: Sends a Wake-on-LAN magic packet to wake a host by its MAC address.
wake-on-lan.whyHere: Wake a sleeping machine on the LAN from your phone or laptop.
howToUse: Enter the target MAC address (colons, hyphens, Cisco dot-notation, or no separators all work), and optionally a subnet-directed broadcast address (for example 192.168.1.255) and a port (9 by default, 7 as an alternative). Then send.
wake-on-lan.algorithm: It builds the standard 102-byte magic packet (six 0xFF bytes followed by the target's 6-byte MAC address repeated 16 times) and sends it as a UDP broadcast. It first tidies the MAC into standard form, and rejects an invalid MAC, a bad broadcast address, or an out-of-range port with a clear message.
fieldNotes: It works on every native platform and is turned off on the web version.

Success means the packet was sent; it makes no claim that the device woke. Wake-on-LAN gets no acknowledgment, a switch may not carry the broadcast across subnets, and the target may have Wake-on-LAN turned off. The tool shows the bytes sent and the packet in hex.
If the system reports 0 bytes sent, try a directed broadcast (for example 192.168.1.255) instead of 255.255.255.255. "Sent" is not the same as "woke"; check the host separately.
wake-on-lan.source: It builds the standard Wake-on-LAN magic packet for the target MAC and sends it as a UDP broadcast.

arp-ndp.name: Lookup (ARP/NDP)
arp-ndp.purpose: Discover the devices sharing your local network: their IP addresses, and their hardware (MAC) address where your platform allows it.
arp-ndp.whyHere: A quick read of who else is on your local network. Reach for it to match IP addresses to hardware addresses on the platforms that support it, or simply to list the devices that answer.
howToUse: Open it and run it. It works out your local network range, checks each address, and attaches the known hardware address wherever the platform makes one available.
arp-ndp.algorithm: It figures out your local network range (usually a /24, or the real one if different) and quickly checks each address by trying to open a connection on a handful of common ports (80, 443, 22, 445, 139, 53, 8080). A device counts as present when it ANSWERS, whether it accepts the connection or actively refuses it. An address that stays silent, times out, or is unreachable is not listed. On macOS and Windows it also reads the system's own neighbour table to attach the real hardware (MAC) address the system has already learned: macOS through a native system call, Windows through the Windows IP Helper API. Neither shells out to a command line. As a safety limit, it refuses to scan anything larger than about 1,000 addresses.
fieldNotes: What each platform can do: macOS and Windows give you the device list plus the real hardware address, read from the system's own neighbour table. iOS and Android give you the device list without hardware addresses, because those systems do not let apps read that table. On web it offers a download fallback.

A missing MAC always tells you WHICH kind of missing it is, and never a guessed value. "Not exposed on this platform" means this platform has no way to read the table at all. "MAC read failed" means the read was tried here and did not work. "Not in the ARP cache" means the read worked and simply had no entry for that device. Empty or all-zero entries are skipped.
Only addresses that answer are listed. A silent address is left out rather than guessed at, so the list is what replied, not what might be there.

Hardware addresses come from the system's neighbour table on macOS and Windows. The Windows path is implemented but has not yet been confirmed on real hardware; if it does not work, the tool reports a failed read rather than a platform limit. For richer detail, including device-type and vendor, use Network Discovery.
arp-ndp.source: It quietly checks each address on your local network and, on macOS and Windows, reads the system's learned neighbour-address table to fill in the MAC.

bgp-asn.name: Lookup (BGP/ASN)
bgp-asn.purpose: For any IP address or network number, shows the network operator, the announced address block, the regional registry, and how many peers and upstream providers it connects through.
bgp-asn.whyHere: Routing-level context: who announces a block of addresses, which regional registry it belongs to, and how it connects to the rest of the internet. Reach for it on questions about an upstream provider or ISP.
howToUse: Enter an IPv4 or IPv6 address, or a network number (AS15169, 15169, or as15169), then look it up.
bgp-asn.algorithm: It looks the answer up through RIPEstat, a free public data service run by RIPE NCC (one of the internet's regional registries). For an IP address it finds the announced block and the network it belongs to, then the operator's name, type, registry, and announced prefixes. For a network number it finds that operator's details and its neighbors, sorted into upstream, peer, and downstream counts. It works out whether you entered an address or a network number before it looks anything up.
fieldNotes: Platform note: this one runs on the native apps only. On web it offers a download fallback instead.

Three outcomes: a clean result; an empty result (the service answered but the address is not in the global routing table, for example a private or reserved IP); or a failure. Peer and upstream counts are best-effort extras, so if that part cannot be fetched they show "Not available" rather than zero.
Any detail the service leaves out shows as "Not available", never invented. Neighbor roles map as: upstream, downstream, or peer.
bgp-asn.source: It looks the details up through RIPEstat, a free public routing-data service run by the RIPE NCC internet registry.

ip-geo.name: IP Geolocation
ip-geo.purpose: For any IP address (or your own public one), shows the country, region, city, coordinates, timezone, the ISP or organization, and the network number.
ip-geo.whyHere: A fast read on where an address appears to be and who runs it. Reach for it to see where an IP resolves and which provider owns it.
howToUse: Enter an IP address or hostname, or leave it blank to locate your own public IP, then look it up. You get a copyable "latitude,longitude" pair and an OpenStreetMap link.
ip-geo.algorithm: It looks the answer up through ipwho.is, a free public geolocation service that returns location, provider, timezone, and network number in one response. Leaving the box blank locates your own public IP; otherwise it locates the address you enter. It does a quick sanity check on your input before spending a lookup, and if the service reports a problem it tells you plainly whether to check your input or wait out a rate limit, rather than treating the reply as valid.
fieldNotes: Platform note: this one runs on the native apps only. On web it offers a download fallback instead.
Coordinates appear as selectable text plus a copyable pair and a link out to a map (there is no embedded map yet). Any missing detail shows as "Not available".
Accuracy is only as good as the data provider's, which is looser for mobile and carrier-shared (CGNAT) addresses. If you hit a rate limit, it says so plainly ("wait a minute and try again").
ip-geo.source: It looks the location up through ipwho.is, a free public IP-geolocation service.

my-current-location.name: Current Location
my-current-location.purpose: Reads your device's GPS location as soon as you open it and shows latitude, longitude, altitude, and horizontal accuracy directly, with no conversion step.
my-current-location.whyHere: The fast "where am I right now" answer. You can get the same fix inside the Lat / Long converter, but most people want a dedicated location tool rather than a coordinate calculator.
howToUse: Open the tool. It asks for Location permission if needed and reads one fix automatically.
Read latitude and longitude (decimal degrees), altitude (meters), and horizontal accuracy (meters).
Tap Update to take a fresh reading. Grant Location, or open Settings, if it prompts you.
my-current-location.algorithm: It uses the same location reader as the Lat / Long tool, asking for the best available accuracy and giving up after 15 seconds. First it checks whether Location is permitted and turned on, then shows the matching state: your fix if granted, a permission request if needed, a shortcut to Settings if blocked, or an honest "unavailable" if no fix comes back. If your device genuinely can't get a satellite fix but Location is on, it falls back to a rough location worked out from your public IP, clearly labeled approximate and never presented as GPS.
my-current-location.example: Granted and outdoors: 40.712800, -74.006000, altitude 12 m, accuracy 5 m.
fieldNotes: Altitude and accuracy can read "Not reported" on hardware without GPS, for example a Mac working from a coarse Wi-Fi fix.
A fallback fix worked out from your public IP is labeled as such and is city-level, not GPS-precise.
Nothing about your location is sent out; the IP-based fallback only reads your public IP.
my-current-location.source: It reads your device's built-in location sensor, and if that can't get a fix it falls back to a rough position based on your public IP, clearly labeled approximate.

mac-oui-lookup.name: MAC Vendor OUI Lookup
mac-oui-lookup.purpose: Turn a MAC (hardware) address into the manufacturer that registered it, fully offline.
mac-oui-lookup.whyHere: Identify a device's maker from its MAC address with no network connection, and, just as usefully, it tells you honestly when a MAC has no real vendor at all (the case for privacy-randomized phone addresses).
howToUse: Enter a MAC address in any common form (colons, hyphens, Cisco dotted aabb.ccdd.eeff, or no separators, any capitalization), then look it up.
mac-oui-lookup.algorithm: It matches your address against a built-in copy of the official IEEE vendor registry, loaded once and kept ready, with no network needed. The registry hands out address blocks in three sizes, and this checks the most specific first: the smallest block, then the medium, then the largest. That way a small sub-assigned block names the real company it was given to, not just the larger parent block.
fieldNotes: This works identically everywhere, since it is fully offline. No platform gives you more or less.

The honest cases: a locally-administered or randomized address (the norm for modern iPhone and Android Wi-Fi) is flagged with no vendor guessed, because any registry match would be pure coincidence. A multicast or group address (not a single device) is also flagged with no vendor. A normal address that simply is not in the built-in copy shows its raw manufacturer prefix (for example B8:27:EB) rather than an invented name.
A randomized phone MAC correctly comes back as "no vendor", which is the right answer, not a miss. The built-in registry lists the date it was captured; refresh it to pick up newer assignments.
CATALOG NOTE: manual category is Networking Tools; catalog id is mac-oui-lookup with route /tools/mac-oui.
mac-oui-lookup.source: It matches the address against a built-in copy of the official IEEE vendor registry, entirely offline.

packet-sender.name: Packet Sender
packet-sender.purpose: Send a custom TCP or UDP message to a host and port, then read whatever comes back (raw bytes shown as hex and as decoded text).
packet-sender.whyHere: A pocket "netcat" for poking at a service: grabbing banners, sending custom probes, testing how a service responds. Reach for it to send a crafted message and see the reply.
howToUse: Enter the host, port, transport (TCP or UDP), and a message (plain text, or hex escapes like \xNN plus \r \n \t \0 \\), then send. The reply appears as hex and as decoded text.
packet-sender.algorithm: It uses ordinary TCP and UDP connections, not low-level raw packets, so it runs cleanly on every platform. Over TCP it connects, sends your message, and reads the reply until the other end closes or goes quiet for the timeout, with a hard overall cap so a chatty stream can't run forever. Over UDP it sends one message and waits up to the timeout for a reply, and getting no reply is treated as a normal outcome rather than an error, because UDP offers no delivery guarantee. Problems are reported precisely: name-lookup failure, connection refused, unreachable, timeout, or invalid input.
fieldNotes: This works the same on every native platform. It is turned off on web.
A UDP "no reply" is honest and expected for many services; it does not mean failure. The message box only rejects your input if a hex escape is written incorrectly, which is worth flagging as a typo.
Binary replies still display, using a placeholder character for bytes that aren't readable text, rather than failing. This is a single send-and-receive, not an ongoing session.
packet-sender.source: It opens an ordinary TCP or UDP connection to the host you name, sends your message, and shows the reply.

ntp-time.name: Time Server (NTP)
ntp-time.purpose: Ask a time server for the exact time and report the server's time, how far off your device's clock is (with direction), and the network round-trip delay.
ntp-time.whyHere: A wrong device clock quietly breaks TLS certificate checks, Kerberos, and 802.1X/EAP, and it throws packet-capture timestamps out of sync between machines. Reach for this to confirm your laptop or phone agrees with a trusted time source, and by how much, before you trust a timestamp or chase down a certificate error.
howToUse: Keep the default server (time.apple.com) or enter another (for example pool.ntp.org), then tap Check time.
Read the Clock offset row: the difference in milliseconds, with a sign, plus a plain verdict (in sync, behind, or ahead). A positive offset means your device is behind the server.
Round-trip delay is how long the request took to go out and come back; a large delay makes the offset less precise.
ntp-time.algorithm: It sends a small standard time request out to the server (on the usual time port, 123) and times the exchange. It notes the moment it sends and the moment the reply arrives, reads the two timestamps the server records for when it received and sent the reply, and uses those four moments to work out both your clock's offset and the network delay. In plain terms, the offset averages how far ahead or behind the server looked on the way there and on the way back, and the delay is the total round-trip time minus the time the server spent turning the request around. It uses a real time-protocol request rather than a web time page, because only this measures the round trip the calculation needs.
ntp-time.example: time.apple.com, stratum 1: server 2026-06-21 14:30:00.120 UTC, clock offset +42 ms (your clock is 42 ms behind), round-trip delay 18 ms.
fieldNotes: Platform note: works on every native app (it only needs to reach a remote server, and since that server is not on your local network, iOS needs no local-network permission). It is turned off on web, where this kind of connection isn't available.

Honesty: a timeout, a name-lookup failure, a reply that is too short, or a server reporting that it is itself unsynchronized all show as a clear error, never a made-up offset or a faked delay. A tiny negative round-trip time caused by clock jitter is rounded up to 0.

Offsets within plus or minus 50 ms read as "in sync"; more than 2 seconds is flagged as a real problem (it breaks TLS, Kerberos, and EAP), and smaller skews as a heads-up. The offset is only as precise as the round trip is even, so a jittery or slow path widens the uncertainty. Nothing is sent anywhere except to the time server you chose, and nothing is stored.

Stratum tells you how close the server is to a reference clock: 1 is a primary source synced to a reference clock, 2 is one hop away, 3 through 15 are each a further hop, and 16 means unsynchronized. See the Date & Time Standards reference for the full stratum table.
ntp-time.source: It sends a standard time request to the server you choose and times the round trip to work out both your clock offset and the network delay.

cli-commands.name: Network CLI Commands
cli-commands.purpose: A side-by-side Windows / macOS / Linux command reference for everyday network-troubleshooting tasks (Wi-Fi link state, reachability, path tracing, DNS, interface setup, DHCP, sockets, ARP, routing), each with its field-common flags, plus a Linux-only shell-essentials group for capture-rig and WLAN Pi work.
cli-commands.whyHere: You are at a client site on whatever laptop is in front of you and need the right command for this operating system without looking it up: "what's the macOS equivalent of ipconfig /all," "how do I renew DHCP on Linux," "how do I see the connected SSID, BSSID, and RSSI from the command line."
howToUse: One card per task. Each card shows the Windows command (in lime), the macOS command, and the Linux command on separate lines, a one-line description, and a short set of common flags.
macOS and Linux are shown as separate columns because they differ on several tasks (ifconfig vs ip, netstat vs ss, DHCP renew, DNS flush); where they match, the same command appears on both lines.

Filter by command name or task (for example "ping" or "DNS"). The Linux-only shell essentials (ls, grep, tail, screen, chmod) appear in their own group at the end. Where a platform has no native command, the card says so plainly rather than leaving a blank. Tap the copy action in the top bar to copy the whole sheet.
cli-commands.example: Representative rows (Task | Windows | macOS | Linux): Connected Wi-Fi link state | netsh wlan show interfaces | wdutil info | iw dev wlan0 link. Interface IP config | ipconfig /all | ifconfig | ip addr. Renew DHCP | ipconfig /renew | sudo ipconfig set en0 DHCP | sudo dhclient -r && sudo dhclient. Flush DNS | ipconfig /flushdns | sudo dscacheutil -flushcache; sudo killall -HUP mDNSResponder | sudo resolvectl flush-caches. Active connections | netstat -ano | netstat -an | ss -tunap. Routing table | route print | netstat -rn | ip route. DNS full detail | (no native command) | dig name | dig name. Linux shell essentials: ls -lah, tail -f file, grep -i pattern file, screen, chmod 755 file.
fieldNotes: Three-column layout: macOS and Linux are kept separate so a command that differs between them is never collapsed into a single wrong "macOS/Linux" value. The macOS Wi-Fi entry shows only wdutil info; the retired airport command is removed entirely.

Older Linux forms (ifconfig, route, arp, iwconfig, netstat) are noted alongside the current forms (ip addr, ip route, ip neigh, iw, ss) so you can read older documentation and still write modern commands. netsh wlan is Windows only; dig, whois, and nbtstat honestly show "no native command" where that is the case.

These are reference text. The Toolbox does not run them; it shows the correct syntax for you to run yourself. Some need administrator or sudo rights, and the flags shown are the common field subset, not the full list.

Basis: consolidated and de-duplicated from Keith's Network CLI sheet and the WLAN Pros Linux command cheat sheets, checked against current Windows, macOS, and Linux documentation. Retired commands were replaced with their current equivalents rather than carried forward.
cli-commands.source: A curated reference built from Keith's Network CLI sheet and the WLAN Pros Linux cheat sheets, checked against current Windows, macOS, and Linux documentation.

apple-wifi-tips.name: Apple Wi-Fi Support Tips
apple-wifi-tips.purpose: Apple's official Wi-Fi support guidance in one place: the access-point settings Apple recommends for Apple devices, how to run Wireless Diagnostics on a Mac, how to read the Option-click Wi-Fi menu, and Apple's step-by-step iPhone and iPad troubleshooting.
apple-wifi-tips.whyHere: Setting up or auditing an access point for Apple clients, or a device will not connect: reach for Apple's own answer rather than a third-party blog. Each section links back to the Apple support article it came from.
howToUse: Start at Section A when setting up or auditing an access point for Apple clients. Apple's two highest-value calls are one SSID across all bands and 20 MHz channel width on 2.4 GHz.
Use Sections B and C on a Mac to read live RF data and capture a diagnostics report. Use Section D on an iPhone or iPad when a device will not connect.
Tap "Open Apple article" in any section to check the current wording on Apple's site. For the field-by-field meaning of the Option-click menu, open the companion macOS Menu-Bar Wi-Fi reference.
fieldNotes: Honesty: Apple does not publish a transmit-power recommendation, so this reference does not invent one, and it says so on screen. Every iOS step comes from a single Apple article; that single-source status is flagged on screen rather than dressed up as several sources agreeing.
The domain note (one SSID for all bands; 20 MHz on 2.4 GHz) is attributed to Keith, not to Apple.

Sources: Apple "Recommended settings for Wi-Fi routers and access points", "Use Wireless Diagnostics on your Mac", and "If you can't connect to Wi-Fi on your iPhone or iPad". All verified live 2026-06-12.
apple-wifi-tips.source: A reference drawn from Apple's own Wi-Fi support articles, each linked in-line and verified live on 2026-06-12.

macos-menubar-wifi.name: macOS Menu-Bar Wi-Fi
macos-menubar-wifi.purpose: The RF data a Wi-Fi pro can pull from a stock Mac with no extra app: the four built-in paths (Option-click menu, wdutil info, the Wireless Diagnostics app, and Shortcuts) and what every RF field means (RSSI, noise, SNR, Tx rate, BSSID, channel, width, PHY mode, MCS, NSS).
macos-menubar-wifi.whyHere: On a Mac you do not need an extra app to read live Wi-Fi RF data. This is the field reference for what each value means and which built-in path gives you which detail. The Apple Wi-Fi Tips screen links here for the per-field meaning.
howToUse: Use the Option-click Wi-Fi menu for a quick inline read of the current connection. Use sudo wdutil info for the full Wi-Fi detail block; without sudo the sensitive RF fields (RSSI, noise, MCS, BSSID) are hidden.
Open the Wireless Diagnostics app from the Option-click menu, then use its Window menu for Scan, Performance graphs, and a channel Sniffer that captures to a .pcap file.
Tap the copy action in the top bar to copy the whole reference, including the wdutil field list and the sudo note.
fieldNotes: The sudo note matters and is carried on screen: run sudo wdutil info, or RSSI, noise, MCS, and BSSID come back hidden.

The airport command is gone (retired in macOS Sonoma 14.4). This reference does not list airport as a working path, because on current macOS it is not one; use wdutil and Wireless Diagnostics instead.

RSSI is your coverage number, noise is the background floor, and RSSI minus noise is the SNR that predicts link quality. Tx rate is the negotiated link rate, not throughput. BSSID identifies the exact AP radio, which is how you track roaming.
Sources: Apple "Use Wireless Diagnostics on your Mac" plus corroborating field-availability references and the Toolbox team's on-device Shortcuts verification. Verified live 2026-06-12.
macos-menubar-wifi.source: A field reference built from Apple's Wireless Diagnostics documentation and on-device verification, verified live on 2026-06-12.

linux-wlan-commands.name: Linux / WLAN Commands
linux-wlan-commands.purpose: A grouped, offline cheat sheet of Linux commands for Wi-Fi work: file and process basics, both modern and older networking commands, the wireless-specific tools (iw, iwconfig, airmon-ng, rfkill), tested monitor-mode sequences, and the setup that lets you capture packets on a Mac without running as root.
linux-wlan-commands.whyHere: You're working on a WLAN Pi, a Linux capture box, or a survey laptop and need to put an adapter into monitor mode on a specific channel and width, or recall the exact iw wording to read the current link.
howToUse: Commands are grouped: File, Directory, Process, Network, Wireless, Monitor-mode, and macOS capture.
Search by command or by group name; matching a group name brings up the whole group.
linux-wlan-commands.example: Commands as shipped. Wireless: iw dev, iw dev wlan0 info (type/channel/mode), iw dev wlan0 link (SSID/signal/rate), iw dev wlan0 scan (sudo), iw dev wlan0 set channel 6, iw phy (PHY caps), iwconfig (legacy), iwlist wlan0 scan (legacy), rfkill list, rfkill unblock wifi. Monitor-mode: sudo airmon-ng start wlan0 (creates wlan0mon), sudo airmon-ng start wlan0 36 (monitor + channel 36), sudo airmon-ng stop wlan0mon, sudo airmon-ng check kill, sudo ifconfig wlan0 down / sudo iwconfig wlan0 mode monitor / sudo ifconfig wlan0 up (3-step), sudo iw dev wlan0 set channel 36 HT40+ (40 MHz secondary above), sudo iw dev wlan0 set channel 40 HT40- (40 MHz secondary below), sudo iwconfig wlan0 mode managed, sudo iw dev wlan0 info, lsusb, sudo dmesg, sudo ethtool -i wlan0, lsmod. macOS capture: sudo dseditgroup -o edit -a USERNAME -t user access_bpf (non-root capture), dscl . read /Groups/access_bpf (verify membership), sudo wdutil info (macOS 14+).
fieldNotes: Good to know: iwconfig, iwlist, and ifconfig are older "wireless extensions" tools. Modern Linux distributions prefer iw and the iproute2 commands. Monitor-mode sequences need sudo and an adapter and driver that support it.

A bit more detail: the older wireless extensions have been retired in favor of iw and nl80211 (both are shown here because field gear still ships the older tools). HT40+ / HT40- picks a 40 MHz channel with the second 20 MHz channel above (+) or below (-) the control channel. Adding your user to the access_bpf group lets you capture on a Mac without running as root. Replace USERNAME and wlan0 with your own user and interface.
Source: a Linux-focused list, with a small macOS capture group, cross-checked against the Linux manual pages, the iw and nl80211 documentation, and the aircrack-ng documentation.
linux-wlan-commands.source: A built-in, offline list of commands you copy and run yourself; the app runs nothing.

wireshark-80211-filters.name: Wireshark 802.11 Filters
wireshark-80211-filters.purpose: Copy-ready Wireshark filters for 802.11 analysis: display filters (typed into the filter bar after you capture) and capture filters (applied while you capture). Covers frame type and subtype, addressing, BSSID and SSID, RadioTap signal details, and RSN cipher and AKM selectors.
wireshark-80211-filters.whyHere: You have a capture open and need the exact filter to isolate deauths, beacons, a specific BSSID, or a security cipher, without guessing field names from memory.
howToUse: Filters are grouped; search the list by syntax or by task, and matching a group name brings up the whole group.
The filter text is selectable so you can copy it.
wireshark-80211-filters.example: Filters as shipped. Frame type/subtype (display): wlan.fc.type == 0 (all management), == 1 (all control), == 2 (all data); wlan.fc.type_subtype == 0 (Assoc req), 1 (Assoc resp), 2 (Reassoc req), 3 (Reassoc resp), 4 (Probe req), 5 (Probe resp), 8 (Beacon), 9 (ATIM), 10 (Disassoc), 11 (Auth), 12 (Deauth), 13 (Action), 24 (Block Ack Req), 25 (Block Ack), 26 (PS-Poll), 27 (RTS), 28 (CTS), 29 (Ack), 36 (Null data), 40 (QoS data), 44 (QoS Null). Address (display): wlan.addr == aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:ff (any field), wlan.ta, wlan.ra, wlan.sa, wlan.da. BSSID/SSID: wlan.bssid == ..., wlan.ssid == "MyNetwork", wlan.ssid contains "Guest". RadioTap: radiotap.channel.freq == 2412, radiotap.datarate >= 6, radiotap.dbm_antsignal > -70, radiotap.dbm_antnoise < -90, radiotap.channel.freq >= 2400 && < 2500 (2.4 GHz), >= 5000 && < 5900 (5 GHz), >= 5925 && <= 7125 (6 GHz). Capture filter (BPF): type mgt, type ctl, type data, type mgt subtype beacon, type mgt subtype probe-req, type mgt subtype deauth, type ctl subtype rts, type ctl subtype ack, wlan host aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:ff. RSN cipher (display): wlan.rsn.pcs.type == 4 (CCMP-128, 00-0F-AC:4), == 8 (GCMP-128, 00-0F-AC:8), == 9 (GCMP-256, 00-0F-AC:9), wlan.rsn.gcs.type == 2 (group cipher TKIP, 00-0F-AC:2). RSN AKM (display): wlan.rsn.akms.type == 1 (802.1X, 00-0F-AC:1), == 2 (PSK, 00-0F-AC:2), == 8 (SAE / WPA3-Personal, 00-0F-AC:8), == 18 (OWE, 00-0F-AC:18).
fieldNotes: Good to know: the display-filter field names match the ones Wireshark uses. Capture filters use the older "type/subtype" wording and only work when you're capturing with a RadioTap or PPI header.

A bit more detail: type_subtype is the combined value (type in the high bits, subtype in the low bits) that matches the IEEE 802.11 frame type and subtype assignments. Capture filters require capturing with a RadioTap header (monitor mode). For the full cipher and AKM number-to-name map, see the RSN groups or the WPA Security reference tool.

Worth flagging (intentional, not a defect): two deliberate corrections are baked in. (1) The RSN cipher-suite and AKM tables were rebuilt from IEEE 802.11-2020 Tables 9-149 (cipher = wlan.rsn.pcs.type / wlan.rsn.gcs.type) and 9-151 (AKM = wlan.rsn.akms.type), because the original card mislabeled cipher values as AKM. (2) The 2.4, 5, and 6 GHz band filters use documented frequency ranges as a safe fallback rather than an unverified 5 GHz flag. Band-edge detail: the 5 GHz range stops below 5900 and the 6 GHz range starts at 5925, so any center frequency in the 5900 to 5924 MHz gap falls into neither band filter. This is intentional and matches what ships.

Source: built on Wireshark's display-filter names and the libpcap capture-filter conventions, compiled from the Wireshark and RadioTap filter references, the pcap-filter documentation, and IEEE 802.11-2020.
wireshark-80211-filters.source: A built-in, offline list of filters you copy into Wireshark; the app runs nothing.

checklist-ap-install.name: How to NOT Have a Wireless Problem
checklist-ap-install.purpose: A work-through-it access point install checklist in three phases (Before, Install, After). Most of it is wired-side verification, on the premise that many "wireless" problems are not wireless problems at all.
checklist-ap-install.whyHere: You're mounting and turning up an access point and want to confirm the cabling, PoE, VLAN, DHCP, and routing path are right before you blame the radio, then document and validate afterward.
howToUse: Three phases; tap each item as you finish it, and the count at the top tracks your progress.

Opening line in the app: many wireless problems are not wireless problems. Work these checks before, during, and after the install, using a LinkSprinter, LinkRunner AT, EtherScope, or CyberScope, or just a laptop with a command window and the right commands.
checklist-ap-install.example: Before Installing Access Point: Cable meets/exceeds Cat5e specs · Total cable distance with patch cords < 100 m · PoE meets the AP's specific requirements · Check 802.3 af, at, or bt · Confirm DHCP address & VLAN · Confirm correct VLAN assignment · Confirm access or trunk port as required · Confirm default gateway · Ping default gateway · Confirm target IP addresses reachable · Confirm DNS reachable · Confirm target DNS addresses reachable · Management VLAN assigned & available. Install Access Point: Install access point (kept as its own one-item phase). After Installing Access Point: Document AP's MAC & assigned name · Document AP's location · Document AP's switch / port used · Document AP's IP address · Confirm AP installed in proper orientation · Confirm external antennas installed correctly · Wait for access point to receive configuration · Wait for a 2nd reboot of the AP if needed · Listen in air for all SSIDs being broadcast · Connect client to each SSID · Check each SSID for proper VLAN & IP pool.
fieldNotes: Your checkmarks aren't saved; they reset when you leave the screen.

Source: Keith Parsons / WLAN Pros original card (© 2024 WLAN Pros), with obvious typos fixed. The "After Installing" list is renumbered to a clean 1-12 (the original card was gap-numbered with no item 2); items are numbered in the order shown.
checklist-ap-install.source: A built-in, offline checklist; tap items to track your progress. Nothing is sent anywhere.

dbm-watt-converter.name: dBm / Watt Converter
dbm-watt-converter.purpose: Live two-way conversion between dBm, Watts, and milliwatts. Type in any one field and the other two update as you go.
dbm-watt-converter.whyHere: RF power shows up in three different units depending on the spec sheet, the regulator, or the radio. This converts between them so you don't reach for a calculator at the access point.
howToUse: Type a value in any of the three fields (dBm, Watts, or milliwatts) and the other two update instantly.
dBm and Watts accept a sign and scientific notation (for example, 1e-10). Milliwatts takes a plain positive decimal.
Watts shows in scientific notation because real Wi-Fi receive levels are tiny (around 1e-10 W). Milliwatts shows four decimal places.
Zero or negative Watts or milliwatts shows a dash in the dBm field, because you can't take the logarithm of zero or a negative number.

inputs.name: dBm
inputs.range: any real number; a sign and scientific notation are accepted

inputs.name: Watts
inputs.range: greater than 0 to convert to dBm; 0 or less shows a dash; a sign and scientific notation are accepted

inputs.name: Milliwatts
inputs.range: greater than 0 to convert to dBm; 0 or less shows a dash; positive decimal

dbm-watt-converter.algorithm: The math is the standard decibel relationship. To go from dBm to milliwatts, raise 10 to the power of (dBm divided by 10). Watts is just that milliwatt figure divided by 1000, since 1 W equals 1000 mW. Going the other way, dBm equals 10 times the base-10 logarithm of the power in milliwatts. Because you can't take a logarithm of zero or a negative number, non-positive Watts or milliwatts show a dash instead of a dBm value.
dbm-watt-converter.example: 0 dBm equals 1 mW, which is 0.001 W. +30 dBm equals 1000 mW, which is 1 W. 20 mW equals about 13.01 dBm.
fieldNotes: 0 dBm is exactly 1 mW by definition. It's the reference point the whole scale is built on.

The on-screen reference card anchors common values: +30 dBm = 1000 mW (1 W, the FCC 2.4 GHz max conducted power), +13 dBm = 20 mW (a common default AP Tx power), 0 dBm = 1 mW, -67 dBm = 0.2 nW (a minimum for enterprise data), and -80 dBm = 10 pW (a typical ambient noise floor).
Watts uses scientific notation on purpose. Wi-Fi receive power sits around 1e-10 W, which written out in full is an unreadable string of zeros.
The formulas match Keith's published RF tools reference so the answers line up everywhere.
dbm-watt-converter.source: Calculated on the device using the standard decibel formulas; nothing is sent anywhere.

channel-frequency.name: Channel / Frequency
channel-frequency.purpose: A two-way Wi-Fi channel and frequency converter for 2.4, 5, and 6 GHz. Channel to frequency: pick a band, a 20 MHz primary channel, and a width to get the center frequency, the 20 MHz channels that make it up, and the low and high band edges. Frequency to channel: enter a frequency in MHz to get back the band and channel.
channel-frequency.whyHere: Spec sheets, surveys, and radios mix channel numbers and frequencies, and the same channel number means different frequencies in 5 and 6 GHz. This converts both ways from the verified channel plan, so you're never guessing whether channel 36 is 5 or 6 GHz.
howToUse: Pick the direction with the toggle at the top.

Channel to frequency: choose the band, then a 20 MHz primary channel, then the width. Only valid primaries are offered (bonded center numbers like 5 GHz 42 or 6 GHz 7 aren't selectable). The result shows the center frequency, the 20 MHz channels that make it up, and the band edges.
Frequency to channel: type a frequency in MHz. Values within plus or minus 1 MHz of a channel center snap to that channel; anything off the grid or outside the Wi-Fi plan is honestly rejected.
A 6 GHz 320 MHz primary that lands in two overlapping 320 MHz channels, or an interior 2.4 GHz 40 MHz primary, shows both placements.

inputs.name: Band
inputs.range: required for channel to frequency (channel numbers collide across 5 and 6 GHz)

inputs.name: Channel
inputs.range: 2.4 GHz 1-14; 5 GHz UNII-1 through UNII-4 set; 6 GHz 1, 5, 9 ... 233 plus special channel 2

inputs.name: Frequency
inputs.range: any value; snaps to a channel within plus or minus 1 MHz, otherwise rejected

channel-frequency.algorithm: For most channels, the center frequency is the band's starting frequency plus 5 MHz times the channel number. The starting frequencies are 2407 MHz for 2.4 GHz, 5000 MHz for 5 GHz, and 5950 MHz for 6 GHz. Two channels are special cases: 2.4 GHz channel 14 sits at 2484 MHz, and 6 GHz channel 2 sits at 5935 MHz. A bonded (wider) channel's center is the average of the centers of the 20 MHz channels inside it, and the band edges are the center plus or minus half the width. Going the other way, the tool snaps your frequency to the 5 MHz grid within plus or minus 1 MHz and returns the one band and channel it matches, since the bands don't overlap in frequency. Channel numbers are checked against the verified list of valid 20 MHz primaries for each band.
channel-frequency.example: 5 GHz channel 36 at 80 MHz: it's made of channels 36/40/44/48, the center channel is 42, the center frequency is 5210 MHz, and the edges are 5170 to 5250 MHz. 6 GHz channel 2 is the special 5935 MHz. Going backward, 5935 MHz maps to 6 GHz channel 2, 5180 MHz maps to 5 GHz channel 36, and 5187 MHz is rejected because it's off the grid.
fieldNotes: Channel and frequency are pure physics, identical worldwide. Which channels you're allowed to use (US 1-11 versus world 1-13, DFS, UNII-4, 6 GHz country adoption, channels 2 and 14) is regulatory and varies by region. The tool shows the physics and flags the regulatory caveats.
The same channel number can mean different bands: channel 36 exists in both 5 and 6 GHz, so channel to frequency always needs the band.
Bonded center numbers (5 GHz 42/50, 6 GHz 7/15/31) are labels for the middle of a wide channel, not selectable 20 MHz channels.
802.11be defines overlapping 320 MHz channels in 6 GHz, so one primary can sit in two of them. Both are shown, without claiming the unverified 320 MHz-1 / 320 MHz-2 naming.
channel-frequency.source: Uses the WLAN Pros channel plan, computed on the device.

ipv4-subnet.name: IP Subnetting (IPv4)
ipv4-subnet.purpose: Works out the full IPv4 subnet breakdown (network, broadcast, netmask, wildcard, first and last usable host, total addresses, and usable host count) from an address plus a CIDR prefix or a dotted mask.
ipv4-subnet.whyHere: The CIDR math you'd otherwise do on paper or in your head at a whiteboard. Reach for it to confirm a network or broadcast boundary, a usable-host range, or a mask-to-prefix conversion.
howToUse: Enter an IPv4 address (for example, 10.20.0.0). You can add the prefix inline as 10.20.0.0/22; an inline prefix wins and the second field is ignored.
Otherwise enter the prefix or mask in the second field: a CIDR prefix (22 or /22) or a dotted mask (255.255.252.0).
The breakdown updates live on every valid keystroke. A host inside the subnet (for example, 10.20.0.37/22) reports the same network as the base address.
Bad input shows a short 'Check your input' note with a specific message rather than a wrong answer.
Use the Copy action in the top bar to copy the breakdown as a labeled block of text.

inputs.name: Prefix or mask
inputs.range: prefix 0-32, or a valid dotted mask (for example, 255.255.252.0); ignored when the address already carries an inline prefix

ipv4-subnet.algorithm: It does the standard binary subnet math. The mask keeps the leftmost bits set by your prefix. The network address is the address with the host bits cleared; the broadcast address is the network with those host bits all set; the wildcard is the inverse of the mask. Total addresses is 2 raised to the number of host bits. For prefixes up to /30, the usable hosts are the total minus 2 (you drop the network and broadcast), the first usable host is the network plus 1, and the last is the broadcast minus 1. A /31 is a special point-to-point case: there's no network or broadcast reservation, so both addresses are usable hosts. A /32 is a single-host route: one address, no range, no broadcast. A dotted mask is only accepted if it's a valid run of 1 bits with no gaps, and an address must be exactly four numbers, each 0 to 255.
ipv4-subnet.example: 10.20.0.0/22 gives netmask 255.255.252.0, wildcard 0.0.3.255, network 10.20.0.0, broadcast 10.20.3.255, first host 10.20.0.1, last host 10.20.3.254, 1024 total addresses, and 1022 usable. (This is the example the screen opens with.)
fieldNotes: A /31 (RFC 3021) is a point-to-point link: there's no network or broadcast to set aside, so both addresses are usable (usable = 2). The screen calls this out.
A /32 is a single-host route: one address, no range, no broadcast (usable = 1).
A host address inside the block reports the subnet's network, not the host itself. Every value comes from the masked base.
A dotted mask has to be a valid contiguous run of 1 bits; something like 255.0.255.0 is rejected as invalid.
No network needed, just math, so it runs on every platform including the web.
ipv4-subnet.source: Calculated on the device with straight subnet math; nothing leaves the app.

port-reference.name: Well-Known Ports
port-reference.purpose: A searchable, offline reference of 89 hand-picked TCP/UDP ports a network or Wi-Fi pro runs into in the field. Search by port number or by part of a service name or description.
port-reference.whyHere: At a packet capture or writing a firewall rule, you ask two questions: what runs on port N, and what port does service X use. This answers both without leaving the app or going online.
howToUse: Type a port number (for example, 443) for an exact-port lookup, or a service name or keyword (for example, radius, dns, vpn) to match against both the service name and the description, ignoring case.
Leave the search box empty to list all 89 ports, sorted from lowest number to highest.
Each result shows the service name, the port number with its protocol label (TCP, UDP, or both), and a one-line description.
A search that matches nothing shows an honest 'No match' note; it never makes up a port.
fieldNotes: What's inside: 89 hand-picked entries spanning ports 1 to 27017 (for example, 53 dns TCP/UDP, 67 dhcp UDP, 123 ntp UDP, 443 https TCP/UDP where UDP 443 carries HTTP/3 QUIC, 1812 radius UDP and 1813 radius-acct UDP for 802.1X / WPA2-Enterprise, and 3389 rdp). Each entry has the port number, its protocol(s), a short service name, and a one-line description.
The table lists TCP and UDP only: 38 are TCP-only, 28 UDP-only, and 23 both.

This is a curated subset of the official IANA port registry, trimmed to the ports you actually meet in the field, not the full list of roughly 49,000 entries. If a port isn't here, it just means it's not in our set, and the screen says so plainly rather than inventing a row.
Fully offline: the list is carried inside the app and works on every platform.
Bad or incomplete entries are simply left out rather than shown as blanks.
port-reference.source: A curated list carried inside the app; searched entirely offline.

unit-converter.name: Unit Converter
unit-converter.purpose: Converts a value between units in one of eight categories: data transfer rate, data storage, length, power, metric prefix, speed, temperature, and time.
unit-converter.whyHere: Field work mixes units constantly. A datasheet quotes throughput in Mbps, your test rig reports MB/s, an RF reading lands in dBm when you wanted milliwatts. Pick a category, pick the two units, read the answer.
howToUse: Pick a category (data transfer rate, data storage, length, power, metric prefix, speed, temperature, or time).
Type a value, then pick the 'from' unit and the 'to' unit.
Read the converted result. It stays blank until the value is a valid number.

inputs.name: Value, from-unit, to-unit
inputs.unit: the units offered in the chosen category
inputs.range: a sign and scientific notation are accepted (a temperature can be negative)

unit-converter.algorithm: Most categories convert through a single base unit: the tool turns your value into the base unit, then into the target unit. Power and temperature don't work that way. Power handles dBm with the same decibel math as the dBm/Watt converter (Watts from dBm is 10 raised to (dBm/10), divided by 1000; dBm from Watts is 10 times the base-10 log of the power in milliwatts). Temperature converts through Kelvin, since it needs both a scale and an offset, not just a multiply.
unit-converter.example: 100 MB is 800 Mbit (storage, decimal). 100 degrees C is 212 degrees F (temperature). 1 W is 30 dBm (power).
fieldNotes: Decimal and binary are kept separate. A KB is 1000 bytes; a KiB is 1024 bytes. The tool never mixes the two, so a storage answer reads true to the standard you picked.
Bits and bytes are different units, not a display toggle. 1 byte = 8 bits, and the converter treats them that way in both the storage and the rate categories.
Temperature and dBm aren't simple multiply-by-a-factor conversions. They're handled with the correct math, so a negative temperature or a sub-milliwatt power reads correctly.
unit-converter.source: Calculated on the device; nothing is sent anywhere.

qr-generator.name: QR Code Generator
