qr-generator.purpose: Turns a URL or some text, or a Wi-Fi network, into a scannable QR code, then lets you share or save it as an image. The Wi-Fi mode builds a 'scan to join' code: point a phone camera at it and the phone offers to join the network.
qr-generator.whyHere: Hand off a config URL or a site address without making anyone type it, or let a guest join your Wi-Fi by pointing a camera at a code instead of reading out a long password. Generate the code on the spot and let them scan it.
howToUse: Pick a content type at the top: URL / Text, or Wi-Fi.
URL / Text: type the text or URL you want to encode.
Wi-Fi: enter the network name (SSID), pick the security type, type the password, and turn on Hidden network if the SSID isn't broadcast.
Optionally choose a module shape (Square, Rounded, or Dots) and a size (Small, Medium, or Large).
The QR code updates live as you type.
Tap Share / Save to send the image through the system share sheet or save it.

inputs.name: Content type
inputs.range: URL / Text encodes any text; Wi-Fi builds a join code

inputs.name: Text or URL
inputs.range: an empty box shows a prompt and no code

inputs.name: Network name (SSID)
inputs.range: required; an empty SSID shows a prompt and no code

inputs.name: Security
inputs.unit: WPA / WPA2 / WPA3, WEP, or None (Wi-Fi mode)
inputs.range: None hides the password field and makes an open-network code

inputs.name: Module shape / Size
inputs.unit: Square, Rounded, or Dots / Small, Medium, or Large
inputs.range: looks only; the code stays dark on white

qr-generator.algorithm: The code is made right on the device, with no network call. It always renders as dark blocks on a white background with a white border (the quiet zone), which is what scanners expect, no matter which shape or size you pick. Wi-Fi mode builds the standard Wi-Fi join text that phone cameras recognize as an offer to join, tagged WPA for the WPA/WPA2/WPA3 family, WEP for WEP, or open for a no-password network. Special characters in the network name and password are handled so the code scans reliably. Share / Save saves the white tile as a PNG at three times its screen size for a crisp image.
qr-generator.example: URL / Text: type https://wlanpros.com and the matching QR appears below the field. Wi-Fi: enter the SSID WLAN-Pros-Guest, pick WPA, and type the password to produce a code a guest can scan to join.
fieldNotes: Dark code on a white background, always. Inverted light-on-dark codes look nice, but many scanners can't read them, so the tool doesn't offer that. Shape and size never change this.
The white border around the code (the quiet zone) is part of the code. Don't crop it out when you share the image, or the QR may not scan.

Wi-Fi mode puts the password in plain text inside the QR, the same as any Wi-Fi join code. Anyone who scans the image can read or join the network, so share it the way you'd share the password itself.
WPA, WPA2, and WPA3 all use the same WPA tag in the code. Pick None only for a genuinely open network; that makes a code with no password.
Everything happens on the device. The text, SSID, and password you encode never leave the app except through the share sheet you trigger.
It's a generator, not a scanner: it makes codes rather than reading them.
qr-generator.source: Generated on the device with no network call.

dtmf-generator.name: DTMF Generator
dtmf-generator.purpose: Plays the Touch-Tone keypad tones (0-9, *, #, and A-D) from a standard 4x4 DTMF grid, one tap at a time or as a continuous loop. It also adds two Telephone Signaling History modes (Blue Box and Red Box) that recreate historical phone-network tones for education and nostalgia.
dtmf-generator.whyHere: Reach for it when you need to drive a system that still listens for Touch-Tones over the air or down a line: an IVR menu, a repeater controller, a legacy PBX, or a piece of test gear. Hold the device near the microphone and play the digit.
howToUse: Use the mode selector at the top to switch between DTMF, Blue Box, and Red Box.
DTMF: tap a key to play its tone for a short burst. The selected key and its two frequencies show at the top.
DTMF: tap Play to loop the selected key's tone continuously; tap Stop to end it.
DTMF: tapping a different key during a loop switches the loop to the new key.
Blue Box and Red Box: tap any signal to hear it; the readout shows its frequencies and burst timing.
dtmf-generator.algorithm: Each DTMF key is the sum of two sine waves: one low-group frequency (the row) and one high-group frequency (the column), per ITU-T Q.23. Low group: 697, 770, 852, 941 Hz. High group: 1209, 1336, 1477, 1633 Hz. A single key tap plays the pair for about 200 milliseconds. The tone is generated right on your device; nothing is dialed or transmitted by the app itself.
dtmf-generator.example: Key 5 is 770 Hz + 1336 Hz. Key 1 is 697 Hz + 1209 Hz. The * key is 941 Hz + 1209 Hz.
fieldNotes: Every key is two tones added together, which is exactly what "Dual-Tone Multi-Frequency" means. That is why the readout shows two frequencies, not one.
The A, B, C, and D keys are real DTMF tones that never appeared on consumer phones. They show up in radio, military, and control systems, which is why the full 4x4 grid is here.

Tones come out of the device speaker. The app makes the sound; it does not place a call or send anything down a phone line. To control remote gear, play the tone into that system's microphone or audio input.
Reliable detection depends on volume, the speaker, and the receiving system. Hold the device close and keep the level up if a stubborn IVR or controller misses a digit.

The Blue Box and Red Box modes are telephone signaling history, not working tools. The tones they play do nothing on any modern phone network: in-band signaling was retired for out-of-band SS7 signaling in the 1980s-1990s, and the coin-tone payphones are gone. They are here for historical and educational interest. See the Blue Box and Red Box help for the full story.
dtmf-generator.source: Generates the two-tone audio on your device and plays it through the speaker; nothing is dialed or transmitted.

morse-code.name: Morse Code
morse-code.purpose: Converts plain text to International Morse code and decodes dots and dashes back to text, live in both directions, with an optional audio tone.
morse-code.whyHere: A field utility for the times Wi-Fi and cellular are both gone: signaling with a light, a buzzer, or a key by ear, reading a beacon ID that idents in Morse, or just settling what a string of dots and dashes says. Encode once and play it as a tone the receiving ear or detector can pick up.
howToUse: Pick a direction: Text to Morse, or Morse to Text.
Type or paste into the top field. The converted result updates live in the card below.
In Morse to Text, separate letters with a single space and words with a slash, for example ... --- ... for SOS.
Tap Play to hear the message as a 600 Hz tone; tap Stop to end it.
Open Prosigns to see the named procedural signals (SOS, AR, SK, and so on).

inputs.name: Text or Morse
inputs.unit: letters, digits, punctuation / dots and dashes
inputs.range: any length; unmapped characters are skipped

morse-code.algorithm: International Morse code per ITU-R M.1677-1. Each letter, digit, and common punctuation mark maps to a fixed run of dots (dits) and dashes (dahs). Encoding joins the symbols within a character with no gap, characters within a word with a single space, and words with a slash. Decoding reverses the same convention, so encodable text converts back and forth without loss (lowercase folds to uppercase). The audio uses standard Morse timing: a dot is one unit, a dash is three units, the gap inside a character is one unit, between characters three units, and between words seven units, all keyed onto a single tone.
morse-code.example: SOS encodes to ... --- ... (S = three dots, O = three dashes). HELLO WORLD encodes to .... . .-.. .-.. --- / .-- --- .-. .-.. -.. with letters spaced and the two words split by a slash.
fieldNotes: There is no Morse code for the space character itself; word breaks are carried by the slash separator, which is why a decoded message shows spaces between words and not blanks inside them.

Prosigns like SOS are run together as one continuous string (...---...) with no internal letter gaps. They are procedural signals, not literal text, so they are listed for reference rather than produced when you encode ordinary words.
Decoding marks any code it does not recognize with a question mark rather than dropping it, so a mistyped symbol stays visible instead of quietly disappearing.
The audio is generated and played on the device speaker as a single tone; nothing is transmitted over the air or down a line by the app.
morse-code.source: Maps each character to its Morse pattern and plays the result as a single tone on your device.

optical-transceivers.name: Optical Transceivers
optical-transceivers.purpose: Searchable, offline reference of 35 optical Ethernet transceiver variants (1G to 400G) grouped by speed tier, plus the 9-row SFP-to-OSFP form-factor ladder. Each variant lists its designation, data rate, reach, fiber type, wavelength, and connector, with reach as the lead, trust-first column.
optical-transceivers.whyHere: When you are picking an optic, the field's first question is "which module, and how far will it go on this fiber?" This answers it without leaving the app or going online, and keeps the IEEE-versus-vendor line visible so you never quote a vendor reach as a standard guarantee.
howToUse: Browse by speed tier. The commonly-ordered tiers (10G / 25G / 100G) surface first and are flagged "Commonly ordered"; 1G / 40G / 200G / 400G follow.

Search by designation (e.g. LR4), reach, fiber (SMF / MMF / OM4), wavelength (e.g. 850 nm), connector (LC / MPO), or tier (e.g. 100G). The match ignores case, looks anywhere in those fields, and narrows the tiers in place.
Read the REACH line first; it is the IEEE maximum on the listed fiber. The fiber chip (MMF / SMF) and connector chip (LC / MPO) give the physical-layer specifics at a glance.
A search that matches nothing shows an honest "No match" card; it never invents a module. The form-factor table is always available below the variants.
fieldNotes: IEEE vs VENDOR: variants ratified in IEEE 802.3 carry a neutral IEEE chip. Vendor and coherent variants (1000BASE-EX, 1000BASE-ZX, 10GBASE-ZR, and 400GBASE-ZR) carry an amber VENDOR chip and a "loss-budget dependent" caveat. Their reach is real and widely sold but NOT an IEEE guarantee; it depends on the link's loss budget. Never quote a vendor reach as a standard figure.

400GBASE-ZR is coherent DWDM (OIF 400ZR), beyond base IEEE 802.3: a metro or data-center-interconnect optic, not a base Ethernet PHY. Its 80-120 km reach depends on the loss budget and the DWDM line system.

Reach figures are IEEE-standard maximums on the listed fiber; real vendor modules may differ. Multimode reach depends on the fiber grade (OM3 vs OM4 vs OM5), so those rows give per-grade numbers rather than one figure.

What it contains: 35 verified variants spanning 1G to 400G across SFP, SFP+, SFP28, QSFP+, QSFP28, QSFP56, and QSFP-DD / OSFP, plus a 9-row form-factor ladder (max rate, lane count, typical power envelope). Power figures are typical vendor ranges, not a single standard, so treat them as guidance.
Fully offline: the table lives inside the app and is read once when the screen opens; malformed rows are skipped, not shown as blanks.

Verified against IEEE 802.3 standards tables (via Wikipedia's clause-mirroring pages) and Cisco / FS.com vendor datasheets. Out-of-scope bleeding edge (800G / 1.6T, CPO / LPO, SFP-DD) was deliberately excluded as not yet stable or field-relevant.
optical-transceivers.source: Reads a built-in, verified table of transceiver variants; fully offline, nothing fetched.

wifi-exposure-perspective.name: How Strong Is Wi-Fi, Really?
wifi-exposure-perspective.purpose: A vendor-neutral comparison of how much energy Wi-Fi puts into your body versus the everyday sun, using verified, stated numbers. No inputs and no live math; it is a read-along reference.
wifi-exposure-perspective.whyHere: Wi-Fi exposure questions come up constantly. This puts the energy in perspective against something everyone already lives with, sunlight, using verified numbers instead of hand-waving. With its stated assumptions (ten access points at 30 dBm / 1 watt EIRP, four meters away, free-space spread), the ring of access points delivers about 0.05 W/m2. The midday sun delivers about 1,000 W/m2, full spectrum, which makes it about 20,000 times stronger per square meter.
howToUse: Read the short version first: one hour of midday sun puts about as much energy into your body as roughly 2.3 years sitting inside a ring of ten Wi-Fi access points 4 meters (13 feet) away.
The energy-parity table shows the same idea as time: 10 seconds of sun is about 2.3 days in the ring, 1 minute is about two weeks, 1 hour is about 2.3 years.
The assumptions card states every number behind the result, so nothing is hidden: 10 access points at 1 W EIRP, 4 m free-space spread, the sun at 1,000 W/m2 full spectrum.
The safety-limit card states where this sits against the FCC (47 CFR 1.1310) and ICNIRP 2020 public limit of 10 W/m2; the ring is about 200 times below it.
wifi-exposure-perspective.algorithm: Free-space power density: divide the access point's EIRP by the area of a sphere at your distance (4 pi times distance squared), measured at 4 m and stated as approximate.
wifi-exposure-perspective.example: At 30 dBm (1 W) EIRP and 4 m, each access point is 0.00497 W/m2; ten of them come to about 0.05 W/m2. The sun at 1,000 W/m2 is about 20,000 times stronger, so 1 hour of sun is about 2.3 years in the ring.
fieldNotes: Honest note on mechanism: Wi-Fi radio energy is non-ionizing. It can only gently warm tissue. It cannot cause the photochemical or DNA damage that sunburn does, at any Wi-Fi power level. This is a comparison of total energy and warmth, never of sunburn: the UV in sunlight burns; Wi-Fi has no UV and no comparable mechanism.

The basis is 30 dBm (1 W) EIRP per access point (the US 2.4 GHz maximum) at 4 meters (13 feet). 4 m is realistic for how far a person sits from the access points around them; free-space density falls with the square of distance, so the figures are conservative for the point being made.

Where it sits against the limit: the FCC and ICNIRP cap public RF exposure at 10 W/m2. The 10-access-point ring at about 0.05 W/m2 is roughly 200 times below that limit, and measured real-world Wi-Fi runs far lower still.

Every figure is stated as approximate and traces to a verified source: FCC 47 CFR 1.1310 and OET-65; ICNIRP 2020 RF guidelines; ASTM G173 / NREL reference solar spectrum; WHO and IARC on non-ionizing RF (Group 2B) versus solar UV (Group 1); peer-reviewed Wi-Fi exposure surveys (PMC5927334, PMC8172712).
wifi-exposure-perspective.source: A read-along reference built from verified, stated numbers; no inputs and no live calculation.

freeradius-wlanpi.name: FreeRADIUS on WLAN Pi
freeradius-wlanpi.purpose: A step-by-step guide to standing up a lab RADIUS server on a WLAN Pi for learning and testing 802.1X (WPA2/WPA3-Enterprise), built around a bundled install script you copy to the Pi and run there.
freeradius-wlanpi.whyHere: Reach for it when you want a working RADIUS server to practice 802.1X against without building a full production AAA stack. The screen shows the install script inline, lets you download it, and points at the two files to customize: the clients file for the shared secret and your own networks, and the user list.
howToUse: Read the lab caveat: the script ships test accounts (student01-student10) with cleartext passwords and a shared secret named secretwlanpros. Change the secret and use real credentials before anything real.
Download install_freeradius.sh (or copy it from the inline view).
Copy it to the Pi: scp install_freeradius.sh wlanpi@<your-Pi-IP>:~
Make it executable: chmod +x install_freeradius.sh
Run it on the Pi: ./install_freeradius.sh
Verify with the listed radtest / journalctl / tcpdump commands.
fieldNotes: The Toolbox runs nothing itself. This is reference text plus a file you run on your own WLAN Pi, not in the app. The inline script and the download are the exact same file.

Lab use only as shipped: cleartext-password test accounts and a known shared secret. Change the secret and add an entry for each of your own networks (your access points or controllers) in the clients configuration, and replace the test users in the user list, before using it on a real network.
The script sets up PEAP/MSCHAPv2, opens UDP 1812, and runs a radtest against student01 to confirm the server answers.
Guide and install script by Ferney Munoz; bundled with permission.
freeradius-wlanpi.source: Reference text plus a downloadable install script by Ferney Munoz that you run on your own WLAN Pi.

antenna-fundamentals.name: Antenna Fundamentals
antenna-fundamentals.purpose: A read-along teaching reference for antenna literacy: what an antenna actually does (it shapes where the radio's energy goes, it does not add power), azimuth vs elevation, why gain trades against beamwidth, polarization and the wall-clock mistake, downtilt, how to read a radiation-pattern polar plot (main lobe, the -3 dB beamwidth points, side lobes, nulls, front-to-back ratio), and which antenna type (omni, patch, sector, Yagi, dish) fits which space. Seven line diagrams are embedded at the points they teach.
antenna-fundamentals.whyHere: It is the antenna-literacy companion to the directional tools in the toolbox (Downtilt, Point-to-Point, Fresnel). When you are choosing or mounting an antenna and want to reason about coverage shape rather than chase a gain number, this is the read-first reference.
howToUse: Read top to bottom: it builds from one idea (an antenna is a shaper, not a booster) through azimuth/elevation, orientation, reading a pattern chart, and what to use where.

Use the embedded diagrams alongside the prose: the polar-plot anatomy diagram labels every feature (main lobe, -3 dB beamwidth, side lobes, nulls, back lobe, front-to-back) so a manufacturer's chart stops being a mystery.
The type table and the closing deployment quick-map are the fast lookups: match the space you are covering to the antenna whose pattern fits it.
fieldNotes: This is conceptual teaching copy, not a calculator and not a manufacturer spec sheet. It deliberately gives no formula for gain vs beamwidth: "more gain means a narrower beam" is always directionally true, but the exact beamwidth for a given gain depends on the specific antenna's design. Read the published beamwidth on the data sheet; do not try to back it out of the gain number.

The per-type beamwidth figures (patch about 30-120 degrees, sector about 60-120, Yagi about 15-40, dish about 3-25) are RANGES across real products, not single specs. The antenna in your hand lands somewhere inside its band.

The cross-polarization penalty is given conceptually: 90 degrees is the worst case (in theory total loss; in practice reflections leave some signal), and a wall-mounted omni costs roughly 6 dB. These are rules of thumb to reason with, not an exact loss figure.
The floor-plan coverage shapes illustrate how a pattern fills a space; they are not survey predictions or measured coverage.

The point-to-point note is one line by design: a link needs real Fresnel-zone clearance around the straight line between antennas, not just a visible path. Use the Fresnel and Point-to-Point tools to size that clearance.
antenna-fundamentals.source: Original WLAN Pros teaching copy and diagrams.

wifi-tools-comparison.name: Wi-Fi Tools Comparison
wifi-tools-comparison.purpose: A vendor-neutral, offline reference that compares professional Wi-Fi survey, design, spectrum-analysis, and troubleshooting toolkits, grouped by the activity each one serves. Each configuration lists its vendor, product, license model, up-front cost, and 3-year total cost of ownership, with a neutral note on what the bundle does and does not include. A per-vendor summary and a typical-toolkit roll-up ride alongside.
wifi-tools-comparison.whyHere: A neutral, capability-level map of the professional Wi-Fi tooling, built from Keith's vendor-interviewed workbook, so a leveling-up engineer can see the four activities and which tools serve which job without a vendor sales pitch.
howToUse: Browse by activity: Design, Validation, Spectrum Analysis, then Troubleshooting. Within each activity, configurations are listed alphabetically by vendor.

Search by vendor (e.g. Ekahau), product, activity (e.g. spectrum), capability (e.g. survey), or license model (e.g. perpetual). The match ignores case, looks anywhere in those fields, and narrows the activities in place.
Read the up-front and 3-year TCO figures as planning estimates, not quotes. Use the vendor Website and Docs links to confirm current pricing and product details before you buy.
A search that matches nothing shows an honest "No match" card; it never invents a tool. The typical-toolkit roll-up and the per-vendor summaries are always available below the activities.
fieldNotes: This comparison is in BETA REVIEW. Vendors are being consulted on the figures, and a few may change before the final release.
Pricing is dated. The figures are as of July 2026; confirm current pricing with each vendor, because prices, bundles, and product names change often.

Cost figures are MODELED ESTIMATES assembled by WLAN Pros from vendor-supplied numbers and list pricing, not vendor-published quotes. Treat every dollar amount as a planning estimate, never a binding price.

This is NOT a ranking. There is no score and no "best". Tools are listed alphabetically and the same vendor can appear under more than one activity, because real toolkits are assembled across vendors. The set reflects vendors interviewed by WLAN Pros, not every tool that exists, so an omitted tool is not a snub.

No vendor logos or product photos appear here. Those are trademarks and copyrighted images that need each vendor's written permission, which is still being gathered. The reference is text and data only for now.
Fully offline: the comparison lives inside the app and is read once when the screen opens; malformed rows are skipped, not shown as blanks.
Source: the Wi-Fi Design, Validation, Spectrum Analysis & Troubleshooting Tools V6 workbook (Keith Parsons, vendor-interviewed, last revised 2026-02-16).
wifi-tools-comparison.source: Reads a built-in, vendor-interviewed comparison table; fully offline, nothing fetched.

power-phasing.name: Power Phasing
power-phasing.purpose: Reference for the three power topologies a field tech meets in IT spaces: US single-phase 120V (one hot plus neutral), split-phase 120/240V (two hots 180 degrees apart, 240V hot-to-hot, 120V hot-to-neutral), and three-phase wye 208V (three hots 120 degrees apart, 208V line-to-line, 120V line-to-neutral). It makes clear the 208-vs-240 distinction that installers confuse.
power-phasing.whyHere: When you read a panel or nameplate, confirm which topology you're on and what hot-to-hot voltage to expect, so you don't feed 240V-rated gear from a 208V wye (about 13 percent low) or mislabel split-phase as two-phase.
howToUse: Each topology card shows its hots, line-to-neutral (L-N), line-to-line (L-L), and phase angle, with a waveform.

Split-phase is 240V hot-to-hot because the two legs are 180 degrees apart (2 x 120 in anti-phase); three-phase wye is 208V hot-to-hot because the legs are 120 degrees apart (the square root of 3 times 120, about 208V).
Use the 208V-vs-240V table to settle the confusion: same 120V hot-to-neutral, different hot-to-hot, different source topology.
power-phasing.algorithm: Line-to-line voltage equals the square root of 3 times the line-to-neutral voltage for a wye system: 120 x 1.732 is about 208V. Split-phase line-to-line is twice the line-to-neutral in anti-phase: 2 x 120 = 240V.
power-phasing.example: A 240V single-phase heater fed from a 208V wye panel sees about 13 percent lower voltage and runs at reduced output, because two legs of a 208V wye are 120 degrees apart (208V), not 180 degrees apart (240V).
fieldNotes: What it shows: three topology cards (single-phase 120V, split-phase 120/240V, three-phase wye 208V) with hots, L-N, L-L, phase angle, where it's seen, and a clarifying note; plus a 208V-vs-240V comparison table and a why-it-matters note.
Split-phase is single-phase, NOT two-phase: the two phasors don't define a rotating field. The center conductor is the grounded, current-carrying neutral (center tap), not the equipment ground.
208V and 240V are not interchangeable: they differ by about 32V and arise from different topologies (a single-phase center tap versus two legs of a 3-phase wye).
Written "Access Point" not "router". Nominal voltages per NEC Article 100/210.
Data source: NEC Article 100/210, split-phase and three-phase EE references, and the line-to-line = square-root-of-3 times line-to-neutral relationship.
power-phasing.source: Reads a built-in reference of power topologies grounded in NEC Article 100/210; fully offline.

ohms-law.name: Ohm's Law & Power Wheel
ohms-law.purpose: Reference for the V / I / R / P relationships every field tech reaches for: the four core identities (V = I x R, P = V x I, P = I squared x R, P = V squared / R), the 12-segment power wheel that expresses each of V, I, R, P in terms of any two of the others, and the single-phase versus three-phase power formulas with the power-factor term.
ohms-law.whyHere: When you need to solve for a missing electrical quantity at a panel or on a nameplate, find the right form of the wheel and read off the relationship, without re-deriving it. The power-factor caveat keeps you from reading V x I as watts on an AC reactive load.
howToUse: The core relationships table gives the four identities and what each solves for; everything else derives from V = I x R and P = V x I.
The power wheel lists each of V, I, R, and P in three forms, so you can pick the one written in terms of the two quantities you already know.
The single-phase versus three-phase table carries the apparent-power (VA) and real-power (W) formulas; real power includes the power-factor term.
ohms-law.algorithm: Apparent power S = V x I (single-phase) or the square root of 3 times line-to-line voltage times line current (three-phase balanced). Real power P = S times the power factor. The power factor equals 1 only for purely resistive loads and DC; for reactive loads it is less than 1, so V x I gives volt-amps (VA), not watts (W).
ohms-law.example: A 230V single-phase load drawing 5A has an apparent power of 230 x 5 = 1150 VA. If its power factor is 0.8, real power is 1150 x 0.8 = 920 W, which is why UPS and PDU equipment is rated in both VA and W.
fieldNotes: What it shows: the four core identities, the 12-form power wheel (four quantities x three forms), and the single-phase versus three-phase power formulas with a power-factor note.

Power factor is 1 only for resistive loads and DC, where P = V x I is exact. On reactive loads (motors, compressors, switch-mode supplies) V x I gives apparent power in VA, not real power in watts; do not read P = V x I as universally giving watts in AC.
Notation: I squared and V squared use the caret in the app, square root is written out, and the power-factor angle is written cos(phi). Written "Access Point" not "router".
Data source: the standard EE identity set, the 12-form Ohm's-law wheel, and the AC real / apparent / reactive power relationships.
ohms-law.source: Reads a built-in reference of the Ohm's-law identities and power-wheel forms; fully offline.

cooling-thermal.name: Cooling & Thermal
cooling-thermal.purpose: The heat-load conversions you need when sizing cooling for IT spaces: watts to BTU/hr to tons of refrigeration, the fact that IT power turns into heat that has to be removed, and the standard airflow (CFM for a given temperature rise) rule.
cooling-thermal.whyHere: When you have to size cooling for a closet or rack from an IT load, turn that load into BTU/hr or tons and read the airflow a given temperature rise needs, so the cooling equipment matches the heat the gear actually produces.
howToUse: The conversion table gives common loads in watts, BTU/hr, and tons, all built from two anchors: 1 W = 3.412 BTU/hr and 1 ton = 12,000 BTU/hr.
The load-to-heat-to-cooling note shows that essentially all IT power becomes heat, so the cooling load in watts equals the IT load in watts before you add margin.
The airflow note tells you how much airflow (CFM) a target supply-to-return temperature rise needs.
cooling-thermal.algorithm: To convert power to heat, multiply watts by 3.412 to get BTU/hr. To get tons, divide BTU/hr by 12,000 (so one ton is about 3,517 W). For airflow, the sensible-heat rule is BTU/hr = 1.08 x CFM x temperature rise in degrees F, which rearranges to CFM = BTU/hr divided by (1.08 x temperature rise). The 1.08 figure is for standard moist air at sea level and shifts with altitude and air density.
cooling-thermal.example: A 1,000 W (1 kW) IT load is 3,412 BTU/hr, about 0.284 tons. Removed across a 20 degree F supply-to-return rise, it needs about 158 CFM of airflow.
fieldNotes: What it shows: a watts / BTU-hr / tons conversion table, the load-to-heat-to-cooling relationship, and the airflow (CFM for a given temperature rise) guidance. No diagram: these relationships are numeric and read cleanly as tables.
Essentially all the electrical power an IT device draws is given off as heat into the room, so the cooling load equals the IT load before you add any headroom for losses and growth.

The 1.08 airflow figure is a standard-condition approximation, not a universal one: check it against site altitude and air density before sizing equipment. "BTU/hr" not "BTUH". An access point is an access point, never a router.
Built from the anchor conversions 1 W = 3.412 BTU/hr and 1 ton = 12,000 BTU/hr, plus the standard sensible-heat airflow rule.
cooling-thermal.source: A built-in reference table using standard heat and airflow conversions; nothing is measured or sent anywhere.

iec-connectors.name: IEC Power Connectors
iec-connectors.purpose: A guide to the IEC 60320 appliance couplers (C1/C2 through C19/C20, including the C13/C14 PC cord and the C15/C16 kettle cord) and the IEC 60309 industrial pin-and-sleeve connectors, with current ratings, temperature classes, and keying.
iec-connectors.whyHere: When you grab a cord or read an inlet on a PDU, server, or UPS, confirm the coupler pair and its rating so you match cord to inlet, and tell the 70 degC C13/C14 PC cord apart from the 120 degC C15/C16 kettle cord.
howToUse: The IEC 60320 table lists each coupler pair with its current rating, maximum temperature, nickname, and typical use. An odd number is the cord connector (female); the even number is the appliance inlet (male), one higher than its mate.
The IEC 60309 table maps each connector color to its voltage band; the earth-pin clock position (in 30-degree steps) is mechanical keying, so incompatible voltages cannot mate.
Use the notes to settle the kettle-cord confusion and to confirm that both color and earth-pin hour must match for two IEC 60309 devices to connect.
iec-connectors.example: A C13 connector (female, on the cord) mates with a C14 inlet (male, on the back of a PC or PDU). A C15 cord fits a C14 inlet, but a C13 cord will not fit a C16 inlet because the C15/C16 keying notch blocks it.
fieldNotes: What it shows: the IEC 60320 appliance-coupler table (pair, current, max temperature, nickname, use) and the IEC 60309 industrial-connector table (color, voltage band, use), with keying notes. A connector-face diagram is planned for a later update; the tables work fully without it.
The "kettle cord" nickname properly belongs to C15/C16 (120 degC hot-condition, keyed by a notch), NOT C13/C14 (70 degC cold-condition "PC cord").
IEC 60309 red spans 380-480V (not a single "415V"): it covers 400V European and 480V US three-phase. Both the color AND the earth-pin clock hour must match to mate.
Based on the IEC 60320 and IEC 60309-2 standards.
iec-connectors.source: A built-in reference table drawn from the published IEC connector standards; nothing is measured or sent anywhere.

nema-connectors.name: NEMA Connectors
nema-connectors.purpose: A guide to North American NEMA straight-blade and locking plug and receptacle configurations: how to read a designation (the L prefix, the configuration code, the current rating, and the P/R suffix) and the device groups by voltage class with phase, wiring, and amp rating.
nema-connectors.whyHere: When you read a NEMA designation on a plug, receptacle, or PDU, decode it correctly instead of misreading the leading number as a voltage, and confirm the phase and wiring so you do not mistake split-phase for three-phase.
howToUse: Walk the decoder left to right across a designation like L21-30P: L (locking), 21 (a configuration code, not a voltage), 30 (the amp rating), P (plug; R is receptacle).
The device-group tables list types by voltage class (125V, 208/240/250V) with phase, pole and wire count, and amp rating.
Use the phase notes to keep the single-phase split 14-series distinct from the three-phase wye L21-series.
nema-connectors.example: L21-30P decodes to L (twist-lock) + 21 (three-phase wye 120/208V, 4-pole 5-wire) + 30 (30A) + P (plug). L21-30R is its receptacle. The leading number is a configuration code, so do no arithmetic on it.
fieldNotes: What it shows: the designation decoder with a worked example, plus device groups (125V straight-blade and locking, and 208/240/250V) listing type, voltage, phase, wiring, and amps. A face-diagram plate is planned for a later update; the tables work fully without it.
The leading number is a voltage/pole/phase CLASS code, not a literal voltage: 21 = three-phase wye 120/208V, 4-pole 5-wire. Never read it as a voltage.
The 14-series is single-phase SPLIT (the 4th pin is neutral), NOT three-phase; only the L21-series is three-phase wye. P = plug (male), R = receptacle (female).
Based on the NEMA configuration and naming references.
nema-connectors.source: A built-in reference table drawn from the published NEMA configuration references; nothing is measured or sent anywhere.

international-plugs.name: International Power Plugs
international-plugs.purpose: A guide to the IEC World Plugs letter system (Types A through M) by region, with each one's national standard, nominal voltage class and frequency, and current rating, plus the CEE 7 European family breakout.
international-plugs.whyHere: When you travel or spec gear for a site abroad, confirm the plug type, nominal mains voltage, and frequency you will meet, so power adapters and equipment ratings match what is at the wall.
howToUse: Search by country at the top (for example, Germany shows Type C and F at 230V/50Hz). Common names and abbreviations work too, such as USA, UK, or Holland; countries with more than one type show all their letters.
The plug-type table lists each letter with its standard, voltage class, current rating, and the countries that use it.
The CEE 7 family breakout shows how the European C, E, and F types and the CEE 7/7 hybrid relate.
Read the voltage class as nominal: most of the world is 230V except North America and Japan at 120V. Watch the warnings (for example, Argentine Type I has line and neutral reversed).
international-plugs.example: A laptop charger rated 100-240V works on both a US Type A/B 120V outlet and a UK Type G 230V outlet with only a mechanical plug adapter; a 120V-only appliance needs a voltage converter, not just an adapter, on a 230V supply.
fieldNotes: What it shows: the IEC World Plugs Type A through M table (type, standard, voltage class, current, countries) and the CEE 7 European family breakout. A face diagram is planned for a later update; the tables work fully without it.
Voltage classes are nominal: 120V in North America and Japan, 230V across most of the rest of the world. Confirm frequency (50 vs 60 Hz) for motor-driven and timing-sensitive gear.
Argentine Type I (IRAM 2073) has line and neutral reversed relative to the Australian Type I; the CEE 7/7 plug is a hybrid designed to fit both French (E) and Schuko (F) sockets.
Based on the IEC World Plugs letter system and the national standards behind it.
international-plugs.source: A built-in reference table drawn from the IEC World Plugs system and national standards; nothing is measured or sent anywhere.

ip-address-reference.name: IP Address Reference
ip-address-reference.purpose: The special-use address blocks for IPv4 and IPv6 (the CIDR prefix, what each block is reserved for, and the standard that defines it), plus the rules for writing IPv6 addresses.
ip-address-reference.whyHere: When you see an unexpected address on a link, this tells you fast whether it is private, loopback, link-local, documentation, or multicast, and which standard governs it.
howToUse: Scroll the IPv4 and IPv6 special-use tables. Read by CIDR prefix.
Check the IPv6 notation card for the canonical-form and zero-compression rules before reading or writing an IPv6 address.
fieldNotes: What it shows: the official IPv4 and IPv6 special-purpose address registries (private-use, loopback, link-local, documentation, and more), each row carrying the standard that defines it.

The multicast blocks 224.0.0.0/4 (IPv4) and ff00::/8 (IPv6) are not in the special-purpose registries: they live in the separate multicast registries and are sourced to RFC 5771 / RFC 1112 and RFC 4291 section 2.7, flagged with a footnote.
Based on the official IANA IPv4 and IPv6 special-purpose address registries, cross-checked against each defining RFC, with IPv6 notation per RFC 5952. Offline and read-only.
ip-address-reference.source: A built-in reference table drawn from the published address registries and RFCs; nothing is measured or sent anywhere.

cidr-table.name: Subnetting / CIDR Table
cidr-table.purpose: A /0 through /32 lookup: prefix length, dotted subnet mask, total addresses, usable hosts, and wildcard (inverse) mask.
cidr-table.whyHere: The fast way to read off a mask, host count, or wildcard for a prefix without doing the powers of two in your head.
howToUse: Find the prefix length (/n) row and read the mask, total addresses, usable hosts, and wildcard across it.
For a specific network, broadcast, or host range, use the IPv4 Subnet Calculator tool.
fieldNotes: What it shows: every prefix from /0 to /32, where the total addresses double each step (2 to the power of 32 minus the prefix length) and usable hosts are that total minus 2, plus the dotted mask and the inverse (wildcard) mask.
Exceptions honored: /31 has 2 usable host addresses (point-to-point, RFC 3021), not 0; /32 is 1 host (single-host route, RFC 4632), not -1. Both rows carry an inline note.
Based on standard CIDR arithmetic cross-referenced to RFC 4632 section 3.1. Offline and read-only.
cidr-table.source: A built-in reference table using standard CIDR arithmetic; nothing is measured or sent anywhere.

naming-conventions.name: Naming & Addressing Conventions
naming-conventions.purpose: Hostname and DNS-label rules, the MAC EUI-48 and EUI-64 formats, the U/L (universal/local) and I/G (individual/group) bits, and the OUI/CID concept.
naming-conventions.whyHere: When a hostname is rejected or a MAC address looks locally administered or multicast, this names the rule and the bit that decides it.
howToUse: Read the hostname rules for label length, allowed characters, and maximum full-name (FQDN) length.

Use the MAC-format and U/L, I/G bit tables (and the first-octet bit-field diagram) to tell a universally administered address from a locally administered one, and an individual address from a group or multicast one.
fieldNotes: What it shows: hostname and full-name (FQDN) rules (RFC 952, RFC 1123 section 2.1, RFC 1035), the MAC EUI-48/EUI-64 format, the U/L and I/G bit positions in the first octet, and OUI/CID assignment.
The first-octet bit-field diagram is decorative for screen readers: every fact it depicts (the U/L and I/G positions and their meanings) is also in the bit table.

Based on IEEE Std 802-2014 (with RFC 5342 section 2.1 as the freely available restatement) and RFC 4291 Appendix A for the Modified EUI-64. The IEEE Registration Authority is the sole assigner of OUI and CID. Offline and read-only.
naming-conventions.source: A built-in reference table drawn from the published IEEE and IETF naming and addressing standards; nothing is measured or sent anywhere.

dns-record-types.name: DNS Record Types
dns-record-types.purpose: The DNS record TYPE codes a network or Wi-Fi pro meets: the numeric TYPE code, what the record does, and the standard that defines it.
dns-record-types.whyHere: When a lookup returns an unfamiliar record type, this says what it carries and where it is defined.
howToUse: Scroll the table. Read by record type (A, AAAA, CNAME, MX, TXT, SRV, CAA, HTTPS/SVCB, and more) or by numeric TYPE code.
fieldNotes: What it shows: each DNS record TYPE with its numeric code, a plain-English purpose, and the standard that defines it.
Current registry state reflected: CAA is now governed by RFC 8659 (replacing RFC 6844); HTTPS/SVCB by RFC 9460.
Based on the official IANA DNS resource-record TYPEs registry, plus the defining RFC cited on each row. Offline and read-only.
dns-record-types.source: A built-in reference table drawn from the published DNS record-type registry; nothing is measured or sent anywhere.

dhcp-options.name: DHCP Options
dhcp-options.purpose: The DHCPv4 option codes a network or Wi-Fi pro meets, led by Option 138 (CAPWAP-AC), which is how a lightweight access point learns its WLAN controller address from DHCP, plus the Option 53 message-type table.
dhcp-options.whyHere: Reading a DHCP capture or a scope config: this names the option code and what value it carries, with the controller-discovery option called out first.
howToUse: Read the option-code table (Option 138 first, then standard options in code order) for the code, name, and purpose.
Use the Option 53 message-type table to decode DISCOVER, OFFER, REQUEST, ACK, and the rest.
fieldNotes: What it shows: common DHCPv4 option codes with names and a plain-English purpose, plus the Option 53 DHCP message types.
Option 138 (CAPWAP-AC, RFC 5417) leads because controller discovery is the option most relevant to a Wi-Fi deployment.

Based on the official IANA BOOTP/DHCP parameters registry, with base options from RFC 2132, relay agent info from RFC 3046, domain search from RFC 3397, and message types from RFC 2132 section 9.6. Offline and read-only.
dhcp-options.source: A built-in reference table drawn from the published DHCP options registry; nothing is measured or sent anywhere.

http-methods.name: HTTP Methods & Headers
http-methods.purpose: The HTTP request methods with their safe and idempotent properties, plus the common request and response headers you meet checking a captive portal, web service, proxy, or API.
http-methods.whyHere: Diagnosing a captive portal or an API call: this says whether a method is safe to retry and what a given header means.
howToUse: Read the methods table for each method and whether it is safe (read-only) and idempotent (repeating it has the same effect).
Read the request- and response-header tables to decode the headers you see in an HTTP Header inspector result.
fieldNotes: What it shows: HTTP request methods (GET, HEAD, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE, OPTIONS, and more) with safe and idempotent flags, plus common request and response headers.
Definitions: safe means read-only with no intended change; idempotent means repeating the request has the same effect as making it once.
Based on the official IANA HTTP method and field-name registries, with method meanings from RFC 9110 (HTTP Semantics) and PATCH from RFC 5789. Offline and read-only.
http-methods.source: A built-in reference table drawn from the published HTTP registries and RFCs; nothing is measured or sent anywhere.

dscp-qos.name: DSCP / QoS Markings
dscp-qos.purpose: The Wi-Fi-to-wired quality-of-service mapping: the four WMM Access Categories, the 802.11 User Priority values they carry, and the DSCP markings RFC 8325 recommends so your wired and wireless traffic agree on what is important.
dscp-qos.whyHere: Quality of service only works end to end when the wired DSCP markings and the Wi-Fi access categories line up. This is that mapping, with the most common mistake flagged.
howToUse: Read across the table for each WMM Access Category, the User Priority values it carries, and the recommended DSCP marking.
Read the warning beneath the table: the common default mapping accidentally demotes voice (EF, DSCP 46) into the video queue.
fieldNotes: What it shows: the four WMM Access Categories (Voice, Video, Best Effort, Background), their 802.11 User Priority values, and the DSCP code points (name, binary, decimal) RFC 8325 recommends.

Two different levels of authority sit on one screen, and the screen labels which is which. The User Priority to Access Category mapping is NORMATIVE - IEEE 802.11 Table 10-1 defines it. The DSCP to Access Category mapping is a CONVENTION: IEEE 802.11 defines no normative DSCP mapping, its own tables are examples in an informative annex, and RFC 8325 says so verbatim. Do not quote the DSCP mapping to a customer as "the standard".
The voice-into-video trap is called out prominently rather than buried, because it is the exact misconfiguration this page exists to catch.

Built from RFC 8325 (Mapping Diffserv to IEEE 802.11), IEEE 802.11 Table 10-1, IEEE 802.1Q, and Wi-Fi Alliance WMM, with DSCP code points from the IANA DSCP registry (RFC 2474 / 2597 / 3246 / 5865). Works fully offline.
dscp-qos.source: A fixed reference table drawn from the QoS standards, built in and available offline.

eap-types.name: 802.1X / EAP Types
eap-types.purpose: The EAP methods a client and authentication server negotiate inside 802.1X, compared across the credential they use, whether they need a server certificate, whether they need a client certificate, and whether both sides prove who they are, plus a note on where each is typically used.
eap-types.whyHere: Choosing or auditing an 802.1X method: this is the side-by-side of what each EAP type needs and what it protects.
howToUse: Read across the table for each EAP method (EAP-TLS, PEAP, EAP-TTLS, EAP-FAST, EAP-PWD, EAP-SIM/AKA, and more) on its four security points.
Read the warning: clients that skip checking the server certificate on a tunneled method (PEAP, TTLS, FAST) are open to evil-twin credential theft.
fieldNotes: What it shows: the EAP methods, the credential each uses, whether it needs a server certificate, whether it needs a client certificate, whether both sides authenticate, plus a typical-use note.
Note: EAP-FAST is RFC 4851; RFC 7170 defines TEAP, its standards-track successor (a different method, not the same one).
Built from each method's defining RFC (5216 EAP-TLS, 5281 EAP-TTLS, 4851 EAP-FAST, 5931 EAP-PWD, 4186 EAP-SIM, 4187 EAP-AKA, 5448 EAP-AKA'). Works fully offline.
eap-types.source: A fixed comparison table drawn from the EAP method standards, built in and available offline.

wifi-feature-matrix.name: 802.11 Feature Matrix
wifi-feature-matrix.purpose: A Wi-Fi 5 to Wi-Fi 7 capability comparison across the features that matter in design: bands, channel width, modulation, OFDMA, MU-MIMO, BSS Coloring, TWT, MLO, preamble puncturing, spatial streams, and the theoretical top PHY rate.
wifi-feature-matrix.whyHere: See what a Wi-Fi generation can and cannot do, side by side, without hunting through four standards.
howToUse: Read each feature down the rows and across the four generation columns (Wi-Fi 5 / 802.11ac, Wi-Fi 6 / 802.11ax, Wi-Fi 6E, Wi-Fi 7 / 802.11be).
fieldNotes: What it shows: which features are present or absent, the stream, width, and QAM ceilings for each generation, and a top theoretical PHY-rate row.

The max PHY rate is a theoretical ceiling, not a real-world client speed. It is marked as a ceiling with a note showing the math (Wi-Fi 7 at about 23.1 Gbps = 8 streams x 320 MHz x 4096-QAM, while no shipping client goes above 2-4 streams). Never treat it as an achievable speed.

The widely-quoted "Wi-Fi 7 = 46 Gbps" figure assumes 16 spatial streams. IEEE 802.11be-2024 (Table 9-417t) caps EHT at 8 streams - max-NSS values 9 to 15 are Reserved - so that figure is not reachable under the ratified amendment. MLO aggregates traffic across links; it does not raise a single link's PHY ceiling.
Built from IEEE 802.11ac/ax and the ratified IEEE 802.11be-2024.
wifi-feature-matrix.source: A fixed comparison table built from IEEE 802.11ac, 802.11ax, and the ratified 802.11be-2024. Works offline.

regulatory-domains.name: Regulatory Domains
regulatory-domains.purpose: The radio regulator that governs Wi-Fi in each market: its name, official website, governing documents, and 2.4, 5, and 6 GHz bands (a dated snapshot to verify against the regulator) for 43 jurisdictions.
regulatory-domains.whyHere: Working a market you do not know? Find its regulator, the governing rule, and a starting band and power note, then confirm against the official site.
howToUse: Search by jurisdiction or regulator abbreviation. Shared abbreviations (NCC, TRA, CRA) include the jurisdiction so they stay clear.
The page leads with a snapshot date: the band and power notes are a dated snapshot, not settled constants.
Tap a regulator's website to confirm any value against the current rule text before you rely on it.
fieldNotes: What it shows: a per-jurisdiction directory of 43 jurisdictions, each with its regulator (name, abbreviation, logo), the governing regulation, official website, and a 2.4/5/6 GHz bands note. The structural rules (band edges, DFS/TPC, which power classes exist) are high confidence; the exact dBm figures are a snapshot to verify against the regulator.
Regulations change: the 6 GHz dBm values are being amended (VLP extended band-wide, new geofenced classes). Never treat these as settled constants. Verify before deploying or certifying.
Cross-checked across FCC and ETSI documents and vendor regulatory white papers, current as of 2026. Works fully offline.
regulatory-domains.source: A fixed reference table cross-checked from official regulator documents, built in and available offline.

datetime-standards.name: Date / Time Standards
datetime-standards.purpose: A reference for ISO 8601 / RFC 3339 date-time formats, UTC-offset notation, the Unix epoch and the 2038 problem, leap seconds (UTC vs TAI), and NTP stratum levels.
datetime-standards.whyHere: Reading a log timestamp, an API field, or an NTP status: this decodes the format and the time scale behind it.
howToUse: Read the format cards for ISO 8601, the stricter RFC 3339 Internet profile, and the common format tokens.
Read the epoch, leap-second, and NTP-stratum cards for the time-scale context behind a raw timestamp.
fieldNotes: What it shows: ISO 8601 and RFC 3339 formats and how they differ, UTC-offset notation, the Unix epoch and the 2038 wraparound, the UTC-vs-TAI leap-second relationship, and NTP stratum levels (1-15 are valid; stratum 0 is the kiss-o'-death / unspecified marker).

Honesty note: the UTC-to-TAI offset of -37 seconds is a static teaching value, current as of the 2017-01-01 leap second. It is marked as static, with a pointer to the IERS Bulletin C, and is never presented as a live figure.
Built from ISO 8601-1:2019, RFC 3339, RFC 5905 (NTP), POSIX.1-2017, and BIPM/IERS. Works fully offline.
datetime-standards.source: A fixed reference drawn from the date-time and NTP standards, built in and available offline.

data-units.name: Data Units
data-units.purpose: Bits vs bytes, the SI (decimal) vs IEC (binary) prefix ladders (kB vs KiB through EB vs EiB), and how link rates measured in bits relate to storage measured in bytes.
data-units.whyHere: Why a '1 TB' drive shows as about 931 GiB, and why a 100 Mbps link is not 100 MB/s, kept in one place.
howToUse: Read the SI vs IEC ladder for each rank (kB/KiB through EB/EiB), its powers, byte counts, and how far the two diverge as a percentage.
Use the bit-vs-byte card and the divide-by-8 rule to turn a bit-rate into byte throughput.
fieldNotes: What it shows: the two parallel prefix ladders (SI base-1000 vs IEC base-1024), how far apart they drift at each step (about 2.4 points per rank, compounding), and the bit-vs-byte relationship.

Note: SI uses a lowercase k for kilo (kB) but uppercase for M and above; the IEC binary prefixes are Ki, Mi, Gi, Ti, Pi, Ei, where Ki uses a capital K, unlike SI's lowercase k. Network and link speeds are quoted in bits per second; storage in bytes.
Built from the BIPM SI prefixes and IEC 80000-13 binary prefixes. Works fully offline.
data-units.source: A fixed reference table drawn from the SI and IEC prefix standards, built in and available offline.

hash-lengths.name: Hash Lengths
hash-lengths.purpose: Common hash algorithms by output size (bits, bytes, and hex characters), family, and security status, with MD5 and SHA-1 flagged as broken and deprecated.
hash-lengths.whyHere: Sizing a digest field or judging an algorithm choice: this is the length and the security verdict at a glance.
howToUse: Read each algorithm's output length in bits, bytes, and hex characters, plus its family and its security status.
fieldNotes: What it shows: MD5, the SHA-1, SHA-2, and SHA-3 families, and more, each with its output length and a security verdict.
MD5 and SHA-1 are marked broken and deprecated. The verdict is spelled out in words alongside the status color, so meaning never rests on color alone.
Built from NIST FIPS 180-4 (SHA-1, SHA-2), FIPS 202 (SHA-3 / Keccak), and RFC 1321 (MD5), with deprecation per NIST SP 800-131A and the 2017 SHAttered SHA-1 collision. Works fully offline.
hash-lengths.source: A fixed reference table drawn from the hash-algorithm standards, built in and available offline.

regex-cheatsheet.name: Regex Cheatsheet
regex-cheatsheet.purpose: Regular-expression syntax (anchors, character classes, quantifiers, groups and references, and alternation and escapes) scoped to the common PCRE2 (Perl-compatible) dialect.
regex-cheatsheet.whyHere: The token you half-remember, looked up fast, with a flag wherever a different dialect would behave differently.
howToUse: Browse the syntax tables by section: anchors, character classes, quantifiers, groups and references, and alternation and escapes.
Watch for the dialect flag: a token marked that way behaves differently outside PCRE2, and the note in that row tells you how.
fieldNotes: What it shows: the common PCRE2 regex tokens with their meaning and examples, grouped by function. A banner at the top names the dialect as PCRE2 (Perl-compatible).

Honesty note: there is no single official regex authority. POSIX (BRE/ERE), PCRE2, ECMAScript, Python, Java, Go (RE2), and .NET all differ. No token is presented as universal unless it truly is; the ones that vary carry a dialect flag.
Built from the common PCRE2 subset per the PCRE2 syntax reference. Works fully offline.
regex-cheatsheet.source: A fixed reference table drawn from the PCRE2 regex syntax reference, built in and available offline.

cable-bend-radius.name: Bend Radius & Pull Tension
cable-bend-radius.purpose: Minimum bend radius and maximum pull tension for copper and fiber, with the TIA-568 numbers kept separate from the rules of thumb that a cable datasheet can override.
cable-bend-radius.whyHere: Before pulling or dressing a run, confirm how tight you can bend it and how hard you can pull it without degrading the cable.
howToUse: Installed UTP bends no tighter than 4 times its outer diameter and pulls at no more than 25 lbf (110 N), both per TIA-568.

Fiber runs about 10 times its outer diameter installed and 20 times while under tension as rules of thumb; bend-insensitive G.657 goes tighter, and the cable datasheet always wins over any rule of thumb.
The 8-times-outer-diameter figure for copper during a pull comes from ISO 11801 and field practice, not TIA.
fieldNotes: Over-pulling stretches the conductors and raises return loss and NEXT.
Each row is labeled Standard or Practice so the distinction survives when you copy it.

rack-units.name: Rack Units
rack-units.purpose: Rack dimensions and mounting hardware: U-to-inches-to-mm conversions, the EIA-310 hole pattern, and 10-32 vs 12-24 vs M6 thread compatibility.
rack-units.whyHere: When sizing equipment to a rack or grabbing the right screw, confirm the U height, the real mounting dimensions, and the thread that matches the rack.
howToUse: 1U is exactly 1.75 in (44.45 mm). Read the conversion table for any U height.
'19-inch' describes only the front panel: the mounting holes sit on 18.312 in centers and the opening is about 17.72 in.
The vertical holes repeat at 0.5 / 0.625 / 0.625 in within each U, not evenly spaced, which is why an off-by-one mount binds.
Match the screw to the rack: 10-32, 12-24, and M6 look alike but will cross-thread and strip if forced.
fieldNotes: Many racks ship as bare square holes and need cage nuts; few come tapped.
Vendor thread guides such as Dell's or HP's are a convention, not a rule.

screw-drives.name: Screw Drives
screw-drives.purpose: Recognize the screw drives you meet on access points, enclosures, brackets, racks, and outdoor gear, and carry the matching bit, including the security and tamper drives.
screw-drives.whyHere: Before a job, know which driver bits you need and whether a fastener is tamper-resistant, so a screw you cannot turn does not stop you.
howToUse: Match the common drives (slotted, Phillips PH1/PH2, Pozidriv PZ1/PZ2, hex, Torx T10 to T25, Robertson) to the right bit.
Pozidriv is easy to mistake for Phillips: the four 45-degree tick marks tell them apart, and the wrong bit strips the head.
Security and tamper drives (pin-Torx, pin-hex, one-way, tri-wing, spanner) each need their own bit, so pack the tamper set for outdoor enclosures.
fieldNotes: Phillips was not designed to cam out; the 1933 patent sought no cam-out.
Torx is sized by T-number, not star. Robertson color coding is a trade convention, not an ISO standard.
screw-drives.source: Based on the ISO 1173, 8764, and 10664 drive standards.

markdown-cheatsheet.name: Markdown Cheatsheet
markdown-cheatsheet.purpose: CommonMark and GitHub Flavored Markdown syntax at a glance: what you type and what it renders as.
markdown-cheatsheet.whyHere: When writing docs, READMEs, wikis, or notes, confirm the exact Markdown for headings, emphasis, links, lists, code, and tables.
howToUse: Each row shows the literal Markdown you type next to what it renders as: headings, emphasis, links, images, lists, task lists, blockquotes, code, and tables.
Rows marked GFM are GitHub Flavored Markdown extensions and may not work in a plain CommonMark renderer.
Gotchas: put a blank line between blocks, escape special characters with a backslash, and end a line with two spaces for a hard line break.
fieldNotes: The You type column is literal text, not rendered, so you can copy the exact syntax.
markdown-cheatsheet.source: Based on the official CommonMark and GitHub Flavored Markdown specifications.

wifi-standards-bodies.name: Wi-Fi Standards Bodies
wifi-standards-bodies.purpose: Who defines, certifies, and coordinates Wi-Fi and adjacent wireless: IEEE, the Wi-Fi Alliance, ITU-R, and more, grouped by the three layers a pro keeps straight.
wifi-standards-bodies.whyHere: People conflate the bodies. This separates who writes the standard from who certifies products from who sets the legal rules, so you cite the right one.
howToUse: Three layers: a standards body (IEEE 802.11) defines how the radio works; a certification body (Wi-Fi Alliance) verifies interoperability and owns the brand; a regulator sets the legal channel and power rules per country.
Tiles are grouped by layer, plus an IoT/adjacent group (Wi-SUN, Connectivity Standards Alliance for Zigbee and Matter, LoRa Alliance).
'Wi-Fi' is a Wi-Fi Alliance trademark, not an acronym and not 'Wireless Fidelity'. The per-country rules live on the Regulatory Domains reference, cross-linked here.
fieldNotes: IEEE writes 802.11; the Wi-Fi Alliance certifies and brands it. They are different organizations.
ETSI appears here as a standards body and also as the EU's referenced harmonizer on the Regulatory Domains page.
wifi-standards-bodies.source: Drawn from each body's official site (IEEE, Wi-Fi Alliance, WBA, ITU, IETF, 3GPP, Bluetooth SIG, CWNP, ETSI, Ecma, Wi-SUN, CSA, LoRa Alliance).

speedtest-services.name: Speed Test Services
speedtest-services.purpose: A curated, offline reference to the popular internet speed tests, framed for a Wi-Fi pro on the two things that actually change the number: single-stream vs multi-stream, and a nearby edge server vs a distant true server. Each service card states what the test measures, whose network it runs on, a rough data-per-test figure, and a link out to run it.
speedtest-services.whyHere: Two speed tests on the same connection can disagree by a wide margin, and the reason is almost never the connection. It is how many streams the test opens and how far away the server sits. This page lets you pick the right test for the question you are asking and read a surprising result correctly instead of blaming the Wi-Fi.
howToUse: Read each service against the two teaching axes at the top: single vs multi-stream, and nearby-edge vs distant-server. Those two choices explain most of the disagreement between tests.
Search by name (for example Ookla, Fast.com, Cloudflare) to jump to a service; a search that matches nothing shows an honest "No match" card, never a made-up entry.
Check the "Runs on" note before you trust a brand as an independent measurement: Waveform runs on Cloudflare, Fast.com runs on Netflix's network, and ISP-branded tests run on Ookla or M-Lab.
Tap a service's website chip to run that test in your browser. This page runs no test itself; it is a reference to what each test does.
speedtest-services.example: Fast.com reports lower than Ookla on the same link because Fast.com is a single-stream test against Netflix's network, while Ookla opens multiple streams to a nearby server. Neither is wrong; they answer different questions.
fieldNotes: Data-per-test figures are the weak column. Each one carries a confidence marker ("est.", "rough est.", or "measured"), and a persistent band states up front that these are community-measured estimates, not vendor-published numbers.
Not all of these are independent measurement backends. Where a brand rides on another service's network, the card shows a "Runs on" note and a top-level caveat repeats it.

Orb is a continuous monitor, not a one-shot test, and carries its own badge and framing note. The Toolbox's own Network Quality tool is the analog, and it deliberately reports no single composite score.

The Toolbox's own internet number sits on these same two axes on purpose: it opens several streams at once across a diverse set of independent public servers, throws away the TCP slow-start ramp, and drops throttled outliers, so it reports an honest, sustained download rather than a peak off the single nearest edge cache.
Fully offline: the service list is bundled in the app, so the page works with no network connection. Brand names appear on a neutral chip so they read in both light and dark.
The list is a curated set of 12 popular speed test services.
speedtest-services.source: A curated list bundled in the app, so it works entirely offline with no network call.

nearby-ap-scan.name: Nearby AP Scan
nearby-ap-scan.purpose: Lists the Wi-Fi access points a scan can see around you (SSID, BSSID, channel, band, and signal) and draws a simple channel-occupancy bar per band so you can read how crowded each channel is at a glance. Runs on Android today.
nearby-ap-scan.whyHere: A fast read of who else is on the air. When you are picking a channel or chasing co-channel interference, seeing the nearby BSSIDs and where they sit across 2.4, 5, and 6 GHz tells you which channels are busy and which are clear, without carrying a separate analyzer.
howToUse: Tap Scan to run a Wi-Fi scan. Results list each visible access point with its SSID, BSSID, channel, band, and signal (RSSI).
Use the sort control to order the list by signal or by channel, and read the per-band occupancy bars to see which channels are crowded.
Re-run the scan to refresh. Android rate-limits background scans, so a rapid re-scan may return the previous results until the throttle window clears.
nearby-ap-scan.example: On a busy 2.4 GHz band the occupancy bars cluster on channels 1, 6, and 11; an access point sitting on channel 3 overlaps both 1 and 6, which the bars make visible at a glance.
fieldNotes: Runs on Android today. iOS and macOS block nearby-AP scanning at the operating-system level, so the tool is left out of the catalog there. Windows is capable of it and can list every visible network, but that path is not wired into this tool yet; the copy says so honestly rather than implying only Apple restricts it.

Clean fields only. The Android scan exposes SSID, BSSID, channel, band, and RSSI for a scanned (non-connected) network. It does not expose a per-network noise floor, SNR, or data rate, so those columns do not exist here and are never shown.

Android throttles Wi-Fi scans. Foreground apps get a limited number of scans per window; when throttled, a re-scan returns the last cached results and the screen notes it rather than faking a fresh scan.
Location permission and Wi-Fi must both be on for the scan to return results; the screen shows an honest Wi-Fi-off or Location-needed card when either is missing.
nearby-ap-scan.source: Reads the nearby-network list the Android system returns from a Wi-Fi scan.

emergency-phrases.name: Emergency Phrases
emergency-phrases.purpose: A searchable, offline phrase translator for a Wi-Fi pro working on-site internationally: about 124 travel and emergency phrases in English with Spanish, French, Italian, and German, grouped into seven situations (basics and courtesy, medical and help, directions, food and lodging, problems and requests, on-site and technical, numbers and time).
emergency-phrases.whyHere: When you land on a job in a country whose language you do not speak, a few of the right phrases get you to the server room, to a doctor, or out of trouble. This keeps them one tap away with no signal needed.
howToUse: Pick how much to show: "One language" (English plus one target you choose) or "All languages" (English plus all four). One language is the default.
In one-language mode, choose the target from the language menu (Spanish, French, Italian, or German).
Search across all five languages at once. Type a phrase in English or in any target and the list filters live.
Tap the copy icon on any phrase to copy just that phrase in the languages currently shown, or use Copy all phrases in the top bar to copy the whole visible list grouped by situation.
emergency-phrases.example: Pick German, search "toilet", and the row shows English "Where is the toilet?" with German "Wo ist die Toilette?". Tap copy to put both on the clipboard.
fieldNotes: TRANSLATIONS ARE DRAFT. The Spanish, French, Italian, and German wording is draft machine translation generated for the beta and has NOT yet been reviewed by a native or professional translator. A banner at the top of the screen says so, and the caveat is included when you copy the list. Verify any critical phrase locally before relying on it.
Standard, simple wording was used throughout; no idioms were invented. Phrases are intentionally plain so they travel across dialects.
Fully offline. The phrase set is bundled in the app, so it works with no signal and no data plan.
Phrases are grouped by situation in a deliberate, curated order, not alphabetized.
emergency-phrases.source: A bundled phrase set that works entirely offline; the non-English wording is draft translation still awaiting review.

blue-box.name: Blue Box (MF)
blue-box.purpose: Plays the R1 Multi-Frequency (MF) trunk-routing tones a 20th-century long-distance switch once used to route a call, plus the 2600 Hz supervisory tone that signaled an idle trunk. A mode of the DTMF Generator.
blue-box.whyHere: Telephone signaling history and nostalgia. A blue box reproduced the in-band MF signaling AT&T's switches used internally. This mode lets you hear those tones and see the frequency pairs behind them. It is an educational exhibit that shows off the same tone synthesizer the DTMF tool uses.
howToUse: Switch to the Blue Box mode with the selector at the top of the DTMF Generator.
Tap a digit (1-0), KP, ST, or 2600 to hear that signal.
The readout shows the signal's frequency pair and burst timing.

inputs.name: Signal
inputs.unit: MF digit 1-0, KP, ST, or 2600 Hz
inputs.range: tap to play; no input required

blue-box.algorithm: Each MF signal is the sum of two sine waves drawn from six frequencies: 700, 900, 1100, 1300, 1500, and 1700 Hz. The digit pairs follow the canonical assignment (for example digit 1 = 700 + 900 Hz, digit 2 = 700 + 1100 Hz). KP (Key Pulse, start of digits) = 1100 + 1700 Hz; ST (Start, end of digits) = 1500 + 1700 Hz; the 2600 Hz supervisory tone is a single frequency. The tones are generated on your device; nothing is dialed or transmitted.
blue-box.example: Digit 5 is 900 + 1300 Hz. KP is 1100 + 1700 Hz. The seize tone is a single 2600 Hz tone.
fieldNotes: These tones do nothing on any modern phone network. The MF and 2600 Hz signaling a blue box exploited lived in the voice band, where the switch listened for it. Carriers moved call control out of the voice band onto a separate data channel (CCIS, then SS7, still in use) in the 1980s-1990s, so there is nothing in the audio path to seize or route. This is reproduced for history and education only.
The digit-to-frequency-pair mapping here follows the canonical assignment by frequency membership. Some hobbyist references order the pairs differently; both describe the same six-frequency set.
Tones come out of the device speaker. The app generates audio; it does not place a call or send anything down a line.
blue-box.source: Generates the tones on your device from the documented Multi-Frequency signaling frequencies.

red-box.name: Red Box (US coin tones)
red-box.purpose: Plays the US ACTS payphone coin-deposit acknowledgement bursts for a nickel, dime, and quarter. All three are a 1700 + 2200 Hz dual tone; only the burst count and timing differ. A mode of the DTMF Generator.
red-box.whyHere: Telephone signaling history and nostalgia. A red box reproduced the tones an ACTS payphone sent up the line to report that coins had been deposited. This mode lets you hear those coin patterns and see the timing behind them, using the same tone synthesizer the DTMF tool uses.
howToUse: Switch to the Red Box mode with the selector at the top of the DTMF Generator.
Tap Nickel, Dime, or Quarter to hear that coin's burst pattern.
The readout shows the 1700 + 2200 Hz pair and the burst timing for that coin.

inputs.name: Coin
inputs.range: tap to play; no input required

red-box.algorithm: Every US red box tone is the 1700 + 2200 Hz dual tone, in coin-specific burst patterns: a nickel is one 66 ms burst; a dime is two 66 ms bursts, 66 ms apart; a quarter is five 33 ms bursts, 33 ms apart. The tones are generated on your device.
red-box.example: A quarter is five 33 ms bursts of 1700 + 2200 Hz, each 33 ms apart.
fieldNotes: These tones do nothing on any modern phone network. Red boxing worked only on Automated Coin Toll System (ACTS) payphones that listened for these tones in the voice path. ACTS was phased out across the US, many payphones added acoustic filters, and payphones themselves are nearly extinct. This is reproduced for history and education only.
This mode covers the well-documented US nickel, dime, and quarter set only. The frequencies and burst timings are the corroborated standard 1700 + 2200 Hz values.
Tones come out of the device speaker. The app generates audio; it does not place a call or send anything down a line.
red-box.source: Generates the tones on your device from the documented ACTS coin-tone frequencies and timings.

keyboard-shortcuts.name: Keyboard Shortcuts
keyboard-shortcuts.purpose: The standard keyboard shortcuts you reach for every day: macOS and Windows system shortcuts, the line-editing keys for the macOS Terminal and Windows PowerShell, how to type special symbols on a Mac with the Option key, and the Greek letters that show up in RF and antenna math.
keyboard-shortcuts.whyHere: A fast lookup when you are driving someone else's machine, switching between macOS and Windows, or need a symbol or a Greek letter (lambda for wavelength, mu for micro, ohms) without hunting through a character viewer.
howToUse: Pick the platform tab (macOS, Windows, Symbols, or Greek). Each tab is a stack of copyable cards.
Find the action you want; the key combo is shown as the keys you press together.
On the Symbols and Greek tabs, the highlighted glyph is selectable, so you can grab a single symbol directly; the top-bar copy action copies the whole reference.
fieldNotes: The true degree sign on a Mac is Option Shift 8; Option 0 gives the similar-looking ordinal, which is flagged on the Symbols tab.
PowerShell reverse history search (Ctrl R) depends on PSReadLine, which ships with Windows PowerShell 5.1+ and PowerShell 7; a bare console host falls back to F7 / F8.
keyboard-shortcuts.source: Compiled from the published Apple and Microsoft keyboard-shortcut lists, standard terminal line-editing bindings, and common RF math notation for the Greek card.

time-zone-maps.name: Time Zones
time-zone-maps.purpose: A UTC offset reference for coordinating work across sites, surveys, and conference calls: a world map that shows where each one-hour offset band sits, an offset rail with anchor cities for every zone, and a United States table with the standard and daylight abbreviations.
time-zone-maps.whyHere: Scheduling a remote survey, a cutover window, or a call across sites. Find each site's offset and the difference in hours is the time difference between you.
howToUse: Find your site's offset on the rail, then the other site's offset; the difference in hours is the time difference.
The world map orients you: vertical bands are one-hour offsets and the prime meridian (UTC 0) runs through Greenwich. Tap it to zoom.
The United States table gives the standard and daylight abbreviations (for example EST in winter, EDT in summer) and notes who skips daylight saving.
fieldNotes: Offsets shown are standard time. When a region is on daylight saving, its local clock runs one hour ahead of the listed offset; in the US that window runs from the second Sunday in March to the first Sunday in November.
Most of Arizona stays on Mountain Standard Time all year, and most of Hawaii does not observe daylight saving. A few zones use half-hour offsets (India UTC +5:30, central Australia UTC +9:30).
time-zone-maps.source: Modernized from Keith's International Time Zones laminated reference, with offsets reconciled against the current IANA time-zone database.

phonetic-alphabet.name: Phonetic Alphabet
phonetic-alphabet.purpose: A scannable A to Z reference. Each letter shows its NATO/ICAO spelling word (Alfa, Bravo, Charlie), its international Morse code pattern, its semaphore arm positions, and its international maritime signal flag.
phonetic-alphabet.whyHere: Spell things out clearly over a noisy radio or phone link, read flag and light signals, or settle whether that was M as in Mike or N as in November.
howToUse: Scan the table for the letter, its NATO spelling word, and its Morse pattern (a dot is a short signal, a dash is a long one).
The plate below carries the semaphore arm positions and the colored maritime signal flag for each letter; tap it to zoom.
Tap Copy to grab the whole A to Z table as text.
fieldNotes: The official NATO/ICAO spelling uses Alfa (not Alpha) and Juliett (double-t) so the words are pronounced correctly across languages.
This covers A to Z only; there are no digit rows. The maritime flag colors are the real international code of signals colors, not brand colors.
phonetic-alphabet.source: Recreated from the standard ICAO/NATO spelling alphabet, international Morse code, semaphore positions, and the international code of signals flags.

diffie-hellman.name: Diffie-Hellman
diffie-hellman.purpose: How two parties agree on a shared secret over a public channel without ever sending the secret itself, taught through the classic paint-mixing analogy with the real math shown alongside.
diffie-hellman.whyHere: This is the key exchange behind WPA3. Understanding it explains why WPA3's SAE handshake resists offline password-guessing attacks and never exposes your password to someone quietly listening in.
howToUse: Start with the diagram (tap to zoom): a common public paint, plus each party's secret color, mixed and swapped, arriving at the same blended secret.
Read the step-by-step explainer to pair each stage of the analogy with its math (A equals g raised to the power a, taken mod p; both sides land on the same shared secret).
The eavesdropper callout and the Wi-Fi tie-in explain why someone listening in cannot recover either secret.
fieldNotes: Mixing paint is easy; un-mixing it is effectively impossible. The math works the same way: one direction is fast to compute, but reversing it (the discrete-logarithm problem) is hard.

Diffie-Hellman is the basis of SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals), the Dragonfly handshake in WPA3. It replaced WPA2's pre-shared-key 4-way handshake and resists offline password-guessing attacks.
diffie-hellman.source: The standard Diffie-Hellman key exchange and its use in WPA3's SAE (Dragonfly) handshake, with a paint-mixing analogy paired to the real math.

rf-bands.name: RF Bands
rf-bands.purpose: A frequency map of where the common wireless technologies live in the radio spectrum, from low to high frequency. It shows the neighbors of the bands you design in: sub-GHz IoT (LoRa, Zigbee, Z-Wave, Wi-Fi HaLow, RFID), GPS, cellular (LTE and 5G), the crowded 2.4 GHz ISM band, and Wi-Fi across 2.4, 5, 6, and 60 GHz.
rf-bands.whyHere: Understanding interference and coexistence starts with knowing who else is in the neighborhood. This is a frequency reference, not a channel plan: read it to see what sits next to your Wi-Fi bands and why a band is crowded.
howToUse: Scan the five grouped tables from low to high frequency. Each row gives the band, the technology, its typical use, and a note. Wi-Fi rows are highlighted so your home turf stands out.
The spectrum-bar plate at the top is a log-scale orientation of the whole map; tap it to zoom.
Check the region-variance list at the bottom for the bands where what operates where genuinely changes by regulator (6 GHz Wi-Fi, sub-GHz ISM, Z-Wave, HaLow, UHF RFID, channel counts).
fieldNotes: All band edges are nominal allocations, not guaranteed-clear channels. Local power limits, DFS, and licensing constrain real use further.

6 GHz shows the biggest regional variance: the US opens 5.925 to 7.125 GHz, the EU opened only 5.945 to 6.425 GHz, and some regions have not opened 6 GHz at all. Sub-GHz IoT lives at 902 to 928 MHz in the US and 863 to 868 MHz in the EU.
rf-bands.source: An RF-bands-at-a-glance reference, with every load-bearing figure cross-checked against two or more independent sources (ITU/ISM band edges, the Wi-Fi channels list, GPS signal frequencies, and 5G NR band tables).

wifi-halow.name: Wi-Fi HaLow
